options.C: Add `model: haiku` to the frontmatter so the analysis uses a faster, more efficient underlying model.
options.D: Add instructions to the skill to compress all outputs into a brief summary before displaying.

why: `context: fork` runs the skill in an isolated sub-agent context so its verbose output doesn't pollute the main conversation, preserving the user's original task focus while keeping full analysis capability.

explanations.A: `context: fork` runs the skill in an isolated sub-agent context so its verbose output doesn't pollute the main conversation, preserving the user's original task focus while keeping full analysis capability.
explanations.B: This fragments a coherent workflow and still pollutes the main context with whichever skill runs.
explanations.C: Model choice affects speed/cost, not context pollution — the verbose output still floods the main session.
explanations.D: Prompt-based compression is unreliable and still mixes analysis output into the main conversation context.

prompt: You paused an investigation session yesterday after Claude analyzed several modules. Overnight, a teammate refactored two of those files. You want to resume the named session today. What's the most reliable approach?

options.A: Resume with `--resume <session-name>` and tell the agent which files changed for re-analysis.
options.B: Resume the named session today and simply proceed exactly as if nothing at all had changed overnight by the teammate.
options.C: Start a completely fresh session today and re-explore every single one of the modules again from scratch.
options.D: Use `fork_session` to branch and hope that the branch automatically picks up the new file contents on its own.

why: Resuming and informing the agent about specific file changes enables targeted re-analysis rather than full re-exploration, while keeping the mostly-valid prior context.

explanations.A: Resuming and informing the agent about specific file changes enables targeted re-analysis rather than full re-exploration, while keeping the mostly-valid prior context.
explanations.B: Stale tool results for the refactored files would mislead the agent.
explanations.C: Wasteful when most prior context is still valid; only two files changed.
explanations.D: Forking branches from a shared baseline but does not automatically refresh stale tool results, and isn't the tool for this need.

prompt: A new team member reports that Claude isn't following the team's documented coding standards, even though you added them to your own setup months ago. You discover you put them in `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`. Why aren't they reaching the new hire?

options.A: User-level `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` is machine-local and not version-controlled; team standards belong in the repo `CLAUDE.md`.
options.B: The new hire simply needs to run the `/init` command once to regenerate the missing user-level configuration file.
options.C: User-level configuration files only ever stay active for the first 30 days after they are first created.
options.D: The standards must first be placed in the `.claude/commands/` directory before they can ever be shared with the whole team.

why: User-level configuration is personal and machine-local. Standards that should reach all developers must live in the project-level `CLAUDE.md` committed to the repo.

explanations.A: User-level configuration is personal and machine-local. Standards that should reach all developers must live in the project-level `CLAUDE.md` committed to the repo.
explanations.B: `/init` generates a project CLAUDE.md by analyzing the codebase; it doesn't copy your personal user-level file to them.
explanations.C: There is no such expiry; the issue is scope, not time.
explanations.D: `.claude/commands/` holds slash commands, not always-loaded project standards; those go in the project CLAUDE.md.

prompt: Your root `CLAUDE.md` has grown huge as you've pasted in coding standards for many different packages. It's now hard to maintain and bloats every request. What's the recommended way to keep it modular?

options.A: Use `@import` syntax to reference focused external standards files, keeping each CLAUDE.md lean.
options.B: Delete most of the accumulated standards and simply rely on Claude's built-in defaults instead.
options.C: Move all of the package standards into a single giant slash command instead of the file.
options.D: Duplicate the entire root CLAUDE.md into every package subdirectory of the repository.

why: The `@import` syntax lets a CLAUDE.md pull in specific external files, so you can break a monolith into focused, maintainable pieces referenced where relevant.

explanations.A: The `@import` syntax lets a CLAUDE.md pull in specific external files, so you can break a monolith into focused, maintainable pieces referenced where relevant.
explanations.B: Dropping standards loses needed guidance; modularization, not deletion, is the fix.
explanations.C: Slash commands are on-demand actions, not a replacement for always-loaded modular standards.
explanations.D: Duplication worsens maintainability and bloat rather than modularizing.

prompt: Your monolithic `CLAUDE.md` mixes testing rules, API conventions, and deployment notes. You want focused, topic-specific files instead of one large document. What's an appropriate organization?

options.A: Split the content into topic files under `.claude/rules/`, one per concern (testing, API, deployment).
options.B: Put each separate topic into its own slash command that users must remember to run before editing.
options.C: Keep one file but add more headers and hope Claude scrolls to the right section.
options.D: Move the rules to `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` so they're shorter per project.

why: Splitting a monolithic CLAUDE.md into focused topic files in `.claude/rules/` improves maintainability and lets conventions be organized (and, with frontmatter, conditionally loaded).

explanations.A: Splitting a monolithic CLAUDE.md into focused topic files in `.claude/rules/` improves maintainability and lets conventions be organized (and, with frontmatter, conditionally loaded).
explanations.B: Conventions should apply automatically, not depend on users invoking commands.
explanations.C: Header reliance keeps the bloat and depends on inference rather than structured organization.
explanations.D: User-level files aren't shared and don't solve organizing project conventions.

prompt: Developers report inconsistent behavior across sessions and suspect the wrong memory files are loading. Which command helps you verify which memory files are actually in effect?

options.A: `/memory` — to inspect and verify which memory files are loaded.
options.B: `/clear` — to wipe all memory and start fresh.
options.C: `/compact` — to summarize the loaded files.
options.D: `/init` — to regenerate all of the project's configuration files from scratch.

why: The `/memory` command shows and lets you edit the loaded memory files, helping diagnose configuration-hierarchy issues causing inconsistent behavior.

explanations.A: The `/memory` command shows and lets you edit the loaded memory files, helping diagnose configuration-hierarchy issues causing inconsistent behavior.
explanations.B: `/clear` resets the conversation; it doesn't reveal which memory files are loaded.
explanations.C: `/compact` summarizes conversation history, not memory-file loading.
explanations.D: `/init` creates a project CLAUDE.md; it doesn't diagnose which memory files are active.

prompt: You want a personal variant of a shared `/deploy-notes` command with extra steps you prefer, without affecting teammates who use the project command. How should you do this?

options.A: Create a differently-named personal command in `~/.claude/commands/`.
options.B: Edit the project's `.claude/commands/deploy-notes.md` directly with your personal steps.
options.C: Add your steps to the root `CLAUDE.md`.
options.D: Delete the project command and recreate it as a personal one.

why: User-scoped commands in `~/.claude/commands/` are personal; giving yours a different name avoids overriding or affecting the shared project command.

explanations.A: User-scoped commands in `~/.claude/commands/` are personal; giving yours a different name avoids overriding or affecting the shared project command.
explanations.B: Editing the shared command changes behavior for the whole team.
explanations.C: CLAUDE.md isn't where command definitions live, and changes there are shared.
explanations.D: Deleting the shared command removes it for everyone.

prompt: You're building a `/scaffold-component` skill that can write files. You want to ensure it can only perform file-write operations and cannot run arbitrary shell commands. What's the right mechanism?

options.A: Set `allowed-tools` in the skill's frontmatter to just the file ops.
options.B: Add a sentence to the skill body saying "do not run shell commands."
options.C: Set `context: fork` so it can't access tools.
options.D: Put the skill in `~/.claude/skills/` so it has fewer permissions.

why: The `allowed-tools` frontmatter restricts which tools a skill may use during execution, e.g., limiting it to file writes to prevent destructive shell actions.

explanations.A: The `allowed-tools` frontmatter restricts which tools a skill may use during execution, e.g., limiting it to file writes to prevent destructive shell actions.
explanations.B: Prose instructions are probabilistic; `allowed-tools` enforces the restriction deterministically.
explanations.C: `context: fork` isolates context, not tool access; it doesn't restrict which tools run.
explanations.D: Scope (user vs project) doesn't restrict tool access; `allowed-tools` does.

prompt: A skill is frequently invoked without the parameter it needs, and developers forget the expected argument. Which frontmatter option best addresses this?

options.A: `argument-hint` — to surface the required parameter when the skill is invoked without it.
options.B: `allowed-tools` — to make the missing argument a strictly required input for the skill.
options.C: `context: fork` — to ask the developer for the arguments inside an isolated subagent.
options.D: `model` — to pick a model capable of inferring the argument the developer forgot.

why: `argument-hint` frontmatter surfaces the expected argument to developers when they invoke the skill without it, reducing misuse.

explanations.A: `argument-hint` frontmatter surfaces the expected argument to developers when they invoke the skill without it, reducing misuse.
explanations.B: `allowed-tools` restricts tool access, not argument prompting.
explanations.C: `context: fork` isolates context; it doesn't prompt for arguments.
explanations.D: `model` selects the model; it doesn't hint required parameters to the developer.

prompt: Your team is deciding between encoding a workflow as a custom slash command/skill versus adding it to `CLAUDE.md`. Which guideline best informs the choice?

options.A: Skills/commands for on-demand workflows; CLAUDE.md for universal standards.
options.B: Always use CLAUDE.md because it's loaded every time, which is more reliable.
options.C: Always use skills because CLAUDE.md is deprecated.
options.D: It doesn't matter; the two are functionally identical.

why: Skills and commands are invoked on demand for specific tasks; CLAUDE.md holds universal context loaded into every request. Match the mechanism to whether the guidance is always-on or on-demand.

explanations.A: Skills and commands are invoked on demand for specific tasks; CLAUDE.md holds universal context loaded into every request. Match the mechanism to whether the guidance is always-on or on-demand.
explanations.B: Always-loading task-specific workflows bloats context; on-demand skills are the right tool for those.
explanations.C: CLAUDE.md is not deprecated; it's the right place for universal standards.
explanations.D: They differ: one is always-loaded context, the other is on-demand invocation.

prompt: You want Terraform-specific conventions to load only when Claude edits files under `terraform/`. How do you express that with a rule file?

options.A: Create a `.claude/rules/` file with frontmatter `paths: ["terraform/**/*"]` so it activates only there.
options.B: Put the Terraform conventions in the root CLAUDE.md file so that they always load on every request.
options.C: Name a slash command `/terraform` and require every user to run it first before editing those files.
options.D: Place a CLAUDE.md inside `terraform/` and hope that it also covers the deeply nested files.

why: Path-scoped rules use a `paths` frontmatter glob so the rule loads only when editing matching files, reducing irrelevant context and token usage.

explanations.A: Path-scoped rules use a `paths` frontmatter glob so the rule loads only when editing matching files, reducing irrelevant context and token usage.
explanations.B: Always-loading Terraform rules wastes context when editing unrelated files; path scoping is more efficient.
explanations.C: Conventions should apply automatically based on file path, not depend on users invoking a command.
explanations.D: A directory CLAUDE.md is acceptable for one directory, but path-glob rules are the precise mechanism for conditional, glob-based activation across a subtree.

prompt: Test files (`*.test.tsx`) live in many directories. You want test conventions applied to all of them regardless of folder. Why are path-scoped rules better than per-directory CLAUDE.md files here?

options.A: A single glob like `**/*.test.tsx` applies conventions by file type across the whole tree.
options.B: Directory-level CLAUDE.md files are known to load noticeably faster than path-scoped rule files.
options.C: Path-scoped rules automatically disable all of the other conventions whenever they are active.
options.D: A CLAUDE.md file is not permitted to contain any testing-related conventions at all.

why: Glob-based rules span directories by matching file patterns, ideal for file types scattered across the codebase; directory-bound CLAUDE.md files can't easily do this.

explanations.A: Glob-based rules span directories by matching file patterns, ideal for file types scattered across the codebase; directory-bound CLAUDE.md files can't easily do this.
explanations.B: Load speed isn't the issue; coverage across scattered files is.
explanations.C: Rules don't disable other conventions; they add conditional ones for matching paths.
explanations.D: CLAUDE.md can contain such conventions; the point is directory binding vs glob coverage.

prompt: You need to add a single null-check to one validation function with a clear stack trace pointing to the exact line. Which approach is appropriate?

options.A: Direct execution — the change is small, well-scoped, and well-understood.
options.B: Plan mode, because every change should be planned first.
options.C: Spawn multiple subagents to debate the fix among themselves before applying it.
options.D: Raise the effort level to max before editing one line.

why: Direct execution suits simple, well-scoped changes; plan mode would be unnecessary overhead for a one-line, clearly understood fix.

explanations.A: Direct execution suits simple, well-scoped changes; plan mode would be unnecessary overhead for a one-line, clearly understood fix.
explanations.B: Plan mode is for complex, multi-file, or architectural work; a single clear fix doesn't need it.
explanations.C: Multi-agent orchestration is wildly out of proportion to a one-line change.
explanations.D: Max effort is for hard reasoning problems, not a trivial, well-understood fix.

prompt: A library migration will touch 45+ files and you must choose between two integration approaches with different infrastructure implications. What's the best use of plan vs direct execution?

options.A: Use plan mode to investigate and design the migration, then direct execution for the approved plan.
options.B: Use direct execution only, switching over to plan mode later if and when something happens to break.
options.C: Use plan mode only and never actually execute anything, in order to completely avoid risk.
options.D: Neither mode; just raise the effort level to maximum and edit all the files directly.

why: Combine plan mode for investigation/architectural decisions with direct execution for implementation — exactly the pattern for a large, decision-heavy migration.

explanations.A: Combine plan mode for investigation/architectural decisions with direct execution for implementation — exactly the pattern for a large, decision-heavy migration.
explanations.B: The complexity and competing approaches are known upfront, warranting planning first.
explanations.C: Planning without execution doesn't deliver the migration; you plan, then execute.
explanations.D: Effort level tunes reasoning depth, not the breadth-of-codebase investigation that plan mode provides.

prompt: During a large refactor, the discovery phase generates a flood of file dumps that crowd the main conversation. How can you keep verbose exploration from exhausting your main context?

options.A: Use the Explore subagent to isolate verbose discovery and return concise summaries to the main chat.
options.B: Paste every single explored file into the main chat so that absolutely nothing is missed.
options.C: Disable plan mode entirely so that Claude is forced to read far fewer files overall.
options.D: Raise the `max_tokens` setting so that the conversation's context window can never fill up at all.

why: The Explore subagent isolates noisy discovery output and returns summaries, preserving the main conversation's context for the actual work.

explanations.A: The Explore subagent isolates noisy discovery output and returns summaries, preserving the main conversation's context for the actual work.
explanations.B: Dumping all files into the main context is exactly what exhausts it.
explanations.C: Reading fewer files weakens the investigation; isolating output via a subagent is the right fix.
explanations.D: max_tokens governs output length, not context window capacity for accumulated discovery.

prompt: When you describe a desired data transformation in prose, Claude implements it slightly differently each time. What's the most effective way to pin down the expected behavior?

options.A: Provide 2-3 concrete input/output examples illustrating the exact transformation.
options.B: Rewrite the prose description to be considerably longer and far more emphatic in tone.
options.C: Lower the temperature to 0 and keep the prose-only description.
options.D: Ask Claude to guess and then correct it repeatedly until it's right.

why: Concrete input/output examples communicate expected transformations far more reliably than prose, which can be interpreted inconsistently.

explanations.A: Concrete input/output examples communicate expected transformations far more reliably than prose, which can be interpreted inconsistently.
explanations.B: More prose still leaves room for inconsistent interpretation; examples are the reliable fix.
explanations.C: Temperature reduces variability but doesn't resolve ambiguity in what the transformation should be.
explanations.D: Trial-and-error is inefficient compared to providing examples that anchor the behavior upfront.

prompt: You're implementing a tricky date-handling utility and want to drive Claude toward a correct, robust implementation. Which iterative technique fits best?

options.A: Write tests covering expected behavior and edge cases first, then iterate by sharing failing tests.
options.B: Ask Claude to write the date-handling code and simply assume that it is correct without any tests.
options.C: Only add tests later, after the date utility feature has already shipped to production users.
options.D: Raise the effort level to maximum and simply hope it gets all the dates right in one shot.

why: Test-driven iteration — writing tests first and feeding failures back — progressively guides Claude toward a correct, edge-case-robust implementation.

explanations.A: Test-driven iteration — writing tests first and feeding failures back — progressively guides Claude toward a correct, edge-case-robust implementation.
explanations.B: Skipping tests forgoes the feedback signal that drives reliable iteration on tricky logic.
explanations.C: Post-hoc tests miss the chance to guide implementation and catch edge cases early.
explanations.D: Effort helps reasoning but doesn't replace the concrete feedback loop of failing tests.

prompt: You're about to implement caching in an unfamiliar subsystem and worry about invalidation pitfalls you haven't anticipated. Which technique surfaces hidden considerations before coding?

options.A: Use the interview pattern: have Claude ask you clarifying questions to surface design considerations first.
options.B: Tell Claude to just implement the caching now and that you will fix any problems that come up later.
options.C: Provide Claude with no context at all and simply grade the resulting implementation afterward.
options.D: Switch to a larger, more capable model and skip the design discussion entirely.

why: The interview pattern has Claude probe for considerations (e.g., invalidation strategy, failure modes) you may not have anticipated, improving the design before code is written.

explanations.A: The interview pattern has Claude probe for considerations (e.g., invalidation strategy, failure modes) you may not have anticipated, improving the design before code is written.
explanations.B: Diving in without surfacing considerations invites avoidable rework in an unfamiliar domain.
explanations.C: Withholding context makes a good first implementation unlikely; the interview pattern elicits the missing context.
explanations.D: Model size doesn't substitute for clarifying the design's hidden requirements.

prompt: You found several review issues in a generated module. Some interact (fixing one changes another); others are independent. How should you communicate them to Claude?

options.A: Send interacting issues together; fix independent ones in sequence.
options.B: Always send all issues one at a time regardless of interaction.
options.C: Always dump every issue at once regardless of independence.
options.D: Only report the first issue and ignore the rest.

why: Interacting problems should be addressed together so the fix accounts for their interplay; independent problems can be handled one at a time.

explanations.A: Interacting problems should be addressed together so the fix accounts for their interplay; independent problems can be handled one at a time.
explanations.B: Splitting interacting issues prevents Claude from reconciling their interplay in a single coherent fix.
explanations.C: Batching independent issues can muddy focus; the distinction is whether they interact.
explanations.D: Ignoring issues leaves defects unaddressed.

prompt: You want to compare two refactoring strategies starting from the same analyzed baseline of the codebase, exploring each independently. Which session mechanism fits?

options.A: Use `fork_session` to create independent branches from the shared analysis baseline.
options.B: Use `--resume` repeatedly on the same session for both strategies.
options.C: Run `/clear` between the two strategies and re-analyze the whole codebase each time.
options.D: Open two unrelated fresh sessions with no shared baseline.

why: `fork_session` branches from a shared baseline so you can explore divergent approaches independently without one polluting the other.

explanations.A: `fork_session` branches from a shared baseline so you can explore divergent approaches independently without one polluting the other.
explanations.B: Resuming the same session mixes the two explorations into one history rather than isolating them.
explanations.C: Clearing discards the shared baseline, forcing wasteful re-analysis for each branch.
explanations.D: Fresh sessions lose the common analyzed baseline that makes the comparison fair and efficient.

prompt: You're returning to a long investigation you named yesterday and most of the prior context is still valid. What's the best way to continue exactly where you left off?

options.A: Use `--resume <session-name>` to continue that specific named conversation.
options.B: Start fresh and paste yesterday's transcript manually.
options.C: Use `fork_session` to pull in the prior conversation's context and continue there.
options.D: Run `/compact` on an empty session to recover the context.

why: Named session resumption (`--resume <session-name>`) continues a specific prior conversation, ideal when prior context is mostly valid.

explanations.A: Named session resumption (`--resume <session-name>`) continues a specific prior conversation, ideal when prior context is mostly valid.
explanations.B: Manual transcript pasting is error-prone and unnecessary when you can resume the named session.
explanations.C: Forking creates a divergent branch; to simply continue the same investigation, resume it.
explanations.D: `/compact` summarizes the current conversation; it can't recover a prior session's context from nothing.

prompt: A session you'd like to resume contains many stale tool results because the codebase changed substantially after that session ran. Why might starting a new session with an injected summary be more reliable than resuming?

options.A: Resuming carries stale tool results that mislead; a fresh, summary-seeded session avoids that.
options.B: Resuming any prior session becomes completely impossible the moment one of its underlying files has changed.
options.C: Starting a completely fresh session is always strictly cheaper than resuming an existing one.
options.D: Injected summaries are a mandatory requirement imposed by the API for every session.

why: When prior tool results are stale, resuming risks reasoning over outdated data; starting fresh with a current structured summary is more reliable.

explanations.A: When prior tool results are stale, resuming risks reasoning over outdated data; starting fresh with a current structured summary is more reliable.
explanations.B: Resuming is possible; it's just less reliable when results are heavily stale.
explanations.C: Cost isn't the deciding factor; staleness of prior context is.
explanations.D: No such requirement exists; the summary approach is a reliability choice for stale-context cases.

prompt: In a long Claude Code session, you notice it starts giving inconsistent answers and references "typical patterns" instead of the specific classes it analyzed earlier. What is happening and what helps?

options.A: Context degradation in long sessions; have the agent keep a scratchpad of key findings to reference later.
options.B: The underlying model has broken; you should restart your computer to fix it.
options.C: Raise the temperature setting so that the answers become more specific again.
options.D: Simply ignore the behavior, because inconsistent generic answers are perfectly normal and harmless.

why: Extended sessions degrade, causing generic answers; persisting key findings to a scratchpad the agent re-reads counteracts the drift.

explanations.A: Extended sessions degrade, causing generic answers; persisting key findings to a scratchpad the agent re-reads counteracts the drift.
explanations.B: This is expected context degradation, not a system fault.
explanations.C: Temperature doesn't restore lost specifics from a degraded context.
explanations.D: Inconsistent, generic answers undermine the work and should be mitigated.

prompt: Your exploration session's context is filling with verbose discovery output, and you want to keep going on the same task while reducing context usage. Which command is designed for this?

options.A: `/compact` — to summarize the conversation while keeping key learned context.
options.B: `/clear` — to wipe out everything in the session and then continue working.
options.C: `/init` — to regenerate the project's CLAUDE.md documentation file.
options.D: `/memory` — to view which memory files are currently loaded.

why: `/compact` condenses the conversation, preserving important learnings, which reduces context usage during extended exploration on a continuing task.

explanations.A: `/compact` condenses the conversation, preserving important learnings, which reduces context usage during extended exploration on a continuing task.
explanations.B: `/clear` discards the valuable context you want to keep for the continuing task.
explanations.C: `/init` creates project docs; it doesn't manage conversation context.
explanations.D: `/memory` inspects memory files, not conversation context size.

prompt: Before moving from a discovery phase into spawning subagents for the next phase, what reduces the risk that context degradation corrupts the downstream work?

options.A: Summarize the discovery phase's key findings and inject that summary into the next phase's context.
options.B: Carry the entire raw discovery transcript forward into every single subagent you spawn next.
options.C: Skip the summarization step entirely in order to save yourself some time.
options.D: Lower the effort level for the next phase of the work.

why: Summarizing one phase's findings and seeding the next phase with that summary preserves accuracy and counters degradation across phase boundaries.

explanations.A: Summarizing one phase's findings and seeding the next phase with that summary preserves accuracy and counters degradation across phase boundaries.
explanations.B: Dumping raw transcripts bloats context and accelerates degradation.
explanations.C: Skipping summary risks the downstream phase working from degraded or missing context.
explanations.D: Effort level doesn't address context preservation across phases.

prompt: What does adding `context: fork` to a skill's frontmatter accomplish?

options.A: It runs the skill in an isolated sub-agent so its output doesn't pollute the main chat.
options.B: It grants the skill unrestricted access to every available tool in the environment.
options.C: It makes the skill run on a noticeably faster and more efficient model.
options.D: It automatically shares the skill with all of your teammates.

why: `context: fork` isolates a skill's execution in a subagent, keeping verbose or exploratory output out of the main session.

explanations.A: `context: fork` isolates a skill's execution in a subagent, keeping verbose or exploratory output out of the main session.
explanations.B: Tool access is governed by `allowed-tools`, not `context: fork`.
explanations.C: Model selection is the `model` option, not `context: fork`.
explanations.D: Sharing depends on project vs user scope, not this option.

prompt: Which list correctly orders the CLAUDE.md configuration hierarchy from broadest to most specific?

options.A: User-level (`~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`) → project-level (root) → directory-level (subdirectory).
options.B: Directory-level → project-level → user-level, applied in exactly that order of priority.
options.C: Only a single root CLAUDE.md file ever exists, so there is no hierarchy at all.
options.D: Slash commands → skills → rules, applied broadest to most specific.

why: The hierarchy runs from user-level (all your projects), to project-level (the repo), to directory-level (a specific subtree).

explanations.A: The hierarchy runs from user-level (all your projects), to project-level (the repo), to directory-level (a specific subtree).
explanations.B: This reverses the broad-to-specific ordering of the hierarchy.
explanations.C: There are user, project, and directory levels, not a single file.
explanations.D: Those are different mechanisms, not the CLAUDE.md hierarchy.

prompt: Your team wants a `/gen-migration` command shared via version control so it's available to everyone after a pull. Where does it belong, and why not the user scope?

options.A: In `.claude/commands/` in the repo; user-scoped `~/.claude/commands/` isn't shared via version control.
options.B: In `~/.claude/commands/`, because user-scoped commands are automatically synced to teammates.
options.C: In the root CLAUDE.md file, since that file is already shared across the whole team via the repo.
options.D: In the `.claude/rules/` directory, because rule files are also shared with the team.

why: Project-scoped commands are committed and shared automatically; user-scoped ones are personal and unshared.

explanations.A: Project-scoped commands are committed and shared automatically; user-scoped ones are personal and unshared.
explanations.B: User commands are not synced to teammates; they're local to your machine.
explanations.C: CLAUDE.md holds context/instructions, not command definitions.
explanations.D: `.claude/rules/` holds conventions, not slash command definitions.

prompt: Which best summarizes when plan mode is the right choice versus direct execution?

options.A: Plan mode for architecturally significant work; direct execution for simple, well-scoped changes.
options.B: Use plan mode for absolutely every task, since careful upfront planning never actually hurts.
options.C: Use direct execution for absolutely everything, since it is consistently faster.
options.D: The choice depends only on the chosen model's size, never on the task itself.

why: Plan mode shines on complexity and architectural decisions; direct execution fits clear, contained changes.

explanations.A: Plan mode shines on complexity and architectural decisions; direct execution fits clear, contained changes.
explanations.B: Planning adds overhead that's wasteful for trivial changes.
explanations.C: Direct execution on complex work risks costly rework when dependencies surface late.
explanations.D: Task complexity, not model size, determines the right mode.

prompt: A rule file applies React-hook conventions but you notice it's loading even when editing backend Python files, wasting context. What's the likely cause and fix?

options.A: Its `paths` glob is too broad; tighten it to match fewer files.
options.B: Rules always load for every file; nothing can be done.
options.C: Move the rule to CLAUDE.md so it loads less often.
options.D: Delete the rule file entirely so that it stops wasting any context at all.

why: Path-scoped rules load only for files matching their `paths` glob; an overly broad glob loads the rule for irrelevant files, so narrowing it fixes the waste.

explanations.A: Path-scoped rules load only for files matching their `paths` glob; an overly broad glob loads the rule for irrelevant files, so narrowing it fixes the waste.
explanations.B: Path-scoped rules are conditional by design; the glob controls activation.
explanations.C: CLAUDE.md always loads; that's broader, not narrower.
explanations.D: Deletion loses needed React conventions; scoping the glob is the targeted fix.

prompt: You want a custom command that takes a filename argument and runs your team's test-writing template against it. How are arguments passed into a custom command?

options.A: Reference the `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder in the command body, substituted with what the user passes.
options.B: Arguments are read from a hidden `args.json` file that sits next to the command file.
options.C: Custom slash commands are not able to accept any arguments from the user at all.
options.D: Arguments must be hard-coded directly into the slash command's own file name when you create it.

why: Custom commands use the `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder in their body, replaced by the text the user supplies when invoking the command.

explanations.A: Custom commands use the `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder in their body, replaced by the text the user supplies when invoking the command.
explanations.B: No such file mechanism exists; `$ARGUMENTS` is the placeholder.
explanations.C: Commands accept arguments via the `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder.
explanations.D: Arguments are passed at invocation and substituted via `$ARGUMENTS`, not encoded in the name.

prompt: Across a multi-hour exploration, you want crash recovery so you can resume if the session dies. Which approach aligns with sound context management?

options.A: Export key findings to a known manifest that can be reloaded on resume.
options.B: Rely on the model itself to simply remember everything if the session happens to restart.
options.C: Keep all of the state only in the live conversation, with no external record at all.
options.D: Increase the `max_tokens` setting so that the running session becomes unable to ever crash.

why: Persisting structured state to a known location and reloading it on resume enables crash recovery and counters context loss.

explanations.A: Persisting structured state to a known location and reloading it on resume enables crash recovery and counters context loss.
explanations.B: The model has no cross-session memory; state must be persisted externally.
explanations.C: Live-only state is lost on crash; a persisted manifest is needed for recovery.
explanations.D: max_tokens has nothing to do with crash recovery or persistence.

prompt: A skill that brainstorms multiple alternative designs tends to flood the main session with exploratory text. Which frontmatter setting best contains it while preserving the brainstorming capability?

options.A: `context: fork`, to run the brainstorming in an isolated subagent and return just the result.
options.B: `allowed-tools`, to carefully limit which tools the brainstorming skill is permitted to use.
options.C: `argument-hint`, to prompt the developer for the inputs the skill expects.
options.D: `model`, to choose a cheaper model for the brainstorming work.

why: `context: fork` isolates verbose/exploratory skills so their output doesn't pollute the main session, while still producing the brainstorming result.

explanations.A: `context: fork` isolates verbose/exploratory skills so their output doesn't pollute the main session, while still producing the brainstorming result.
explanations.B: Tool restriction doesn't address context pollution from exploratory text.
explanations.C: Argument hints help with invocation, not output isolation.
explanations.D: Model choice affects cost/speed, not whether output pollutes the main context.

prompt: You want a database schema file's contents available to Claude in every request without it having to search for the file each time. What's the recommended approach in CLAUDE.md?

options.A: Reference the schema file with `@` import syntax in CLAUDE.md so it loads into every request.
options.B: Paste the entire database schema into the body of a custom slash command instead.
options.C: Add a rule with a `paths` glob that points at the schema file's location.
options.D: Tell Claude in a prose instruction to memorize the entire schema file just one single time.

why: Mentioning a file via `@` in CLAUDE.md includes its contents automatically in each request, so Claude can answer schema questions without searching.

explanations.A: Mentioning a file via `@` in CLAUDE.md includes its contents automatically in each request, so Claude can answer schema questions without searching.
explanations.B: A slash command is on-demand; the goal is always-available context, which the `@` import provides.
explanations.C: Path-scoped rules load when editing matching files, not to always include a specific file's contents.
explanations.D: The model can't memorize across requests; importing the file keeps it in context.

prompt: A teammate argues plan mode is pointless because "you can always fix mistakes afterward." In the context of a 45-file architectural change, what's the strongest counterpoint?

options.A: Plan mode enables safe design first, preventing costly rework at this scale.
options.B: Plan mode is reliably faster than direct execution for absolutely every kind of task you might face.
options.C: Direct execution is simply incapable of modifying any more than one single file at a time.
options.D: Plan mode flatly guarantees that the resulting code will be completely bug-free.

why: For large architectural changes, planning first avoids expensive rework; fixing afterward is far more costly than designing the approach upfront.

explanations.A: For large architectural changes, planning first avoids expensive rework; fixing afterward is far more costly than designing the approach upfront.
explanations.B: Plan mode adds overhead and isn't faster for simple tasks; its value is for complex work.
explanations.C: Direct execution can modify many files; the point is planning reduces rework risk on complex changes.
explanations.D: No mode guarantees bug-free code; plan mode reduces rework risk, it doesn't eliminate all bugs.

prompt: You have a convention that must apply to all `*.proto` files spread across `services/` and `shared/`. Which option applies it precisely without loading for unrelated files?

options.A: A `.claude/rules/` file with `paths: ["**/*.proto"]` frontmatter.
options.B: A root CLAUDE.md section, loaded for every file.
options.C: Two directory CLAUDE.md files, one each in `services/` and `shared/`.
options.D: A slash command `/proto` run before editing.

why: A glob-scoped rule (`**/*.proto`) applies the convention to all proto files across directories while staying inactive for other file types.

explanations.A: A glob-scoped rule (`**/*.proto`) applies the convention to all proto files across directories while staying inactive for other file types.
explanations.B: Always-loading wastes context on unrelated files; a path glob is precise.
explanations.C: Directory files miss proto files elsewhere and duplicate the convention; a single glob covers them all.
explanations.D: Conventions should apply automatically by path, not depend on a manual command.

prompt: After resuming a session, you want Claude to re-analyze only the two files that changed rather than re-reading the whole codebase. What's the best practice?

options.A: Tell the resumed session exactly which files changed so it performs targeted re-analysis.
options.B: Force a complete full re-exploration of every single file in the project just to be safe.
options.C: Avoid mentioning the changes and let Claude detect them automatically.
options.D: Start a brand-new session from scratch to force Claude to re-read every file.

why: Informing a resumed session about specific file changes enables targeted re-analysis instead of costly full re-exploration.

explanations.A: Informing a resumed session about specific file changes enables targeted re-analysis instead of costly full re-exploration.
explanations.B: Full re-exploration is wasteful when only two files changed and prior context is mostly valid.
explanations.C: Claude won't reliably detect external file changes on its own; you should point them out.
explanations.D: A new session discards still-valid context unnecessarily; resume and target the changed files.

prompt: Claude keeps producing a CSV-to-JSON conversion that handles the common rows but mishandles quoted commas and empty fields. What's the most effective way to close these gaps?

options.A: Provide concrete input/output examples covering the quoted-comma and empty-field edge cases.
options.B: Tell Claude to simply 'handle all of the edge cases' without giving any examples.
options.C: Lower the temperature setting and then re-run the exact same prompt again.
options.D: Switch the output format over to XML entirely in order to sidestep the quoted-comma issues.

why: Examples that explicitly include the edge cases communicate the exact expected handling far better than prose, closing the gaps.

explanations.A: Examples that explicitly include the edge cases communicate the exact expected handling far better than prose, closing the gaps.
explanations.B: Vague instructions don't pin down specific edge-case behavior; concrete examples do.
explanations.C: Temperature doesn't teach the missing edge-case behavior.
explanations.D: Changing format dodges rather than fixes correct CSV parsing, and isn't what's asked.

prompt: Which statement about user-level (`~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`) configuration is correct?

options.A: It applies to all your projects on your machine but isn't shared with teammates via version control.
options.B: It is automatically committed to each repository and shared with the entire development team.
options.C: It overrides project-level files for all teammates.
options.D: It can only contain slash command definitions.

why: User-level CLAUDE.md is personal and machine-wide across your projects, and isn't shared through the repo.

explanations.A: User-level CLAUDE.md is personal and machine-wide across your projects, and isn't shared through the repo.
explanations.B: It's personal and machine-local; project-level CLAUDE.md is the shared, committed one.
explanations.C: It affects only your machine and doesn't reach teammates at all.
explanations.D: It contains instructions/standards; commands live in `commands/` directories.

prompt: When does it make sense to choose a skill over adding the same guidance to CLAUDE.md?

options.A: When it supports an on-demand workflow you invoke deliberately, not always-loaded context.
options.B: Whenever the guidance happens to be fairly short, since CLAUDE.md can only ever hold longer content.
options.C: Never, because CLAUDE.md can already do everything that skills are able to do.
options.D: Only in the specific case where teammates are not supposed to be able to see the guidance at all.

why: Skills suit on-demand, task-specific workflows; CLAUDE.md suits universal, always-loaded context. Choose by whether it's invoked deliberately or always needed.

explanations.A: Skills suit on-demand, task-specific workflows; CLAUDE.md suits universal, always-loaded context. Choose by whether it's invoked deliberately or always needed.
explanations.B: Length isn't the criterion; on-demand vs always-loaded is.
explanations.C: Skills offer on-demand invocation, isolation, and tool restriction that always-loaded CLAUDE.md doesn't.
explanations.D: Visibility is a scope concern (user vs project), not the skill-vs-CLAUDE.md decision.

prompt: While exploring a large codebase, you want the main agent to keep a high-level coordinating view while details of specific questions are investigated elsewhere. What approach helps?

options.A: Spawn subagents to investigate specific questions while the main agent keeps high-level coordination.
options.B: Have the main agent itself read every single file in order to stay fully and continuously informed.
options.C: Disable subagents entirely so that all of the reasoning stays in one single place.
options.D: Increase the temperature setting in order to improve the coordination.

why: Delegating focused investigations to subagents keeps verbose detail out of the main context, letting the main agent coordinate at a high level.

explanations.A: Delegating focused investigations to subagents keeps verbose detail out of the main context, letting the main agent coordinate at a high level.
explanations.B: Reading everything in the main agent floods its context and causes degradation.
explanations.C: Centralizing all detail defeats the context-preservation benefit of delegation.
explanations.D: Temperature doesn't affect context coordination strategy.

prompt: Why might glob-pattern rules be preferable to a subdirectory CLAUDE.md when a convention must apply to files of one type that appear throughout the codebase?

options.A: Glob rules match by pattern across all directories; a subdirectory CLAUDE.md governs its subtree.
options.B: Glob-pattern rules consistently load much faster than directory-level CLAUDE.md files do.
options.C: Subdirectory-level CLAUDE.md files are simply not a supported mechanism at all.
options.D: Glob-pattern rules are only ever capable of matching files within one single directory.

why: A glob rule spans the whole tree by file type; a directory CLAUDE.md is bound to its location, so scattered files of that type wouldn't all be covered.

explanations.A: A glob rule spans the whole tree by file type; a directory CLAUDE.md is bound to its location, so scattered files of that type wouldn't all be covered.
explanations.B: The advantage is cross-directory coverage by pattern, not load speed.
explanations.C: Directory-level CLAUDE.md files are supported; they're just directory-bound.
explanations.D: Globs like `**/*.ext` match across directories, which is precisely their advantage.

prompt: You're unsure whether a task is simple enough for direct execution. Which signal most strongly indicates you should switch to plan mode?

options.A: The task spans many files and requires choosing between architecturally different approaches.
options.B: The fix is a single line of code and you already have a clear, pointed stack trace.
options.C: You already know precisely which two characters in the file you are going to need to change.
options.D: You simply want the answer as fast as possible on an entirely trivial change.

why: Multi-file scope plus genuine architectural decisions are the hallmark signals that plan mode's exploration and design phase is warranted.

explanations.A: Multi-file scope plus genuine architectural decisions are the hallmark signals that plan mode's exploration and design phase is warranted.
explanations.B: A clear one-line fix is the canonical case for direct execution, not plan mode.
explanations.C: A precise, tiny, well-understood change fits direct execution.
explanations.D: For trivial changes, direct execution is the faster, appropriate path.

prompt: Your CI bot skill should never delete files. Which configuration enforces that, and why is a prose instruction insufficient?

options.A: Restrict the skill via `allowed-tools` to only what it needs; prose can be ignored.
options.B: Add the phrase 'NEVER delete files' in bold to the skill body; that on its own is sufficient.
options.C: Use the `context: fork` option to reliably prevent the skill from deleting files.
options.D: Lower the model's sampling temperature setting so that it gradually learns to avoid file deletes.

why: `allowed-tools` deterministically limits the skill's capabilities, whereas a prose 'don't delete' instruction relies on probabilistic compliance.

explanations.A: `allowed-tools` deterministically limits the skill's capabilities, whereas a prose 'don't delete' instruction relies on probabilistic compliance.
explanations.B: Emphatic prose still relies on probabilistic compliance and isn't a hard guarantee.
explanations.C: Forking isolates context, not tool capabilities; it doesn't block deletes.
explanations.D: Temperature can't enforce capability restrictions.

prompt: You want to compare two distinct testing strategies that both branch from the same baseline analysis of the test suite. After exploring both, you'll pick one. What's the cleanest workflow?

options.A: Fork the baseline with `fork_session`, explore each strategy, then choose.
options.B: Do both strategies within one single session sequentially, mixing their two contexts together.
options.C: Clear the context between strategies and re-derive the baseline analysis each time.
options.D: Use `--resume` on the same session name twice at the same time, simultaneously.

why: `fork_session` creates independent branches from a shared baseline, perfect for comparing divergent strategies without cross-contamination.

explanations.A: `fork_session` creates independent branches from a shared baseline, perfect for comparing divergent strategies without cross-contamination.
explanations.B: A single session intermixes the two explorations, muddying the comparison.
explanations.C: Clearing forces wasteful re-analysis and loses the shared baseline.
explanations.D: Resuming the same named session doesn't create independent branches the way forking does.

prompt: A directory-level `CLAUDE.md` exists in `packages/api/`. When does its guidance apply?

options.A: Primarily when working with files in that subtree, layering on top of project and user config.
options.B: Globally, for every single file that is located anywhere in the entire repository.
options.C: Only if you first explicitly run a dedicated slash command in order to load it into the session.
options.D: Never, because only the single root CLAUDE.md file is ever actually read by Claude.

why: Directory-level CLAUDE.md adds guidance scoped to its subtree, composing with the broader project- and user-level configuration.

explanations.A: Directory-level CLAUDE.md adds guidance scoped to its subtree, composing with the broader project- and user-level configuration.
explanations.B: Directory-level files are scoped to their subtree, not the whole repo.
explanations.C: Directory CLAUDE.md loads based on location, not manual invocation.
explanations.D: The hierarchy includes directory-level files, which do apply within their subtree.

prompt: You and Claude disagree on the intended output shape for a report generator, and prose keeps producing near-misses. Beyond examples, which complementary technique helps lock in the contract early?

options.A: Assert the exact output shape in tests, then iterate on failures.
options.B: Ask Claude to pick whatever shape it prefers.
options.C: Avoid examples and tests; just describe it again at length.
options.D: Raise temperature for more variety in shapes.

why: Test-driven iteration codifies the contract; sharing failing assertions drives Claude to the exact intended shape, complementing concrete examples.

explanations.A: Test-driven iteration codifies the contract; sharing failing assertions drives Claude to the exact intended shape, complementing concrete examples.
explanations.B: Delegating the contract to the model perpetuates the mismatch.
explanations.C: More prose is what's already failing; examples and tests are the reliable tools.
explanations.D: More variety worsens the mismatch rather than locking the contract.

prompt: Which directory holds personal (not team-shared) skills, and what's the implication for teammates?

options.A: `~/.claude/skills/`; personal skills there don't affect teammates and can use distinct names.
options.B: `.claude/skills/` in the repo; any personal skills you place there will still remain private to you.
options.C: Skills can never be personal at all; every single skill is automatically shared with the team.
options.D: Personal skills must instead be added directly into the project's shared CLAUDE.md file by hand.

why: User-scoped skills live in `~/.claude/skills/`, are personal, and using different names avoids affecting or colliding with team-shared skills.

explanations.A: User-scoped skills live in `~/.claude/skills/`, are personal, and using different names avoids affecting or colliding with team-shared skills.
explanations.B: Repo `.claude/skills/` is shared via version control, not private.
explanations.C: User-scoped skills in `~/.claude/skills/` are personal.
explanations.D: Skills live in skills directories, not CLAUDE.md.

prompt: In an extended session, Claude begins referencing 'typical' implementations rather than the actual classes it read earlier. Besides a scratchpad, what else helps maintain fidelity as context grows?

options.A: Summarize key findings before each new phase and re-inject them, using `/compact` to reduce noise.
options.B: Keep adding raw file dumps to the conversation so that nothing at all is ever lost.
options.C: Restart the underlying model's hardware in order to clear the degradation problem up completely.
options.D: Set the temperature all the way to 0 in order to restore the lost specifics.

why: Phase summaries plus `/compact` reduce accumulated noise while preserving the concrete findings, counteracting generic-answer drift.

explanations.A: Phase summaries plus `/compact` reduce accumulated noise while preserving the concrete findings, counteracting generic-answer drift.
explanations.B: Raw dumps accelerate context bloat and degradation.
explanations.C: This is context degradation, not a hardware fault.
explanations.D: Temperature can't recover specifics lost from a degraded context.

prompt: For an open-ended task like 'add comprehensive tests to this legacy module,' how should plan mode and execution combine?

options.A: Use plan mode to map structure and high-impact areas into a prioritized plan, then execute it.
options.B: Skip the planning entirely and just write the tests file-by-file in an arbitrary order.
options.C: Plan the work exhaustively but then never actually execute, in order to avoid mistakes.
options.D: Raise the effort to max and write absolutely everything in one single giant pass.

why: Open-ended legacy work benefits from planning to map the code and prioritize, then executing the plan with adaptation — the canonical plan-then-execute pattern.

explanations.A: Open-ended legacy work benefits from planning to map the code and prioritize, then executing the plan with adaptation — the canonical plan-then-execute pattern.
explanations.B: Without mapping structure and priorities, coverage tends to be uneven and low-impact.
explanations.C: Planning without execution doesn't deliver the tests.
explanations.D: A single giant pass on a legacy module dilutes attention; plan-then-execute is more reliable.

prompt: Your repo already has an `AGENTS.md` for another tool, and you don't want to duplicate its instructions in CLAUDE.md. What's a clean way to reuse it?

options.A: Add `@AGENTS.md` near the top of CLAUDE.md, then add Claude-specific notes below.
options.B: Copy the entire contents of AGENTS.md into CLAUDE.md by hand, keeping both in sync.
options.C: Rename AGENTS.md to CLAUDE.md and simply lose support for the other tool that needs it.
options.D: Put the entire AGENTS.md content inside a custom slash command that you run instead.

why: The `@` import lets CLAUDE.md pull in `AGENTS.md` so you avoid duplication, then layer Claude-specific instructions afterward.

explanations.A: The `@` import lets CLAUDE.md pull in `AGENTS.md` so you avoid duplication, then layer Claude-specific instructions afterward.
explanations.B: Manual duplication creates drift; the `@` import avoids it.
explanations.C: Renaming breaks the other tool; importing preserves both.
explanations.D: Always-loaded context should be imported into CLAUDE.md, not gated behind a command.

prompt: A developer keeps invoking `/seed-db` without the environment argument it requires, causing confusing failures. Which two adjustments most directly help?

options.A: Add an `argument-hint` so the missing parameter is prompted, and document the argument in the body.
options.B: Add both the `context: fork` option and the `model: haiku` option to the command's frontmatter.
options.C: Move the command from the project scope over into `~/.claude/commands/` instead.
options.D: Restrict the `allowed-tools` setting so that it blocks access to the database tool.

why: `argument-hint` surfaces the required parameter at invocation, and documenting it in the body clarifies usage — directly addressing the missing-argument confusion.

explanations.A: `argument-hint` surfaces the required parameter at invocation, and documenting it in the body clarifies usage — directly addressing the missing-argument confusion.
explanations.B: Neither isolation nor model choice addresses a missing required argument.
explanations.C: Changing scope doesn't prompt for or document the argument.
explanations.D: Blocking the tool prevents the command from working at all, rather than guiding correct argument use.

prompt: A `.claude/rules/` file has frontmatter `paths: ["**/*.test.ts", "**/*.test.tsx"]`. What behavior does this produce?

options.A: The conventions load only when editing files matching those globs.
options.B: The rule loads for every file in the repository at all times.
options.C: The rule disables linting for test files.
options.D: The rule only loads if invoked by a slash command.

why: Path-scoped rules activate conditionally based on the `paths` globs, so these conventions apply specifically to matching test files and stay out of context otherwise.

explanations.A: Path-scoped rules activate conditionally based on the `paths` globs, so these conventions apply specifically to matching test files and stay out of context otherwise.
explanations.B: The `paths` globs restrict activation to matching files, not all files.
explanations.C: Rules provide conventions/context; they don't toggle linting.
explanations.D: Path-scoped rules load automatically on matching edits, not via manual invocation.

prompt: Which pair correctly matches the session mechanism to its purpose?

options.A: `--resume <name>` continues a specific prior conversation; `fork_session` branches from a shared baseline.
options.B: `--resume` always starts completely fresh, while `fork_session` simply deletes the original session.
options.C: Both of these two mechanisms actually do exactly the same thing as each other.
options.D: `fork_session` continues the very same conversation, while `--resume` is what creates branches.

why: Resume continues a named session in place; fork creates a divergent branch from a shared baseline for parallel exploration.

explanations.A: Resume continues a named session in place; fork creates a divergent branch from a shared baseline for parallel exploration.
explanations.B: Resume continues (doesn't start fresh) and forking doesn't delete the original.
explanations.C: They serve distinct purposes: continuation vs. branching.
explanations.D: This reverses their roles.

prompt: Which is the best example of an appropriate direct-execution task (no plan mode needed)?

options.A: Adding a single date-validation conditional to one function with a clear failing test.
options.B: Splitting a large monolithic application into separate independent microservices.
options.C: Migrating a library across 45+ files with several competing approaches.
options.D: Redesigning the entire module dependency graph of the application.

why: A small, well-scoped, well-understood change with a clear test is the canonical direct-execution case.

explanations.A: A small, well-scoped, well-understood change with a clear test is the canonical direct-execution case.
explanations.B: That's a large architectural change calling for plan mode.
explanations.C: Large, decision-heavy migrations warrant plan mode first.
explanations.D: Architectural redesign is a plan-mode task, not direct execution.

prompt: You're fixing two defects in a parser: defect A changes how tokens are split, and defect B depends on how tokens are split. How should you present them to Claude?

options.A: Describe both in one message because they interact, so the fix accounts for their interplay.
options.B: Fix defect B first in complete isolation, and only then fix defect A separately afterward.
options.C: Only bother to fix defect A and just assume that defect B will resolve itself.
options.D: Send each individual character-level change to Claude as its own separate message.

why: Interacting defects should be addressed together; fixing them separately risks one fix invalidating the other.

explanations.A: Interacting defects should be addressed together; fixing them separately risks one fix invalidating the other.
explanations.B: Because B depends on A's behavior, isolating them risks inconsistent fixes.
explanations.C: Assuming B self-resolves leaves a real defect unhandled.
explanations.D: Over-fragmenting prevents the model from reasoning about the interaction.

prompt: What is the primary benefit of having an exploration subagent return a concise summary to the main agent rather than the raw file contents it read?

options.A: It preserves the main agent's context budget while still surfacing the key findings to proceed.
options.B: It causes the exploration subagent to run on a noticeably faster model than before.
options.C: It permanently deletes all of the explored files from the disk afterward.
options.D: It disables the main agent's own set of tools for the entire duration of the exploration.

why: Returning summaries instead of raw contents keeps the main conversation's context lean while conveying the essential findings.

explanations.A: Returning summaries instead of raw contents keeps the main conversation's context lean while conveying the essential findings.
explanations.B: Summarizing output is unrelated to which model the subagent uses.
explanations.C: Summarizing output doesn't touch the filesystem.
explanations.D: Delegation doesn't disable the main agent's tools; it just isolates verbose discovery.

prompt: A teammate's behavior differs from yours because instructions you rely on live only in your user-level config. What's the correct remedy to standardize the team?

options.A: Move the shared instructions into the project-level `CLAUDE.md` committed to the repo for everyone.
options.B: Ask every teammate on the project to manually copy your `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` file themselves.
options.C: Put the instructions in a slash command teammates must remember to run.
options.D: Leave them in user-level config; teammates will eventually sync.

why: Standardizing the team requires putting the instructions in the version-controlled project CLAUDE.md, which all developers load.

explanations.A: Standardizing the team requires putting the instructions in the version-controlled project CLAUDE.md, which all developers load.
explanations.B: Manual copying is brittle and drifts; project-level config is the maintainable, shared source of truth.
explanations.C: Universal standards should auto-load via CLAUDE.md, not depend on manual command invocation.
explanations.D: User-level config never syncs to teammates; it stays personal.

prompt: Which combination of frontmatter options would let a code-analysis skill run without flooding the main session and without the ability to modify files?

options.A: `context: fork` to isolate the output and `allowed-tools` limited to read-only operations.
options.B: The `argument-hint` option combined with setting `model: haiku` in the frontmatter.
options.C: Setting `model: opus` together with writing a much longer skill description.
options.D: Only a prose note in the body politely saying 'read-only, please.'

why: `context: fork` keeps verbose analysis out of the main session, while `allowed-tools` restricted to read-only prevents file modification.

explanations.A: `context: fork` keeps verbose analysis out of the main session, while `allowed-tools` restricted to read-only prevents file modification.
explanations.B: Neither isolates output nor restricts file modification.
explanations.C: Model and description don't control context isolation or tool permissions.
explanations.D: Prose is probabilistic; `allowed-tools` enforces read-only deterministically and `context: fork` handles isolation.

prompt: Plan mode's main value proposition for complex changes is best described as:

options.A: Safe exploration and design before committing changes, reducing the chance of rework.
options.B: Guaranteeing that the resulting code will always compile correctly on the very first try.
options.C: Completely eliminating any need to test the changes that get made.
options.D: Making small, trivial fixes noticeably faster than plain direct execution does.

why: Plan mode lets Claude investigate and design first, surfacing dependencies and approaches before any change is made, which prevents expensive rework.

explanations.A: Plan mode lets Claude investigate and design first, surfacing dependencies and approaches before any change is made, which prevents expensive rework.
explanations.B: Plan mode doesn't guarantee compilation; it reduces rework risk through upfront design.
explanations.C: Testing is still needed; planning addresses design and exploration, not verification.
explanations.D: For small fixes, direct execution is faster; plan mode's value is on complex work.

prompt: Where do path-scoped convention files live, and what frontmatter field controls when they activate?

options.A: In `.claude/rules/`, with a YAML `paths` field of globs controlling activation.
options.B: In the `.claude/commands/` directory, controlled by an `args` field in the frontmatter.
options.C: In the root CLAUDE.md file, controlled by a `globs` field in its frontmatter.
options.D: In `~/.claude/skills/`, controlled by an `allowed-tools` field there.

why: Path-scoped rules reside in `.claude/rules/` and use the `paths` frontmatter (globs) to decide which files trigger their loading.

explanations.A: Path-scoped rules reside in `.claude/rules/` and use the `paths` frontmatter (globs) to decide which files trigger their loading.
explanations.B: `.claude/commands/` holds slash commands, not path-scoped rules.
explanations.C: CLAUDE.md always loads and isn't the path-scoped rule mechanism.
explanations.D: That's the skills directory; `allowed-tools` restricts tools, not path activation.

prompt: When iterating on a complex utility, why is sharing a failing test with Claude more effective than re-describing the bug in prose?

options.A: A failing test is an executable specification of expected behavior, giving Claude a precise target.
options.B: Prose descriptions of a bug become strictly forbidden whenever any tests already exist for it.
options.C: Tests automatically make the underlying model run at a higher effort level.
options.D: Sharing a test disables the model's need to read the surrounding code at all.

why: Tests encode exact expected behavior; sharing failures gives Claude an unambiguous target, unlike prose that can be interpreted loosely.

explanations.A: Tests encode exact expected behavior; sharing failures gives Claude an unambiguous target, unlike prose that can be interpreted loosely.
explanations.B: Prose isn't forbidden; tests are simply a more precise signal.
explanations.C: Tests don't change effort level; they provide a concrete correctness signal.
explanations.D: Claude still reads the code; tests add a precise target, they don't replace understanding the code.

prompt: You're about to spawn several subagents for a multi-phase refactor. To keep them effective despite a long prior conversation, what should you inject into each subagent's initial context?

options.A: A concise, structured summary of the relevant findings from prior phases, not the raw transcript.
options.B: The complete, entirely unedited conversation history from all of the prior phases.
options.C: Nothing at all; each subagent should simply rediscover everything itself from scratch.
options.D: Only the bare list of file names, without including any of the actual investigation findings.

why: Seeding subagents with a focused summary of prior findings preserves needed context without bloating them with the full noisy transcript.

explanations.A: Seeding subagents with a focused summary of prior findings preserves needed context without bloating them with the full noisy transcript.
explanations.B: Dumping the whole transcript bloats each subagent's context and degrades performance.
explanations.C: Rediscovering everything wastes effort when a summary can carry forward key findings.
explanations.D: Bare file names omit the findings that make the summary useful.

prompt: You want a skill that summarizes a large dependency graph but must not pollute the main chat and should run a cheaper model for cost. Which frontmatter combination fits?

options.A: `context: fork` for isolation, plus the `model` option set to a cheaper model.
options.B: The `argument-hint` option combined together with the `allowed-tools` option.
options.C: Only the `allowed-tools` frontmatter option, on its own.
options.D: A simple prose note in the body asking for brevity.

why: `context: fork` isolates the verbose summary from the main session, and the `model` option selects a cheaper model for the cost goal.

explanations.A: `context: fork` isolates the verbose summary from the main session, and the `model` option selects a cheaper model for the cost goal.
explanations.B: These address argument prompting and tool restriction, not isolation or model choice.
explanations.C: Restricting tools neither isolates output nor chooses a cheaper model.
explanations.D: Prose can't guarantee isolation or set the model; frontmatter options do.

prompt: A change has clear scope and a single obvious approach, but it touches 6 files mechanically (a rename). Which mode is most appropriate and why?

options.A: Direct execution — the approach is unambiguous despite several files.
options.B: Plan mode — any change touching more than one file requires planning.
options.C: Neither; such changes can't be automated.
options.D: Plan mode, then discard the plan and do it manually.

why: Plan mode is for architectural ambiguity and competing approaches; a mechanical, unambiguous rename across a few files is well-suited to direct execution.

explanations.A: Plan mode is for architectural ambiguity and competing approaches; a mechanical, unambiguous rename across a few files is well-suited to direct execution.
explanations.B: File count alone doesn't mandate plan mode; architectural ambiguity does. A clear rename is direct-execution-friendly.
explanations.C: Mechanical renames are exactly the kind of change Claude handles directly.
explanations.D: That wastes the planning step on an unambiguous task.

prompt: After running the system on the topic "impact of AI on creative industries," each subagent completes successfully, but the final reports cover only visual arts, completely missing music, writing, and film. The coordinator's logs show it decomposed the topic into "AI in digital art creation," "AI in graphic design," and "AI in photography." What is the most likely root cause?

options.A: The synthesis agent lacks instructions for identifying coverage gaps in the findings it receives from other agents.
options.B: The coordinator agent's task decomposition is too narrow, resulting in subagent assignments that don't cover all relevant domains of the topic.
options.C: The web search agent's queries are not comprehensive enough and need to be expanded to cover more creative industry sectors.
options.D: The document analysis agent is filtering out sources related to non-visual creative industries due to overly restrictive relevance criteria.

why: The coordinator's logs reveal the root cause directly: it decomposed "creative industries" into only visual-arts subtasks, omitting music, writing, and film. The subagents executed their assigned tasks correctly.

explanations.A: The synthesis agent worked correctly on what it received; the gap was created upstream by the coordinator.
explanations.B: The coordinator's logs reveal the root cause directly: it decomposed "creative industries" into only visual-arts subtasks, omitting music, writing, and film. The subagents executed their assigned tasks correctly.
explanations.C: The web search agent executed its assigned (too-narrow) subtasks correctly.
explanations.D: No filtering occurred; those domains were never assigned in the first place.

prompt: The web search subagent times out while researching a complex topic. You need to design how this failure information flows back to the coordinator agent. Which error propagation approach best enables intelligent recovery?

options.A: Return structured error context to the coordinator including the failure type, the attempted query, any partial results, and potential alternative approaches.
options.B: Implement automatic retry logic with exponential backoff within the subagent, returning a generic "search unavailable" status only after all retries are exhausted.
options.C: Catch the timeout within the subagent and return an empty result set marked as successful.
options.D: Propagate the timeout exception directly to a top-level handler that terminates the entire research workflow.

why: Structured error context gives the coordinator the information it needs to make intelligent recovery decisions — retry with a modified query, try an alternative, or proceed with partial results.

explanations.A: Structured error context gives the coordinator the information it needs to make intelligent recovery decisions — retry with a modified query, try an alternative, or proceed with partial results.
explanations.B: The generic status hides valuable context from the coordinator, preventing informed decisions.
explanations.C: Marking failure as success prevents any recovery and risks incomplete research outputs.
explanations.D: Terminating the whole workflow is unnecessary when recovery strategies could succeed.

prompt: During testing, the synthesis agent frequently needs to verify specific claims while combining findings. Currently it returns control to the coordinator, which invokes the web search agent, then re-invokes synthesis — adding 2-3 round trips and 40% latency. 85% of verifications are simple fact-checks; 15% require deeper investigation. What's the most effective approach to reduce overhead while maintaining reliability?

options.A: Give the synthesis agent a scoped `verify_fact` tool for simple lookups, while complex verifications continue delegating to the web search agent through the coordinator.
options.B: Have the synthesis agent accumulate all verification needs and return them as a batch to the coordinator at the end of its pass, which sends them all to the web search agent at once.
options.C: Give the synthesis agent access to all web search tools so it can handle any verification need directly without round-trips.
options.D: Have the web search agent proactively cache extra context around each source during initial research, anticipating what the synthesis agent might need to verify.

why: This applies least privilege: the synthesis agent gets only what it needs for the 85% common case, while the existing coordination pattern handles complex cases.

explanations.A: This applies least privilege: the synthesis agent gets only what it needs for the 85% common case, while the existing coordination pattern handles complex cases.
explanations.B: Batching creates blocking dependencies since synthesis steps may depend on earlier verified facts.
explanations.C: This over-provisions the synthesis agent, violating separation of concerns and degrading tool selection.
explanations.D: Speculative caching cannot reliably predict what the synthesis agent will need to verify.

prompt: The synthesis subagent produces reports that omit source attribution, even though the web search and document agents collected URLs and page numbers. The coordinator passes only a prose summary of prior findings to synthesis. What change best preserves attribution?

options.A: Pass structured findings that separate each claim's content from its metadata (source URLs, document names, page numbers).
options.B: Instruct the synthesis subagent to invent plausible-looking citations that fit each claim it writes into the report.
options.C: Have the synthesis subagent re-run all the web and document searches itself to recover the original sources.
options.D: Lower the synthesis subagent's temperature so that it stops dropping the citations from its written output.

why: Subagents don't inherit parent context; using structured data to separate content from metadata when passing context preserves attribution through synthesis.

explanations.A: Subagents don't inherit parent context; using structured data to separate content from metadata when passing context preserves attribution through synthesis.
explanations.B: Fabricating citations is a serious correctness and trust violation.
explanations.C: Wasteful and unreliable; the sources were already collected and just need to be passed through.
explanations.D: Temperature doesn't control whether metadata that was never provided appears in the output.
