prompt: An engineer needs the agent to change a single uniquely-identifiable line in a 4,000-line file without rewriting the whole file. Which tool fits best?

options.A: Use Edit with a unique text match to make the targeted change in place.
options.B: Use Write to output the entire 4,000-line file with the one line changed.
options.C: Use Grep to replace the matched line directly in the file contents.
options.D: Use Glob to find the line by its pattern and apply the change there.

why: Edit makes targeted edits by matching unique text, which is ideal for changing one line without rewriting the file.

explanations.A: Edit makes targeted edits by matching unique text, which is ideal for changing one line without rewriting the file.
explanations.B: Write rewrites the whole file, which is wasteful when a targeted Edit will do.
explanations.C: Grep only searches; it does not modify files.
explanations.D: Glob matches file paths, not lines within a file, and cannot edit.

prompt: Production logs show an agent run timing out while it repeatedly reads large files trying to locate where an env var `STRIPE_KEY` is consumed. What is the most effective first step?

options.A: Read each service file fully in sequence until the usage is spotted.
options.B: Grep for `STRIPE_KEY` to pinpoint the files, then Read only those.
options.C: Glob for files containing `stripe` in the name and read all matches.
options.D: Write a temporary index file cataloguing every variable by hand first.

why: Grep locates the symbol quickly across the repo so only the relevant files get Read, avoiding wasteful full reads.

explanations.A: Sequential full reads are exactly the slow pattern causing the timeout.
explanations.B: Grep locates the symbol quickly across the repo so only the relevant files get Read, avoiding wasteful full reads.
explanations.C: Filename matching misses files where the var is used but the name differs.
explanations.D: Manually building an index is more work than a direct content search.

prompt: A developer wants to experiment with a personal MCP server on their own machine without affecting teammates or the repo. Where should it be configured?

options.A: In the project `.mcp.json` so it stays close to the related code.
options.B: In the user-level config (`~/.claude.json`) so it stays personal to them.
options.C: Inside the committed `CLAUDE.md` so Claude reads it each session.
options.D: In a new `.github/workflows` file so CI picks the server up too.

why: User-level config is for personal or experimental servers that apply to your machine without being shared via version control.

explanations.A: Project `.mcp.json` is committed and shared with the whole team, not personal.
explanations.B: User-level config is for personal or experimental servers that apply to your machine without being shared via version control.
explanations.C: CLAUDE.md provides instructions, not MCP server configuration, and would be shared.
explanations.D: Workflow files configure CI, not a local personal MCP server.

prompt: Your team needs a standard integration with GitHub for the agent. What is the recommended approach before writing any custom tooling?

options.A: Author a separate bespoke tool for every single GitHub API endpoint the team could conceivably need.
options.B: Prefer an existing community or official MCP server for the standard integration.
options.C: Have the agent call the GitHub REST API via raw Bash curl every time.
options.D: Embed all GitHub logic inside the project CLAUDE.md as instructions.

why: For standard integrations, reusing an existing community or official MCP server avoids writing and maintaining bespoke tool code.

explanations.A: Hand-authoring tools for every endpoint is the maintenance burden MCP exists to avoid.
explanations.B: For standard integrations, reusing an existing community or official MCP server avoids writing and maintaining bespoke tool code.
explanations.C: Raw curl scatters undocumented commands and lacks proper tool schemas.
explanations.D: CLAUDE.md gives guidance but does not provide executable tools or integration.

prompt: An MCP server exposes the production database schema as a resource. How does this most help the agent's workflow?

options.A: It lets the agent execute schema migrations automatically against production without any human review step.
options.B: It grants write access to the database tables through the resource URI.
options.C: It exposes the schema as content the agent can read, reducing exploratory tool calls.
options.D: It replaces the need for any CLAUDE.md documentation in the repo.

why: MCP resources expose content catalogs like schemas and docs, so the agent can read them directly instead of making many exploratory tool calls.

explanations.A: Resources expose read-only content; they do not execute migrations.
explanations.B: Resources are read-only content catalogs, not write paths.
explanations.C: MCP resources expose content catalogs like schemas and docs, so the agent can read them directly instead of making many exploratory tool calls.
explanations.D: Resources complement project memory; they do not replace CLAUDE.md guidance.

prompt: After adding a new MCP server to `.mcp.json` and reconnecting, when does the agent become aware of that server's tools?

options.A: Tools from all configured MCP servers are discovered at connection time.
options.B: Only after the tools are individually re-declared inside CLAUDE.md.
options.C: Only once the user manually types each tool name into the prompt.
options.D: Never, until the project is rebuilt and the CLI binary reinstalled.

why: Tools from every configured MCP server are discovered when the connection is established, making them available to the agent.

explanations.A: Tools from every configured MCP server are discovered when the connection is established, making them available to the agent.
explanations.B: MCP tools are discovered automatically; you do not redeclare them in CLAUDE.md.
explanations.C: Discovery is automatic at connection; users need not name tools manually.
explanations.D: No rebuild or reinstall is needed; reconnecting discovers the tools.

prompt: A teammate reports that a shared MCP server defined in `.mcp.json` fails to authenticate on their machine, though the config references `${JIRA_TOKEN}`. What is the most likely root cause?

options.A: Environment variable expansion is unsupported, so the token never resolves.
options.B: The teammate has not set the `JIRA_TOKEN` environment variable in their shell.
options.C: The token must be committed inline in `.mcp.json` to authenticate at all.
options.D: Shared servers can only ever live in user-level `~/.claude.json`.

why: The committed config references `${JIRA_TOKEN}` by design; each developer must supply their own value via the environment variable, which the teammate has not set.

explanations.A: Environment variable expansion is supported; that is the intended mechanism.
explanations.B: The committed config references `${JIRA_TOKEN}` by design; each developer must supply their own value via the environment variable, which the teammate has not set.
explanations.C: Committing secrets is wrong; expansion exists precisely to avoid that.
explanations.D: Shared servers belong in project `.mcp.json`; user config is for personal servers.

prompt: Two MCP server scopes are available: project-level `.mcp.json` and user-level `~/.claude.json`. Which statement best captures the trade-off?

options.A: Both scopes are identical except that one uses JSON and one uses YAML.
options.B: User-level config is committed and shared with the whole team, while project-level config stays private to you.
options.C: Project-level config is committed and shared; user-level is personal to your machine.
options.D: Project-level config can hold secrets safely while user-level cannot.

why: Project `.mcp.json` is checked in for shared team tooling, while user-level `~/.claude.json` holds personal or experimental servers for your machine only.

explanations.A: Both are JSON; the real difference is sharing scope, not format.
explanations.B: This reverses the truth: project-level is shared, user-level is private.
explanations.C: Project `.mcp.json` is checked in for shared team tooling, while user-level `~/.claude.json` holds personal or experimental servers for your machine only.
explanations.D: Neither scope should hold raw secrets; both rely on env var expansion.

prompt: A docs MCP server exposes a catalog of library documentation as resources. Why prefer reading that resource over having the agent Grep the codebase for usage examples?

options.A: The built-in Grep tool is automatically disabled whenever any MCP server is connected to the current session.
options.B: The resource provides authoritative content directly, cutting exploratory tool calls.
options.C: Resources execute the library code so the agent sees real runtime output.
options.D: Grep cannot search any file that a library has installed under node_modules.

why: Resources expose curated documentation as content, so the agent reads authoritative material instead of inferring usage from many exploratory searches.

explanations.A: Grep remains available; MCP servers do not disable built-in tools.
explanations.B: Resources expose curated documentation as content, so the agent reads authoritative material instead of inferring usage from many exploratory searches.
explanations.C: Resources expose content, not executed code or runtime output.
explanations.D: Grep can search those files; the advantage is reduced exploration, not a Grep limitation.

prompt: When running the agent in CI (e.g. GitHub Actions) with an MCP server, how must tool permissions be handled compared to local development?

options.A: Each tool from each MCP server must be listed explicitly in allowed tools.
options.B: Permissions are inherited automatically from the repository settings.
options.C: A single blanket permission covers every MCP operation in CI runs.
options.D: No permissions are needed because CI is a trusted environment.

why: Unlike local development, CI has no permission shortcut: every tool from every MCP server must be individually listed in the allowed tools.

explanations.A: Unlike local development, CI has no permission shortcut: every tool from every MCP server must be individually listed in the allowed tools.
explanations.B: CI does not auto-inherit tool permissions from repo settings.
explanations.C: There is no blanket permission; tools must be enumerated.
explanations.D: CI still requires explicit allow-listing of each tool.

prompt: A custom MCP tool named `query_db` is frequently misused by the agent, which passes free-text questions instead of SQL. The schema is correct. What should you improve first?

options.A: Rename the tool to `db` so the agent thinks less about what it does.
options.B: Lower the model's effort level so that it spends fewer reasoning tokens deciding how to make the call.
options.C: Sharpen the tool description and parameter descriptions to state SQL is required.
options.D: Add three more parameters so the agent has more options to choose from.

why: Clear, specific tool and parameter descriptions are what guide the model to call a tool correctly; stating that a valid SQL string is required fixes the misuse.

explanations.A: A shorter name gives the model less, not more, guidance about correct use.
explanations.B: Effort level does not fix an ambiguous tool contract.
explanations.C: Clear, specific tool and parameter descriptions are what guide the model to call a tool correctly; stating that a valid SQL string is required fixes the misuse.
explanations.D: More parameters add confusion; the issue is unclear guidance, not missing inputs.

prompt: When defining an MCP tool with the Python SDK, what is the role of the `Field(description=...)` on each parameter?

options.A: It encrypts the parameter value before the tool function receives it.
options.B: It helps the model understand what each parameter expects when calling the tool.
options.C: It sets a default value used whenever the model omits the parameter.
options.D: It registers the parameter as a separate standalone MCP resource exposed under its own URI.

why: Parameter field descriptions tell the model what each argument means, which is essential for the model to call the tool correctly.

explanations.A: Field descriptions do not encrypt anything; they document the parameter.
explanations.B: Parameter field descriptions tell the model what each argument means, which is essential for the model to call the tool correctly.
explanations.C: A description is documentation, not a default-value mechanism.
explanations.D: Descriptions do not create resources; they annotate tool parameters.

prompt: Your agent has many MCP servers connected, and `/context` shows tool definitions consuming a large share of the window before any work begins. What is the most effective remedy?

options.A: Disable the MCP servers not relevant to the current task to free context.
options.B: Increase the effort level so the model can reason past the clutter.
options.C: Read fewer project files so the tool definitions take relatively less.
options.D: Commit the tool definitions to CLAUDE.md so they load only once.

why: MCP servers load all their tool definitions into context even when unused, so disabling irrelevant servers reclaims that space.

explanations.A: MCP servers load all their tool definitions into context even when unused, so disabling irrelevant servers reclaims that space.
explanations.B: Effort level does not reduce the context cost of tool definitions.
explanations.C: Reading fewer files does not shrink the always-loaded tool definitions.
explanations.D: Putting definitions in CLAUDE.md would add context, not save it.

prompt: A tool has a CLI equivalent like `gh` for GitHub. Why might invoking the CLI through Bash be more context-efficient than the equivalent MCP server?

options.A: The CLI runs faster because it is compiled and the MCP server is interpreted.
options.B: The CLI returns smaller results that the model never has to read at all.
options.C: The CLI does not add persistent tool definitions to the context window.
options.D: The CLI bypasses the agentic loop and answers the user directly itself.

why: An MCP server loads tool definitions into context persistently; a CLI invoked via Bash adds no such definitions, so it is more context-efficient.

explanations.A: Execution speed is not the context argument; persistent definitions are.
explanations.B: CLI output still enters context; the saving is on tool definitions, not results.
explanations.C: An MCP server loads tool definitions into context persistently; a CLI invoked via Bash adds no such definitions, so it is more context-efficient.
explanations.D: The CLI runs through the Bash tool inside the loop; it does not bypass it.

prompt: If your MCP tools exceed roughly 10% of the context window, what does Claude Code do automatically?

options.A: It silently disconnects the least-recently-used MCP server until total usage falls back below 10%.
options.B: It switches to tool search mode, discovering the right tools on demand.
options.C: It compacts the entire conversation history to make room for tools.
options.D: It refuses to start the session until servers are manually disabled.

why: When MCP tools exceed about 10% of the window, Claude Code switches to tool search mode, discovering tools on demand rather than loading them all.

explanations.A: It does not drop servers; it changes how tools are surfaced.
explanations.B: When MCP tools exceed about 10% of the window, Claude Code switches to tool search mode, discovering tools on demand rather than loading them all.
explanations.C: Compaction summarizes conversation, not tool definitions, and is a different trigger.
explanations.D: The session still starts; it just adopts tool search mode.

prompt: An architect wants frequently-loaded capabilities that do not consume context up front the way MCP tool definitions do. Which option fits that goal?

options.A: Skills, which load only a name and description until Claude needs them.
options.B: Adding every server to `.mcp.json` so they share one definition block.
options.C: Raising the effort level so the model tolerates larger tool payloads.
options.D: Pasting the tool schemas directly into the user prompt each session.

why: Skills keep just a name and description in context and load the full contents only when needed, unlike MCP servers that load all tool definitions upfront.

explanations.A: Skills keep just a name and description in context and load the full contents only when needed, unlike MCP servers that load all tool definitions upfront.
explanations.B: More servers in `.mcp.json` add more definitions, increasing context cost.
explanations.C: Effort level has no effect on how tool definitions consume context.
explanations.D: Pasting schemas into the prompt adds context rather than saving it.

prompt: The agent must replace a config value but the surrounding text it picked is not unique in the file. Which sequence avoids the failure while still making a precise change?

options.A: Expand the matched text to include enough surrounding context to be unique, then Edit.
options.B: Glob the file repeatedly until the duplicate lines are filtered out.
options.C: Grep the value and let Grep overwrite the first occurrence it finds.
options.D: Read the file and then report back to the user that this particular value cannot be edited safely at all.

why: Edit requires a unique match, so widening the snippet with surrounding context makes it unique and lets the targeted Edit succeed.

explanations.A: Edit requires a unique match, so widening the snippet with surrounding context makes it unique and lets the targeted Edit succeed.
explanations.B: Glob matches paths, not lines, and cannot disambiguate text.
explanations.C: Grep does not modify files; it only searches content.
explanations.D: The edit is achievable by making the match unique or by Read plus Write.

prompt: An engineer asks the agent to find all TODO comments and also list every YAML file in the repo. Which pairing of tools is correct?

options.A: Glob for the TODO comments inside file content, and Grep for the `**/*.yaml` file paths by name.
options.B: Grep for `TODO` across file contents, and Glob for `**/*.yaml` file paths.
options.C: Read every file for TODOs, and Read the directory tree for YAML files.
options.D: Bash grep for both tasks since the built-in tools cannot do either job.

why: Grep searches contents (the TODO comments) while Glob matches file paths by pattern (the YAML files); each tool is used for its purpose.

explanations.A: This swaps the tools: Glob does not search content and Grep does not match paths.
explanations.B: Grep searches contents (the TODO comments) while Glob matches file paths by pattern (the YAML files); each tool is used for its purpose.
explanations.C: Read works on single files and cannot enumerate matches across a tree.
explanations.D: The built-in Grep and Glob handle these tasks directly; Bash is unnecessary.

prompt: The agent has the file path and wants the complete current contents of a 50-line module before reasoning about a refactor. Which tool should it use?

options.A: Edit, since reading and editing are the same underlying operation.
options.B: Glob, since matching the path returns the file's full contents.
options.C: Read, which performs a full-file operation to return the contents.
options.D: Grep, since searching for any character returns the whole file.

why: Read is the full-file operation for retrieving a file's complete contents, which is what the refactor reasoning needs.

explanations.A: Edit modifies text; it is not for retrieving full contents.
explanations.B: Glob returns matching paths, not the contents of files.
explanations.C: Read is the full-file operation for retrieving a file's complete contents, which is what the refactor reasoning needs.
explanations.D: Grep returns matching lines with context, not a guaranteed full-file read.

prompt: A team standardizes on a Playwright MCP server for browser-driven UI checks. What is the cleanest way to ensure every teammate gets it automatically?

options.A: Email teammates the `claude mcp add` command to run individually.
options.B: Define it in the committed project `.mcp.json` so it is shared on checkout.
options.C: Add it to each person's user-level `~/.claude.json` together over a guided screen-share session.
options.D: Document the install steps in a wiki and ask everyone to follow them.

why: A project-scoped `.mcp.json` under version control gives everyone on the codebase the same server automatically when they check it out.

explanations.A: Manual per-developer setup is error-prone and not automatic.
explanations.B: A project-scoped `.mcp.json` under version control gives everyone on the codebase the same server automatically when they check it out.
explanations.C: User-level config is personal and not shared with the team.
explanations.D: A wiki relies on manual action; committed config is automatic.

prompt: A team coding convention (use server actions, not API routes) should apply to everyone on the project. Where should it live so the whole team benefits via source control?

options.A: In the user-level `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`, which applies to every project on your own machine.
options.B: In the project root `CLAUDE.md`, which is committed and shared with the team.
options.C: In `CLAUDE.local.md`, which holds personal, uncommitted instructions.
options.D: In a custom slash command file under `.claude/commands/`.

why: Project-root CLAUDE.md is committed to source control and shared, so team conventions belong there.

explanations.A: User-level memory is personal to your machine and is not shared with the team.
explanations.B: Project-root CLAUDE.md is committed to source control and shared, so team conventions belong there.
explanations.C: CLAUDE.local.md is not shared with other engineers; it is for personal customizations.
explanations.D: Commands automate workflows; they are not the place for always-on conventions.

prompt: You want certain instructions to apply across every project you personally work on, regardless of the repo. Which file is appropriate?

options.A: The project root `CLAUDE.md`, committed separately in each and every repository you happen to touch.
options.B: A `.claude/rules/` file added separately to every repository you use.
options.C: The user-level `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`, used across all projects on your machine.
options.D: A `CLAUDE.local.md` placed once in your home directory's root.

why: The user-level `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` applies to all projects on your machine, which is exactly the personal cross-project scope.

explanations.A: Project-root files apply per repo and are shared, not personal across all projects.
explanations.B: Per-repo rules files do not span all your projects automatically.
explanations.C: The user-level `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` applies to all projects on your machine, which is exactly the personal cross-project scope.
explanations.D: CLAUDE.local.md is a per-project, uncommitted file, not a global personal one.

prompt: Your repo already has an `AGENTS.md` for another tool and you do not want to duplicate its content into `CLAUDE.md`. What is the recommended approach?

options.A: Copy the full AGENTS.md text into CLAUDE.md and then keep both copies in sync manually by hand.
options.B: Rename AGENTS.md to CLAUDE.md so Claude reads it as project memory.
options.C: Add `@AGENTS.md` on the first line of CLAUDE.md to import its contents.
options.D: Configure the import inside the project `.mcp.json` server block.

why: The @import syntax (`@AGENTS.md` on the first line of CLAUDE.md) loads that file's contents first, avoiding duplication while keeping Claude-specific notes below.

explanations.A: Manual duplication is exactly what the @import syntax avoids.
explanations.B: Renaming breaks the other tool that relies on AGENTS.md.
explanations.C: The @import syntax (`@AGENTS.md` on the first line of CLAUDE.md) loads that file's contents first, avoiding duplication while keeping Claude-specific notes below.
explanations.D: `.mcp.json` configures servers, not memory-file imports.

prompt: You added a new directory-level `CLAUDE.md` and edited the project root one, but suspect the agent is not loading what you expect. How do you verify which memory files are active?

options.A: Run `/memory` to see and verify the loaded memory files.
options.B: Run `/compact` and read the summary to infer which files loaded.
options.C: Delete the root file and watch whether the agent's behavior changes.
options.D: Inspect `.mcp.json` to confirm the memory files are registered there.

why: The `/memory` command lets you view and verify which CLAUDE.md files are currently loaded into the session.

explanations.A: The `/memory` command lets you view and verify which CLAUDE.md files are currently loaded into the session.
explanations.B: Compaction summarizes conversation; it does not report loaded memory files.
explanations.C: Destructive trial-and-error is unnecessary when `/memory` reports the truth.
explanations.D: Memory files are not registered in `.mcp.json`; that file configures servers.

prompt: An engineer keeps personal scratch notes and shortcuts that should never reach teammates. Which file should hold them?

options.A: The committed project root `CLAUDE.md` so they are versioned safely.
options.B: `CLAUDE.local.md`, which holds personal instructions and is not shared.
options.C: A `.claude/rules/` file committed alongside the team's shared rules.
options.D: The user-level config file so that the personal notes follow them into every single project they open.

why: CLAUDE.local.md is the uncommitted, personal file meant for instructions you do not share with other engineers.

explanations.A: The root file is shared with the team, so personal notes do not belong there.
explanations.B: CLAUDE.local.md is the uncommitted, personal file meant for instructions you do not share with other engineers.
explanations.C: Committed rules files are shared; they are not for personal-only notes.
explanations.D: User-level memory spans all projects, which is broader than per-project personal notes.

prompt: A database schema file is relevant to many tasks, and you want its contents available without the agent searching for it each time. How should you wire this into project memory?

options.A: Paste the entire schema file's text directly into the body of CLAUDE.md and maintain it there.
options.B: Reference it with `@prisma/schema.prisma` so its contents load each request.
options.C: Add the schema path to the deny list in `.claude/settings.json`.
options.D: Expose it only through a custom slash command the user must invoke.

why: Using @import in CLAUDE.md pulls the file's contents into every request, so the agent can answer schema questions without searching.

explanations.A: Pasting the full schema bloats CLAUDE.md and drifts out of sync with the source.
explanations.B: Using @import in CLAUDE.md pulls the file's contents into every request, so the agent can answer schema questions without searching.
explanations.C: A deny rule blocks access; it does not preload the file.
explanations.D: A command requires manual invocation; @import makes the content always available.

prompt: When two CLAUDE.md instructions conflict — one in the user-level file and one in the project root file — which guidance reflects the intended hierarchy for a project?

options.A: Project-level memory governs project work; user-level holds personal defaults.
options.B: User-level always wins because it is loaded earliest in the session.
options.C: Whichever file is larger in bytes is treated as authoritative by Claude.
options.D: Both memory files are ignored entirely unless the same instruction is also repeated inside a slash command.

why: Project-level CLAUDE.md captures project-specific, team-shared guidance, while user-level holds your personal cross-project defaults that the project context refines.

explanations.A: Project-level CLAUDE.md captures project-specific, team-shared guidance, while user-level holds your personal cross-project defaults that the project context refines.
explanations.B: Load order does not make user-level override project-specific guidance for a project.
explanations.C: File size has no bearing on precedence.
explanations.D: Memory files are honored without needing a slash command to restate them.

prompt: An engineer is about to ask the agent for a multi-file feature touching an unfamiliar pipeline and wants to review the approach before any code is written. Which mode fits best?

options.A: Plan mode, which reads files and returns a reviewable plan before executing.
options.B: Direct execution with auto-accept so edits land as fast as possible.
options.C: Raising the effort level, which produces the plan and then immediately applies it all at once without review.
options.D: A custom slash command that commits the change without a plan step.

why: Plan mode uses read-only tools to research the codebase and produce a plan you approve before any edits, ideal for broad multi-file work.

explanations.A: Plan mode uses read-only tools to research the codebase and produce a plan you approve before any edits, ideal for broad multi-file work.
explanations.B: Auto-accept skips the review step the engineer explicitly wants.
explanations.C: Effort level controls reasoning depth, not the plan-then-approve gate.
explanations.D: A commit command does not provide the pre-implementation review requested.

prompt: Choosing between plan mode and a higher effort level: which framing best describes when to use each?

options.A: Plan mode is strictly for making fast edits, whereas a higher effort level is strictly for slow and careful edits.
options.B: Plan mode handles breadth across the codebase; higher effort handles depth on hard logic.
options.C: Plan mode disables tools entirely; higher effort enables every tool.
options.D: Plan mode is cheaper in tokens; higher effort is always free to use.

why: Plan mode suits multi-step, multi-file breadth, while a higher effort level suits deep reasoning on complex logic or debugging.

explanations.A: Neither is primarily about edit speed; they address breadth versus depth.
explanations.B: Plan mode suits multi-step, multi-file breadth, while a higher effort level suits deep reasoning on complex logic or debugging.
explanations.C: Plan mode still uses read-only tools; effort level does not toggle tool access.
explanations.D: Both consume extra tokens; cost is not the distinguishing factor here.

prompt: An engineer only wants a summary of where authentication lives, with the verbose file-by-file discovery kept out of the main conversation. What is the best approach?

options.A: Read every candidate auth file directly in the main thread and skim through all of the output yourself.
options.B: Use the Explore subagent so discovery runs in isolation and returns a summary.
options.C: Enter plan mode and approve the plan to surface the discovery inline.
options.D: Run `/compact` first so the discovery output is summarized afterward.

why: The Explore subagent runs the verbose exploration in its own context and returns just a summary, keeping the main context clean.

explanations.A: Reading in the main thread is exactly the verbose clutter to avoid.
explanations.B: The Explore subagent runs the verbose exploration in its own context and returns just a summary, keeping the main context clean.
explanations.C: Plan mode is for planning changes; here no change is intended, just a summary.
explanations.D: Compaction acts after the fact; it does not isolate discovery up front.

prompt: While reviewing a generated plan, an engineer notices a missing edge case. What is the appropriate action before any code is written?

options.A: Approve the plan as written and then circle back to fix the missing edge case in a follow-up prompt later.
options.B: Revise the plan (or edit it) so the edge case is covered before approving.
options.C: Switch to a higher effort level and resubmit the same prompt verbatim.
options.D: Clear the session so the planner forgets the flawed first attempt.

why: Plan mode exists precisely so you can course-correct before code is written; revise or edit the plan to cover the edge case, then approve.

explanations.A: Approving locks in the gap; the pre-code review is the cheapest place to fix it.
explanations.B: Plan mode exists precisely so you can course-correct before code is written; revise or edit the plan to cover the edge case, then approve.
explanations.C: Effort level does not address a specific missing requirement in the plan.
explanations.D: Clearing discards useful context; revising the plan is the targeted fix.

prompt: Your team runs the same boilerplate-generation steps constantly with project-specific conventions. What is the cleanest way to standardize this into one repeatable invocation?

options.A: Paste the full instructions into the prompt manually on each run.
options.B: Create a custom slash command markdown file under `.claude/commands/`.
options.C: Add the steps to `.mcp.json` so a server runs them automatically.
options.D: Define a PreToolUse hook that emits the boilerplate instructions.

why: A markdown file in `.claude/commands/` becomes a slash command, turning the repeated workflow with its conventions into one reusable invocation.

explanations.A: Manual pasting is the repetition a custom command eliminates.
explanations.B: A markdown file in `.claude/commands/` becomes a slash command, turning the repeated workflow with its conventions into one reusable invocation.
explanations.C: `.mcp.json` configures servers, not reusable prompt workflows.
explanations.D: Hooks intercept tool calls; they are not an on-demand workflow command.

prompt: A custom command should accept a file path the engineer passes at runtime, like `/write_tests the use-auth.ts file`. How do you make it accept that input?

options.A: Include the `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder in the command's markdown file.
options.B: Add a `@parameters` decorator at the top of the command file.
options.C: Declare the parameters inside `.claude/settings.json` permissions.
options.D: Append command-line flags when invoking the command in the prompt.

why: The `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder in the command file captures whatever the user passes at runtime, making the command reusable with different inputs.

explanations.A: The `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder in the command file captures whatever the user passes at runtime, making the command reusable with different inputs.
explanations.B: There is no `@parameters` decorator for custom commands.
explanations.C: Settings configure permissions and hooks, not command arguments.
explanations.D: Arguments flow through `$ARGUMENTS`, not CLI flags typed into the prompt.

prompt: After creating a new file `audit.md` in `.claude/commands/`, what must the engineer do for the `/audit` command to become available?

options.A: Nothing extra; Claude Code picks the command up automatically without a restart.
options.B: Restart Claude Code so it rescans the commands directory on launch.
options.C: Register the command name inside `.claude/settings.json` first.
options.D: Run `/init` once again so that Claude regenerates its entire command index from scratch on launch.

why: After creating the command file, Claude Code detects it automatically and no restart is required.

explanations.A: After creating the command file, Claude Code detects it automatically and no restart is required.
explanations.B: No restart is needed; the command is picked up automatically.
explanations.C: Commands are not registered in settings; the filename defines the command.
explanations.D: `/init` analyzes the codebase for CLAUDE.md; it is unrelated to command discovery.

prompt: An engineer wants a capability with a name and short description loaded into context, where the full instructions load only when Claude decides to use it. Which mechanism is that?

options.A: A Skill, whose full contents load on demand when Claude needs it.
options.B: An MCP server, which loads only metadata until the first tool call.
options.C: A CLAUDE.md @import, which streams its target file in piece by piece.
options.D: A PostToolUse hook, which injects instructions after each tool call.

why: A Skill keeps only its name and description in context and loads the full instructions only when Claude determines it should use it.

explanations.A: A Skill keeps only its name and description in context and loads the full instructions only when Claude determines it should use it.
explanations.B: MCP servers load all their tool definitions into context upfront, not lazily.
explanations.C: An @import loads the full referenced file into every request, not on demand.
explanations.D: Hooks run commands around tool calls; they are not lazily-loaded capabilities.

prompt: A team wants the full commit, push, and PR-creation flow handled in one step instead of three manual commands. Which built-in feature does this?

options.A: The `/commit-push-pr` skill, which handles all three steps in one invocation.
options.B: A PreToolUse hook matched to the Bash tool running git commands.
options.C: The `/init` command, which sets up the repository for committing.
options.D: An MCP resource that exposes the full git history and pending diff as readable content for review.

why: The `/commit-push-pr` skill performs the commit, push, and PR creation together in a single step.

explanations.A: The `/commit-push-pr` skill performs the commit, push, and PR creation together in a single step.
explanations.B: A hook intercepts tool calls; it does not orchestrate the full PR workflow on request.
explanations.C: `/init` generates a CLAUDE.md; it does not commit or open PRs.
explanations.D: A resource exposes content; it does not perform commits or PRs.

prompt: An engineer keeps correcting the agent to never add API routes. To stop the repetition across future sessions, what is the most durable fix?

options.A: Repeat the correction at the start of every new session manually.
options.B: Save the rule to CLAUDE.md so it loads automatically next session.
options.C: Lower the effort level so the agent makes fewer creative choices.
options.D: Disable the Write tool so it cannot create API route files at all.

why: Persisting the correction in CLAUDE.md means the agent reads it at the start of every session, preventing the repeated mistake.

explanations.A: Manual repetition each session is the toil a memory file removes.
explanations.B: Persisting the correction in CLAUDE.md means the agent reads it at the start of every session, preventing the repeated mistake.
explanations.C: Effort level does not encode a specific project rule.
explanations.D: Disabling Write blocks legitimate edits too; a memory rule is the right scope.

prompt: A task requires both broad understanding across many files and deep reasoning about a tricky algorithm. What is a reasonable configuration?

options.A: Use plan mode for the breadth and raise the effort level for the depth.
options.B: Use only auto-accept mode, which covers both breadth and depth at once.
options.C: Use only the lowest effort level to keep the long task affordable.
options.D: Avoid both features since combining them is not supported together.

why: The two features compose: plan mode handles broad multi-file understanding while a higher effort level handles the deep algorithmic reasoning.

explanations.A: The two features compose: plan mode handles broad multi-file understanding while a higher effort level handles the deep algorithmic reasoning.
explanations.B: Auto-accept governs edit approval, not breadth or reasoning depth.
explanations.C: Lowest effort undercuts the deep reasoning the algorithm needs.
explanations.D: Combining plan mode and higher effort is explicitly supported.

prompt: When you start a fresh session in a project that has a project-root `CLAUDE.md`, how is that file used?

options.A: Claude reads it automatically at the start and appends it to your prompt.
options.B: It loads only after you explicitly run `/init` in the session.
options.C: It loads only when you mention a file in it with the `@` symbol.
options.D: It is ignored unless it is also copied into `.claude/settings.json`.

why: Claude reads the project CLAUDE.md automatically at session start and appends its contents, acting like a persistent project system prompt.

explanations.A: Claude reads the project CLAUDE.md automatically at session start and appends its contents, acting like a persistent project system prompt.
explanations.B: `/init` generates the file; an existing one loads automatically without it.
explanations.C: The file loads regardless; @import additionally pulls referenced files.
explanations.D: CLAUDE.md is read directly; it is not registered in settings.

prompt: An engineer wants a reusable code-reviewer that runs with fresh context and is restricted to read-only tools so it never edits files. What should they configure?

options.A: A subagent definition checked into the repo, scoped to read-only tools.
options.B: A PostToolUse hook that re-runs the linter after every file edit.
options.C: An MCP resource exposing the diff so the main agent reviews itself.
options.D: A user-level CLAUDE.md note asking the agent to be more careful.

why: A code-reviewer subagent runs in its own fresh context and should be restricted to read-only tools and committed so the whole team uses the same reviewer.

explanations.A: A code-reviewer subagent runs in its own fresh context and should be restricted to read-only tools and committed so the whole team uses the same reviewer.
explanations.B: A linter hook formats or checks; it is not a fresh-context reviewer agent.
explanations.C: Self-review carries the main agent's bias and a resource is not a reviewer.
explanations.D: A memory note does not create an isolated read-only reviewer.

prompt: While hand-rolling the agent loop, the model returns a `tool_use` block. After you execute the tool, how must you send the result back so the conversation stays valid?

options.A: Append a `tool_result` block keyed to the matching `tool_use_id`.
options.B: Append the output as a plain assistant text message in the history.
options.C: Send the output as a brand-new system prompt for the next turn.
options.D: Discard the output and let the model re-derive it on the next call.

why: Each tool result must be a `tool_result` block carrying the same `tool_use_id` as the request so the model can correlate it with the call.

explanations.A: Each tool result must be a `tool_result` block carrying the same `tool_use_id` as the request so the model can correlate it with the call.
explanations.B: Tool output must be a structured tool_result, not free assistant text.
explanations.C: Results do not go in the system prompt; they are user-role tool_result blocks.
explanations.D: Discarding the result breaks the loop; the model needs the tool_result to proceed.

prompt: Your hand-built loop returns a response whose `stop_reason` is `end_turn`. What does this signal about the agentic loop?

options.A: The model wants another tool executed before it can continue.
options.B: The model has finished this turn and the loop should stop iterating.
options.C: The request hit the token limit and must be retried with more budget.
options.D: An error occurred and the loop should restart from the first prompt.

why: `end_turn` means the model has completed its turn, so the loop terminates rather than executing more tools.

explanations.A: A pending tool request is signaled by `tool_use`, not `end_turn`.
explanations.B: `end_turn` means the model has completed its turn, so the loop terminates rather than executing more tools.
explanations.C: A length cutoff has its own stop reason; `end_turn` is normal completion.
explanations.D: `end_turn` is a clean finish, not an error condition.

prompt: A response contains two `tool_use` blocks in a single assistant turn. How should the loop respond before the next model call?

options.A: Execute only the first tool and ignore the second to keep it simple.
options.B: Merge both requests into one tool call and return a single result.
options.C: Execute both tools and append a tool_result for each, keyed by its id.
options.D: Stop the loop because multiple tool calls indicate a malformed reply.

why: Every tool_use block must get its own tool_result keyed to the matching tool_use_id before the next model call.

explanations.A: Ignoring a requested tool leaves an unmatched tool_use and breaks the turn.
explanations.B: Each tool_use is distinct and needs its own correlated result.
explanations.C: Every tool_use block must get its own tool_result keyed to the matching tool_use_id before the next model call.
explanations.D: Multiple tool_use blocks are valid; the loop handles each one.

prompt: An agent's main context is filling with verbose exploration output from answering 'where are the auth endpoints?'. Which technique keeps the main context clean?

options.A: Delegate the exploration to a subagent that returns only a summary.
options.B: Raise the effort level so the model reasons through the clutter faster.
options.C: Read every candidate file in the main thread to settle it quickly.
options.D: Add the endpoints to `.mcp.json` so they no longer need exploring.

why: A subagent works in its own isolated context and returns just the answer, so the verbose exploration never clutters the main context.

explanations.A: A subagent works in its own isolated context and returns just the answer, so the verbose exploration never clutters the main context.
explanations.B: Effort level does not reduce how much exploration output enters context.
explanations.C: Reading in the main thread is what fills the context in the first place.
explanations.D: `.mcp.json` configures servers; it does not store discovered endpoints.

prompt: What is the defining property of a subagent's context relative to the main agent?

options.A: It shares the very same context window, so both agents see every piece of intermediate output produced.
options.B: It operates in its own isolated context window separate from the main agent.
options.C: It can only ever run after the main agent has fully stopped.
options.D: It permanently inherits every tool result the main agent produced.

why: Each subagent runs in a completely separate context window, which is why only its summary returns to the main agent.

explanations.A: Sharing context would defeat the purpose; subagents are isolated.
explanations.B: Each subagent runs in a completely separate context window, which is why only its summary returns to the main agent.
explanations.C: Subagents run in parallel with the main agent, not strictly afterward.
explanations.D: Isolated context means it does not inherit the main agent's tool results.

prompt: Why is a subagent recommended for a pre-push code review rather than asking the main agent to review its own work?

options.A: A subagent runs faster because it skips the agentic loop entirely.
options.B: A subagent can edit files directly while the main agent cannot.
options.C: A subagent gives a fresh perspective without the main agent's session bias.
options.D: A subagent is the only component permitted to call the Bash tool.

why: A reviewer subagent has fresh context and does not carry the bias the main agent built up while writing the code.

explanations.A: Subagents still use the loop; the value is fresh, unbiased context.
explanations.B: A reviewer should be read-only; editing is not the reason to use one.
explanations.C: A reviewer subagent has fresh context and does not carry the bias the main agent built up while writing the code.
explanations.D: Both can call Bash subject to permissions; that is not the rationale.

prompt: An engineer ended a session yesterday mid-refactor and wants to continue exactly where they left off today. Which capability does that?

options.A: Resuming the prior session so its accumulated context is restored.
options.B: Running `/clear`, which starts fresh while keeping yesterday's memory.
options.C: Running `/init`, which reloads the previous conversation automatically.
options.D: Adding the work to `.mcp.json` so the next session reconnects to it.

why: Resuming a session restores the prior conversation and context so work continues from where it stopped.

explanations.A: Resuming a session restores the prior conversation and context so work continues from where it stopped.
explanations.B: `/clear` discards context; it does not resume the prior session.
explanations.C: `/init` generates a CLAUDE.md; it does not restore a session.
explanations.D: `.mcp.json` configures servers; it does not persist a session's progress.

prompt: Using the Agent SDK, an engineer wants to branch from an existing session to try an alternative approach without disturbing the original. Which option supports this?

options.A: `fork_session`, which branches from the existing session into a new one.
options.B: `allowedTools`, which restricts the tool set the new branch may use.
options.C: `/compact`, which copies the session into a summarized duplicate.
options.D: A PreToolUse hook, which automatically clones the entire conversation just before each and every tool call.

why: Forking a session branches off the existing one, letting you explore an alternative path while preserving the original session.

explanations.A: Forking a session branches off the existing one, letting you explore an alternative path while preserving the original session.
explanations.B: `allowedTools` limits tools; it does not branch a session.
explanations.C: Compaction summarizes the current session; it does not fork a branch.
explanations.D: Hooks intercept tool calls; they do not fork sessions.

prompt: After resuming a session with `--resume`, the engineer had edited several files outside Claude in the meantime. What is the most effective first step?

options.A: Immediately ask for edits, trusting the resumed context is still accurate.
options.B: Inform the resumed session which files changed so its context is current.
options.C: Fork the session first so the stale context is duplicated safely.
options.D: Lower the effort level so the model relies less on remembered files.

why: A resumed session holds a stale view, so telling it which files changed lets it re-read them and avoid acting on outdated content.

explanations.A: The resumed context predates the external edits, so it may be stale.
explanations.B: A resumed session holds a stale view, so telling it which files changed lets it re-read them and avoid acting on outdated content.
explanations.C: Forking copies the stale state; it does not refresh it.
explanations.D: Effort level does not update the session's stale file knowledge.

prompt: When Claude opens a PR via `gh pr create`, the session is linked to that PR. How does the engineer later resume work on that PR?

options.A: Re-run `/init` inside the PR branch to relink the session to it.
options.B: Run `claude --from-pr <PR_NUMBER>` to pick up where they left off.
options.C: Open a brand-new session and paste the PR diff into the first prompt.
options.D: Add the PR number to `.mcp.json` so the server reattaches the session.

why: `claude --from-pr <PR_NUMBER>` resumes the session linked to that PR, restoring context to address reviews or fix builds.

explanations.A: `/init` generates CLAUDE.md; it does not resume a PR-linked session.
explanations.B: `claude --from-pr <PR_NUMBER>` resumes the session linked to that PR, restoring context to address reviews or fix builds.
explanations.C: A fresh session loses the linked context that --from-pr restores.
explanations.D: `.mcp.json` configures servers and has nothing to do with PR linkage.

prompt: An engineer wants the agent to think harder on a single difficult prompt without changing the session's effort level. What achieves that?

options.A: Include the keyword `ultrathink` in that prompt to signal extra reasoning.
options.B: Run `/effort max`, which only ever affects the one next prompt.
options.C: Switch to plan mode, which forces deep reasoning on every reply.
options.D: Add a Stop hook that re-prompts the model to reconsider its answer.

why: The `ultrathink` keyword signals more reasoning for that single turn without altering the session-wide effort level.

explanations.A: The `ultrathink` keyword signals more reasoning for that single turn without altering the session-wide effort level.
explanations.B: `/effort` changes the session's level persistently, not just one prompt.
explanations.C: Plan mode adds breadth and a planning gate, not per-prompt reasoning depth.
explanations.D: A Stop hook runs after responses; it is not a per-prompt reasoning signal.

prompt: A subagent is configured to preload a skill. How does skill loading differ inside a subagent compared to the main conversation?

options.A: The subagent loads the entire skill into context, unlike the main conversation.
options.B: The subagent loads only the skill's name, never its instructions.
options.C: The subagent cannot use skills at all and must use MCP tools instead.
options.D: The subagent simply shares the main conversation's already-loaded skill state instead of loading its own.

why: When preloaded into a subagent, the whole skill is loaded into its context, whereas in the main conversation a skill loads its full contents only on demand.

explanations.A: When preloaded into a subagent, the whole skill is loaded into its context, whereas in the main conversation a skill loads its full contents only on demand.
explanations.B: Preloading puts the full skill into the subagent, not just the name.
explanations.C: Subagents can preload skills; they are not limited to MCP tools.
explanations.D: Subagents have isolated context; they do not share loaded skill state.

prompt: An engineer hand-builds an SDK loop but forgets to feed each tool's output back before the next model call. What failure should they expect?

options.A: The model keeps acting as if the tool never ran, unable to use the result.
options.B: The model automatically re-runs every tool until results appear on their own.
options.C: The session immediately ends with a permanent permission error.
options.D: The CLI silently switches to tool search mode to recover the output.

why: Without appending the tool_result keyed to its tool_use_id, the model has no record of the output and cannot progress on it.

explanations.A: Without appending the tool_result keyed to its tool_use_id, the model has no record of the output and cannot progress on it.
explanations.B: The model does not auto-rerun tools; the loop owner must supply results.
explanations.C: Missing results are a protocol gap, not a permission error.
explanations.D: Tool search mode addresses context size of definitions, not missing results.

prompt: Your pipeline script runs `claude "Analyze this pull request for security issues"` but the job hangs indefinitely. Logs indicate Claude Code is waiting for interactive input. What's the correct approach to run Claude Code in an automated pipeline?

options.A: Add the `-p` flag: `claude -p "Analyze this pull request for security issues"`.
options.B: Set the environment variable `CLAUDE_HEADLESS=true` before running the command.
options.C: Redirect stdin from /dev/null: `claude "..." < /dev/null`.
options.D: Add the `--batch` flag: `claude --batch "..."`.

why: The `-p` (or `--print`) flag is the documented way to run Claude Code in non-interactive mode. It processes the prompt, outputs the result to stdout, and exits without waiting for user input — exactly what CI/CD pipelines require.

explanations.A: The `-p` (or `--print`) flag is the documented way to run Claude Code in non-interactive mode. It processes the prompt, outputs the result to stdout, and exits without waiting for user input — exactly what CI/CD pipelines require.
explanations.B: This references a non-existent feature.
explanations.C: A Unix workaround that doesn't properly address Claude Code's command syntax.
explanations.D: This references a non-existent flag.

prompt: Your team wants to reduce API costs. Real-time Claude calls power two workflows: (1) a blocking pre-merge check that must complete before developers can merge, and (2) a technical debt report generated overnight for review the next morning. Your manager proposes switching both to the Message Batches API for its 50% cost savings. How should you evaluate this?

options.A: Use batch processing for the technical debt reports only; keep real-time calls for pre-merge checks.
options.B: Switch both workflows to batch processing with status polling to check for completion.
options.C: Keep real-time calls for both workflows to avoid batch result ordering issues.
options.D: Switch both to batch processing with a timeout fallback to real-time if batches take too long.

why: The Batches API offers 50% savings but has processing times up to 24 hours with no latency SLA — unsuitable for blocking pre-merge checks where developers wait, but ideal for overnight batch jobs.

explanations.A: The Batches API offers 50% savings but has processing times up to 24 hours with no latency SLA — unsuitable for blocking pre-merge checks where developers wait, but ideal for overnight batch jobs.
explanations.B: Relying on "often faster" completion isn't acceptable for blocking workflows.
explanations.C: A misconception — batch results can be correlated using `custom_id` fields.
explanations.D: Adds unnecessary complexity when matching each API to its appropriate use case is simpler.

prompt: A pull request modifies 14 files across the stock tracking module. Your single-pass review analyzing all files together produces inconsistent results: detailed feedback for some files but superficial comments for others, obvious bugs missed, and contradictory feedback. How should you restructure the review?

options.A: Split into focused passes: analyze each file individually for local issues, then run a separate integration-focused pass examining cross-file data flow.
options.B: Require developers to split large PRs into smaller submissions of 3-4 files before the automated review runs.
options.C: Switch to a higher-tier model with a larger context window to give all 14 files adequate attention in one pass.
options.D: Run three independent review passes on the full PR and only flag issues that appear in at least two of the three runs.

why: Splitting reviews into focused passes addresses the root cause: attention dilution when processing many files at once. File-by-file analysis ensures consistent depth, and a separate integration pass catches cross-file issues.

explanations.A: Splitting reviews into focused passes addresses the root cause: attention dilution when processing many files at once. File-by-file analysis ensures consistent depth, and a separate integration pass catches cross-file issues.
explanations.B: Shifts burden to developers without addressing the review architecture.
explanations.C: A larger window doesn't solve attention dilution across many simultaneous files.
explanations.D: Voting across full-PR passes still suffers the same per-pass attention dilution.

prompt: Developers complain your CI review bot flags too many false positives on comment style and local naming, eroding trust even though its security findings are accurate. What's the most effective prompt change?

options.A: Write specific review criteria defining which issues to report (bugs, security) versus skip (minor style, local patterns), rather than relying on confidence-based filtering.
options.B: Add "only report high-confidence findings" to the prompt.
options.C: Reduce the model temperature so it produces fewer findings overall.
options.D: Have the bot post every finding but sort them by a self-reported severity score.

why: Explicit categorical criteria outperform vague instructions like "be conservative." Defining exactly what to report and what to skip restores trust without suppressing accurate categories.

explanations.A: Explicit categorical criteria outperform vague instructions like "be conservative." Defining exactly what to report and what to skip restores trust without suppressing accurate categories.
explanations.B: General confidence-based instructions fail to improve precision compared to specific categorical criteria.
explanations.C: Temperature reduces variability, not the rate of low-value categories; it could also suppress accurate findings.
explanations.D: This still floods developers with the false positives that erode trust.

prompt: Your CI job posts Claude's review as a PR comment, but a downstream script must parse the findings to set a pass/fail status. Right now it tries to regex the plain-text output and frequently breaks. What's the most reliable way to get machine-parseable findings?

options.A: Run `claude -p` with `--output-format json` so the result is a structured object.
options.B: Ask Claude in the prompt to "always format your output as valid JSON" and parse the stdout text.
options.C: Pipe the text output through a second `claude -p` call that reformats it into JSON.
options.D: Set `--output-format markdown` and parse the headings with a Markdown library.

why: `--output-format json` produces a structured, machine-parseable result instead of free-form prose, removing the brittle regex parsing that breaks on formatting changes.

explanations.A: `--output-format json` produces a structured, machine-parseable result instead of free-form prose, removing the brittle regex parsing that breaks on formatting changes.
explanations.B: Prompt-only formatting is unreliable; Claude may still wrap output in prose or code fences, so a dedicated output format is preferable.
explanations.C: A second model call adds cost and latency and is unnecessary when a structured output format exists.
explanations.D: Parsing Markdown headings is still brittle, and the goal is a guaranteed machine-readable shape, not prose.

prompt: You use `--output-format json`, but the schema of the findings varies between runs — sometimes a `severity` field is present, sometimes not — which breaks your PR-comment formatter. How do you constrain the output shape?

options.A: Provide a schema via `--json-schema` so findings share required fields.
options.B: Add a retry loop that re-runs the job until the output happens to include all fields.
options.C: Lower the temperature to zero so the field set is deterministic across runs.
options.D: Have the formatter default any missing fields to `null` and ignore the inconsistency.

why: Supplying a `--json-schema` constrains the output to a defined shape with required fields, so the formatter receives a consistent structure every run.

explanations.A: Supplying a `--json-schema` constrains the output to a defined shape with required fields, so the formatter receives a consistent structure every run.
explanations.B: Retrying until the shape matches is wasteful and non-deterministic; a schema enforces the structure directly.
explanations.C: Temperature affects token sampling, not which optional fields appear; it does not guarantee a schema.
explanations.D: Patching downstream hides the problem but loses required severity data; enforcing the schema at the source is correct.

prompt: A new engineer asks why the CI review job pairs `-p` with `--output-format json` instead of just using `-p` alone. What's the best explanation of what each part contributes?

options.A: `-p` makes the run non-interactive so it won't hang; `--output-format json` makes results parseable.
options.B: `-p` enables JSON internally and `--output-format json` is only a redundant alias for older versions.
options.C: `--output-format json` runs the job headless and `-p` pretty-prints the JSON for humans.
options.D: Both flags are required only when an MCP server is attached and are optional otherwise.

why: `-p` handles the headless/non-interactive concern (no hang), and `--output-format json` handles machine-readability — two distinct needs in CI.

explanations.A: `-p` handles the headless/non-interactive concern (no hang), and `--output-format json` handles machine-readability — two distinct needs in CI.
explanations.B: `-p` controls interactivity, not output format; the two flags are not aliases.
explanations.C: This reverses the roles of the two flags.
explanations.D: Neither flag is tied to MCP usage; they address interactivity and output shape generally.

prompt: The CI review repeatedly criticizes the team's intentional use of integration tests over unit tests and flags the use of a custom logging wrapper as "non-standard." The criteria are correct in general but wrong for this project. What's the most effective fix?

options.A: Document the project's testing standards and accepted conventions in `CLAUDE.md`.
options.B: Append the conventions to every PR description so they appear in the diff Claude reviews.
options.C: Lower the model's effort level so it reasons less about testing philosophy.
options.D: Switch the review to a smaller model that has weaker opinions about conventions.

why: `CLAUDE.md` provides project-specific context, testing standards, and review criteria to the CI-invoked Claude, so it stops applying generic norms that conflict with the team's deliberate choices.

explanations.A: `CLAUDE.md` provides project-specific context, testing standards, and review criteria to the CI-invoked Claude, so it stops applying generic norms that conflict with the team's deliberate choices.
explanations.B: PR descriptions are inconsistent and not guaranteed context; CLAUDE.md is read automatically each run.
explanations.C: Reducing effort degrades review quality broadly and doesn't convey the project's actual standards.
explanations.D: Model choice doesn't communicate the project's standards; providing them as context does.

prompt: Your team maintains a `CLAUDE.md` for local development. You want the CI review bot to apply the same review criteria the team uses interactively. What's the relationship you should rely on?

options.A: The CI-invoked Claude reads the committed `CLAUDE.md`, giving CI and local sessions the same context.
options.B: CI runs ignore `CLAUDE.md` entirely, so review criteria must be passed as a command-line argument each time.
options.C: Only `CLAUDE.local.md` is read in CI, so criteria must be duplicated into that uncommitted file.
options.D: CI reads a separate `CI.md` file that must be created specifically for pipeline runs.

why: Because CLAUDE.md is committed and read at session start, a CI-invoked instance inherits the same project context and review criteria as interactive sessions.

explanations.A: Because CLAUDE.md is committed and read at session start, a CI-invoked instance inherits the same project context and review criteria as interactive sessions.
explanations.B: CI-invoked Claude does read CLAUDE.md; it is not ignored in headless mode.
explanations.C: CLAUDE.local.md is personal and not committed, so it cannot be the shared CI source; the committed CLAUDE.md is.
explanations.D: There is no special CI.md convention; the committed CLAUDE.md provides the context.

prompt: Your workflow generates code with one Claude session, then asks that same session to review the code it just wrote. Reviewers notice it rarely flags its own design flaws. What's the most effective architectural change?

options.A: Have an independent Claude instance review the code, separate from the session that generated it.
options.B: Increase the generating session's effort level so it reasons harder when reviewing its own code.
options.C: Ask the generating session to re-read its output twice before reporting findings.
options.D: Instruct the session to adopt a skeptical persona while reviewing its own output.

why: The generating session retains its own reasoning and is biased toward defending its decisions; an independent instance reviews with fresh context and catches more issues.

explanations.A: The generating session retains its own reasoning and is biased toward defending its decisions; an independent instance reviews with fresh context and catches more issues.
explanations.B: Extra reasoning in the same session doesn't remove the bias of having authored the code.
explanations.C: Re-reading within the same session still carries the original reasoning and self-justification.
explanations.D: A persona prompt doesn't overcome the structural bias of self-review; an independent instance does.

prompt: A teammate argues that running review in the same session that wrote the code is fine because "it already understands the context best." Why is this reasoning flawed for catching defects?

options.A: It retains the reasoning that produced the code, so it rarely questions its own decisions.
options.B: A same-session review always exceeds the context window and silently truncates the code.
options.C: The session caches its tool results, which prevents it from re-reading the files at all.
options.D: Reviewing in the same session forces a lower temperature that suppresses findings.

why: Retained generation reasoning biases the model toward its earlier choices, reducing its likelihood of questioning them — the core self-review limitation.

explanations.A: Retained generation reasoning biases the model toward its earlier choices, reducing its likelihood of questioning them — the core self-review limitation.
explanations.B: Context exhaustion is a separate concern and not the reason self-review underperforms.
explanations.C: Tool-result caching is not the mechanism; the issue is reasoning bias toward its own decisions.
explanations.D: Self-review doesn't change temperature; the limitation is the retained reasoning bias.

prompt: After a PR's first review, the author pushes new commits to address feedback and the pipeline re-runs the review. Developers complain the bot re-reports the same issues they already fixed or acknowledged. What's the best fix?

options.A: Pass the prior review findings into the re-run so Claude reports only new or still-unaddressed issues.
options.B: Only review the files touched by the newest commit and ignore the rest of the PR.
options.C: Suppress all comments on a re-run and require a human to re-trigger review manually.
options.D: Lower the severity threshold on every re-run so that far fewer findings appear in the comment overall.

why: Including prior findings gives the re-run a baseline, so it focuses on what changed and stops repeating issues that were resolved or acknowledged.

explanations.A: Including prior findings gives the re-run a baseline, so it focuses on what changed and stops repeating issues that were resolved or acknowledged.
explanations.B: Limiting to the newest commit can miss interactions and still re-flags issues without knowing what was already addressed.
explanations.C: Silencing the re-run loses value; supplying prior findings is the targeted solution.
explanations.D: A blunt threshold change can hide genuine new issues and doesn't address the duplication.

prompt: Your test-generation step in CI keeps producing tests that duplicate scenarios already covered in the existing suite, bloating the suite. What's the most effective way to reduce duplicate generation?

options.A: Provide the existing test files as context so generation avoids scenarios that are already covered.
options.B: Cap the number of generated tests per file so duplicates are statistically less likely.
options.C: Run the suite first and feed only the coverage percentage into the prompt.
options.D: Delete and regenerate the entire existing test suite from scratch on every CI run for consistency.

why: Giving Claude the existing tests lets it see what's already covered and target gaps instead of re-creating duplicate scenarios.

explanations.A: Giving Claude the existing tests lets it see what's already covered and target gaps instead of re-creating duplicate scenarios.
explanations.B: A cap limits volume but doesn't tell Claude what's already covered, so duplicates persist.
explanations.C: A bare coverage number doesn't identify which specific scenarios exist; the actual test files do.
explanations.D: Regenerating everything is destructive and wasteful; supplying existing tests as context is targeted.

prompt: You build a CI step that launches a second Claude instance to review changes made by the first, modeled on the query-duplication hook pattern. Which SDK/tooling fact about this design is accurate?

options.A: An independent reviewing instance gives unbiased feedback but adds API usage and time per run.
options.B: A second instance shares the first instance's context window, so it needs no extra tokens.
options.C: The reviewing instance can edit the first instance's files automatically without permissions.
options.D: Launching a second instance is free because reviews don't count against rate limits.

why: Spawning a separate instance for review yields fresh-eyes feedback (no generation bias) at the cost of additional API usage and runtime — the documented trade-off.

explanations.A: Spawning a separate instance for review yields fresh-eyes feedback (no generation bias) at the cost of additional API usage and runtime — the documented trade-off.
explanations.B: Separate instances have separate context windows and consume their own tokens.
explanations.C: A reviewer should be read-only; it doesn't silently edit, and tools must be permitted explicitly.
explanations.D: Every instance consumes API usage and counts toward limits; reviews are not exempt.

prompt: When configuring the review subagent that runs in CI, which tool restriction best matches its role and reduces risk?

options.A: Restrict the reviewer to read-only tools so it flags issues rather than editing files.
options.B: Grant the reviewer full write access so that it can fix any issues it finds inline as it reviews.
options.C: Grant only Bash so it can run formatters and commit the corrected files.
options.D: Disable all tools so the reviewer works purely from the diff text in the prompt.

why: A code reviewer should surface issues, not modify code; restricting it to read-only tools matches its role and prevents unintended edits.

explanations.A: A code reviewer should surface issues, not modify code; restricting it to read-only tools matches its role and prevents unintended edits.
explanations.B: A reviewer that edits code blurs review and authorship and reintroduces self-review bias on its own fixes.
explanations.C: Committing from the reviewer mixes responsibilities and risks unreviewed changes; read-only is appropriate.
explanations.D: Read-only tools let the reviewer inspect related files for context; disabling all tools is overly limiting.

prompt: You're adding a new CI capability: a large refactor of how reviews are orchestrated across many files, touching several workflow scripts and the review prompt templates. Before writing code, which approach fits best?

options.A: Use Plan Mode first so Claude explores the scripts and proposes a plan.
options.B: Skip planning and prompt Claude to make all edits directly to move faster.
options.C: Raise the effort level to max and prompt for the edits without exploring files.
options.D: Run the change as a batch job overnight so it doesn't block your workflow.

why: Plan Mode suits multi-file, multi-step changes requiring broad codebase understanding; it lets you review and redirect before any code is written.

explanations.A: Plan Mode suits multi-file, multi-step changes requiring broad codebase understanding; it lets you review and redirect before any code is written.
explanations.B: Direct edits on a broad refactor lead to course-correction later; planning is best for multi-file changes.
explanations.C: Effort helps with hard logic, not with breadth across many files; Plan Mode addresses the breadth need.
explanations.D: Batching is for cost/latency on independent requests, not for a single interactive multi-file refactor.

prompt: A reviewer asks you to fix a single off-by-one in one well-understood line of a CI script. Which approach is most appropriate?

options.A: Prompt Claude to make the one-line edit directly, since it is small and understood.
options.B: Enter Plan Mode so Claude can explore the whole repository before touching the line.
options.C: Spawn an independent review instance before making any edit to the line.
options.D: Submit the fix as part of an overnight Batches API job to save cost.

why: Direct editing fits a small, well-scoped change; Plan Mode's exploration overhead is unnecessary for a single known line.

explanations.A: Direct editing fits a small, well-scoped change; Plan Mode's exploration overhead is unnecessary for a single known line.
explanations.B: Full exploration is overkill for a trivial, well-understood one-line fix.
explanations.C: An independent review is for catching defects in non-trivial work, not for a single known one-line fix.
explanations.D: Batching targets latency-tolerant bulk work, not a quick interactive one-line edit.

prompt: Your team disagrees about when to use Plan Mode versus a higher effort level while building review tooling. Which framing is correct?

options.A: Plan Mode handles breadth across files; higher effort handles depth on logic.
options.B: Plan Mode is simply a slower but more accurate version of raising the effort level on a task.
options.C: Higher effort is for writing code and Plan Mode is only for debugging.
options.D: Plan Mode disables tools entirely, while higher effort enables every tool.

why: Plan Mode is for multi-file breadth and exploration; effort level is for depth on complex logic — distinct axes that can be combined when a task needs both.

explanations.A: Plan Mode is for multi-file breadth and exploration; effort level is for depth on complex logic — distinct axes that can be combined when a task needs both.
explanations.B: They address different complexity types (breadth vs depth), not speed-vs-accuracy of the same thing.
explanations.C: This mislabels both: Plan Mode is for exploration/multi-step work, effort is for hard reasoning.
explanations.D: Plan Mode uses read-only tools to plan; effort level does not toggle tool availability.

prompt: Every PR in your repo should run the same review steps the same way. You want to package those steps so any engineer (and the CI workflow) invokes them consistently. What's the most appropriate Claude Code mechanism?

options.A: Create a custom slash command in `.claude/commands/` that encodes the steps.
options.B: Tell each engineer to paste the same long review prompt manually every single time they review.
options.C: Add the steps to `CLAUDE.local.md` so they're available in every session.
options.D: Register the steps as an MCP server so they load as tools on startup.

why: Custom commands are markdown files in .claude/commands/ that capture repeatable, project-specific workflows with consistent steps — ideal for a standardized review routine.

explanations.A: Custom commands are markdown files in .claude/commands/ that capture repeatable, project-specific workflows with consistent steps — ideal for a standardized review routine.
explanations.B: Manual pasting is error-prone and inconsistent; a custom command enforces the same steps.
explanations.C: CLAUDE.local.md is personal and uncommitted; it neither shares with the team nor packages an invokable command.
explanations.D: An MCP server exposes external tools, not a packaged prompt workflow; a custom command fits better and avoids context cost.
