prompt: You want one review command to accept the path of the file or module under review so it can be reused across PRs. How do you make a custom command accept that input?

options.A: Include the `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder in the command's markdown file.
options.B: Define an `@parameters` decorator at the top of the command file.
options.C: Declare the parameters in `settings.json` under a command schema block.
options.D: Pass the path as an environment variable the command reads at startup.

why: `$ARGUMENTS` lets a custom command accept runtime input (such as a file path), making one command reusable across different targets.

explanations.A: `$ARGUMENTS` lets a custom command accept runtime input (such as a file path), making one command reusable across different targets.
explanations.B: There is no @parameters decorator for custom commands; arguments use the $ARGUMENTS placeholder.
explanations.C: Command arguments are not declared in settings.json; the $ARGUMENTS placeholder handles them.
explanations.D: Custom commands take arguments via $ARGUMENTS, not via ad hoc environment variables.

prompt: After committing a new `/review-pr` command file into `.claude/commands/` during an active session, a teammate asks whether they must restart Claude Code to use it. What's correct?

options.A: No restart is needed; Claude Code picks up new command files automatically.
options.B: Yes; commands are only loaded at startup, so the session must be restarted.
options.C: Only after running `/reload-commands` will the new command appear.
options.D: Only after committing and pushing to the remote will the command be usable.

why: Custom commands are detected automatically after the file is created — no session restart required.

explanations.A: Custom commands are detected automatically after the file is created — no session restart required.
explanations.B: Commands are picked up automatically without restarting.
explanations.C: There is no reload-commands step; detection is automatic.
explanations.D: Local file presence is enough; pushing to a remote is unrelated to command discovery.

prompt: The official GitHub integration auto-runs Claude on each pull request. Your team wants Claude to use Playwright tools during PR review in CI. What permissions configuration is required?

options.A: Explicitly list each MCP tool in `allowed_tools`; Actions has no shortcut.
options.B: No special permissions are needed because Actions inherits repository settings.
options.C: A single blanket permission covering all MCP operations is sufficient.
options.D: Permissions are pulled automatically from `.claude/settings.local.json` in CI.

why: In GitHub Actions there is no interactive permission shortcut; every tool from each MCP server must be individually listed in allowed_tools.

explanations.A: In GitHub Actions there is no interactive permission shortcut; every tool from each MCP server must be individually listed in allowed_tools.
explanations.B: Repository settings do not grant tool permissions to Claude; tools must be listed explicitly.
explanations.C: There is no blanket MCP permission in Actions; each tool is enumerated.
explanations.D: The local settings shortcut applies to local development, not GitHub Actions, which requires explicit listing.

prompt: You want the CI-run Claude to have the dev server already running and to know how to query the database during review. In the GitHub Actions workflow, where do you convey that environment context?

options.A: Put it in `custom_instructions`, describing the server, logs, and db access.
options.B: Embed the context as comments inside the pull request diff.
options.C: Add the context to the GitHub repository description field.
options.D: Pass it as a positional prompt argument to the `claude` binary inside the run step of the job.

why: custom_instructions in the workflow conveys project setup context (server already running, log location, db query method) to the CI-invoked Claude.

explanations.A: custom_instructions in the workflow conveys project setup context (server already running, log location, db query method) to the CI-invoked Claude.
explanations.B: Diff comments are not a reliable channel for environment setup context; custom_instructions is the documented field.
explanations.C: The repository description is metadata Claude doesn't consume as setup context.
explanations.D: Environment/setup context belongs in custom_instructions, not as a raw positional prompt argument.

prompt: A type-checking hook is proposed for CI so that when Claude edits a function signature, missed call sites are caught immediately. Which hook type and behavior fits this need?

options.A: A PostToolUse hook running `tsc --noEmit` after edits, feeding errors back.
options.B: A PreToolUse hook that blocks the edit until types are manually verified.
options.C: A Stop hook that runs the compiler only once Claude finishes the entire session.
options.D: A Notification hook that emails the type errors to the developer.

why: A PostToolUse hook fires after file edits, runs the type checker, and feeds errors back so Claude fixes affected call sites — exactly the documented pattern.

explanations.A: A PostToolUse hook fires after file edits, runs the type checker, and feeds errors back so Claude fixes affected call sites — exactly the documented pattern.
explanations.B: PreToolUse runs before the edit, so it cannot type-check the result; PostToolUse is correct.
explanations.C: Waiting until Stop delays feedback; PostToolUse gives immediate per-edit checking.
explanations.D: Notification hooks fire on notifications/idle, not after edits, and emailing doesn't feed errors back to Claude.

prompt: Your CI must guarantee that commits to the `main` branch are blocked during automated runs, not merely discouraged via a prompt instruction. What's the most reliable mechanism?

options.A: A PreToolUse hook that blocks the command and returns feedback via exit code 2.
options.B: An instruction in `CLAUDE.md` telling Claude that it must never commit directly to the `main` branch.
options.C: A PostToolUse hook that reverts the commit after it happens.
options.D: Lowering the effort level so Claude is less likely to attempt a commit.

why: Hooks are deterministic — they always run. A PreToolUse hook can block the action (exit code 2) and feed the reason back, unlike a prompt instruction that may be ignored.

explanations.A: Hooks are deterministic — they always run. A PreToolUse hook can block the action (exit code 2) and feed the reason back, unlike a prompt instruction that may be ignored.
explanations.B: CLAUDE.md guidance is usually followed but not guaranteed; a hard guarantee requires a hook.
explanations.C: PostToolUse cannot block the action; the commit would already have occurred.
explanations.D: Effort level has no bearing on enforcing a hard branch-protection rule.

prompt: When the PreToolUse hook blocks a forbidden command in CI, how should the hook communicate the reason so Claude can adjust its next action?

options.A: Exit with code 2 and write the reason to stderr, which is fed back to Claude.
options.B: Exit with code 0 and write the reason to standard output for Claude to read and act upon.
options.C: Exit with code 1 and write the reason to a log file Claude polls.
options.D: Throw an uncaught exception so the runner aborts the whole job.

why: Exit code 2 blocks the tool call, and the stderr message is delivered to Claude as feedback explaining why, so it can adapt.

explanations.A: Exit code 2 blocks the tool call, and the stderr message is delivered to Claude as feedback explaining why, so it can adapt.
explanations.B: Exit code 0 allows the call to proceed, so it would not block anything.
explanations.C: Other non-zero codes surface a non-blocking error to the user and don't block the call; code 2 is the blocking signal.
explanations.D: Aborting the job is heavy-handed and loses the targeted feedback path of exit code 2 plus stderr.

prompt: You share review hooks across the team and store the hook script inside the repo. Which practice ensures the hook command resolves regardless of Claude's working directory in CI?

options.A: Reference the script via the `CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` environment variable in the command.
options.B: Hardcode an absolute path to one developer's checkout location.
options.C: Rely on a relative path and require everyone on the team to always run Claude from the repository root.
options.D: Copy the script into `~/.claude/` on every machine before each run.

why: CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR lets the hook command reference repo-stored scripts so they work no matter what the current working directory is.

explanations.A: CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR lets the hook command reference repo-stored scripts so they work no matter what the current working directory is.
explanations.B: A hardcoded personal path breaks for teammates and CI; use CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR.
explanations.C: Relative paths are fragile across directories; the project-dir variable is the documented solution.
explanations.D: Per-machine copying is unmaintainable; referencing it via CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR keeps it in-repo and portable.

prompt: Hooks are configured at three levels. For review hooks that the whole team should get automatically when they clone the repo, which settings file should hold them?

options.A: `.claude/settings.json`, the project-level file checked into the repo.
options.B: `~/.claude/settings.json`, the global file on each developer's machine.
options.C: `.claude/settings.local.json`, the personal uncommitted file.
options.D: A `hooks.json` file placed in the repository root.

why: Project-level .claude/settings.json is committed and shared, so the whole team inherits the same hooks on clone.

explanations.A: Project-level .claude/settings.json is committed and shared, so the whole team inherits the same hooks on clone.
explanations.B: The global file affects only that machine and isn't shared via the repo.
explanations.C: settings.local.json is personal and not committed, so teammates wouldn't receive it.
explanations.D: Hooks live in Claude settings files under .claude/, not a root hooks.json.

prompt: Your reviewers want Claude's CI findings to reference exact files and line numbers so comments anchor correctly on the PR. What's the most effective way to ensure this in the headless run?

options.A: Define a schema requiring `file` and `line` fields and emit JSON.
options.B: Ask Claude in prose to "mention line numbers when relevant" and parse them out later.
options.C: Increase the context window so Claude can see every line of the diff.
options.D: Run the review twice and only keep findings whose text mentions a number.

why: A required-field schema plus JSON output forces each finding to carry file and line, so the formatter can anchor PR comments precisely.

explanations.A: A required-field schema plus JSON output forces each finding to carry file and line, so the formatter can anchor PR comments precisely.
explanations.B: Prose instructions don't guarantee the fields exist or are machine-extractable; a schema does.
explanations.C: Window size doesn't force structured location fields into the output.
explanations.D: Text-matching numbers is unreliable; a schema requiring file/line fields is the correct enforcement.

prompt: Your nightly job uses the Agent SDK to programmatically review many directories. You want each reviewer run constrained to inspection only. Which SDK option matches the local `--allowedTools` behavior?

options.A: Pass `allowedTools` in the query options, e.g. limited to `Read` and `Glob`.
options.B: Set `readOnly: true` in the query options to disable all writes.
options.C: Call `query()` without any options at all, since the SDK already defaults to a read-only tool set.
options.D: Import `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` and toggle its review mode.

why: The SDK's allowedTools option narrows the tool set (e.g., Read and Glob) and is the SDK equivalent of the CLI's --allowedTools flag — appropriate for a read-only reviewer.

explanations.A: The SDK's allowedTools option narrows the tool set (e.g., Read and Glob) and is the SDK equivalent of the CLI's --allowedTools flag — appropriate for a read-only reviewer.
explanations.B: There is no readOnly flag; you restrict capability by listing allowedTools.
explanations.C: By default the SDK has the full tool set; you must pass allowedTools to narrow it.
explanations.D: @anthropic-ai/claude-code is the CLI and can't be imported; the SDK package is @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk.

prompt: Before merging, the team wants Claude to act as a fresh-eyes reviewer that doesn't carry the bias of the session that wrote the feature. Which built-in Claude Code feature is designed for this?

options.A: A code-reviewer subagent, which runs in its own isolated context window.
options.B: The `/compact` command, which summarizes the entire current session right before the review step.
options.C: Raising the effort level so the main agent reviews more thoroughly.
options.D: The `/rewind` command, which removes distracting history before review.

why: A subagent reviews with a separate context window and fresh eyes, avoiding the main session's authorship bias — the documented pre-push review approach.

explanations.A: A subagent reviews with a separate context window and fresh eyes, avoiding the main session's authorship bias — the documented pre-push review approach.
explanations.B: Compacting condenses the same session's history; it doesn't remove authorship bias the way a separate context does.
explanations.C: More effort in the same session retains the original reasoning bias.
explanations.D: Rewinding edits the same session's history; it isn't a separate-context reviewer.

prompt: A teammate added the review prompt to `CLAUDE.md` but it's now enormous, listing dozens of niche rules, and CI reviews have gotten less focused. What's the best corrective principle?

options.A: Keep `CLAUDE.md` compact; too much irrelevant context hurts performance.
options.B: Move the rules into more files and @-import all of them on the first line.
options.C: Raise the effort level to compensate for the larger instruction set.
options.D: Duplicate the rules into each PR description so they reinforce CLAUDE.md.

why: Because CLAUDE.md is appended to every request, bloated irrelevant context dilutes focus and degrades performance; keep it lean and relevant.

explanations.A: Because CLAUDE.md is appended to every request, bloated irrelevant context dilutes focus and degrades performance; keep it lean and relevant.
explanations.B: Importing the same bloat just relocates it; the issue is excessive irrelevant context, not file count.
explanations.C: Effort doesn't counteract context dilution from an overloaded CLAUDE.md.
explanations.D: Duplication adds more noise; trimming CLAUDE.md to the essentials is the fix.

prompt: Your CI wraps `claude -p` and parses `--output-format json`, but occasionally a finding's free-text `message` field contains characters that break your naive string parsing. What's the root-cause fix?

options.A: Parse the emitted result with a real JSON parser instead of string matching on stdout.
options.B: Add a prompt rule that bans all special characters from appearing in the `message` field of findings.
options.C: Switch back to plain-text output and parse with regexes instead.
options.D: Strip all punctuation from the output before parsing it.

why: JSON output is meant to be consumed by a JSON parser, which correctly handles escaping; ad hoc string matching is the actual defect.

explanations.A: JSON output is meant to be consumed by a JSON parser, which correctly handles escaping; ad hoc string matching is the actual defect.
explanations.B: Constraining message content is fragile and unnecessary when a JSON parser handles escaping correctly.
explanations.C: Plain text is even harder to parse reliably; the JSON output with a real parser is the goal.
explanations.D: Stripping punctuation corrupts findings; using a proper JSON parser is the correct approach.

prompt: Your review prompt says "check that code comments are accurate," and the bot flags comments for tone, wording, and outdated phrasing — mostly noise. You want it to flag only genuinely misleading comments. Which instruction is most effective?

options.A: "Flag a comment only when its claimed behavior contradicts what the code actually does."
options.B: "Check that all of the code comments are accurate and well written throughout the file."
options.C: "Review comments carefully and report anything that seems off to you."
options.D: "Be conservative when commenting on code comments in the diff."

why: A precise, testable criterion (claimed behavior contradicting actual code) targets real defects and excludes tone/wording noise, unlike the vague "check accuracy."

explanations.A: A precise, testable criterion (claimed behavior contradicting actual code) targets real defects and excludes tone/wording noise, unlike the vague "check accuracy."
explanations.B: This is still vague and invites tone/style nitpicking — the original problem.
explanations.C: "Seems off" is subjective and produces noisy, low-value flags.
explanations.D: General "be conservative" guidance fails to define what counts; specific categorical criteria work better.

prompt: Two prompt variants are proposed to cut false positives. Variant 1: "only flag high-confidence issues." Variant 2: a list of explicit categories to report (e.g., null dereference, injection) and categories to skip (e.g., formatting). Which is likely to perform better and why?

options.A: Variant 2, because explicit categorical criteria outperform vague confidence-based instructions.
options.B: Variant 1, because asking for high confidence directly tells the model to suppress weak findings.
options.C: Neither differs in practice; both phrasings yield the same false-positive rate.
options.D: Variant 1, since shorter prompts always reduce the chance of over-flagging.

why: Specific, categorical definitions of what to report and skip improve precision more reliably than general "high-confidence" or "be conservative" phrasing.

explanations.A: Specific, categorical definitions of what to report and skip improve precision more reliably than general "high-confidence" or "be conservative" phrasing.
explanations.B: General confidence instructions don't reliably improve precision; categories do.
explanations.C: They differ meaningfully; explicit criteria measurably reduce noise versus vague instructions.
explanations.D: Brevity isn't the determinant; specificity of criteria is.

prompt: One review category — "possible performance concerns" — accounts for most false positives and is destroying developer trust, while security and correctness categories are accurate. You need a fix this sprint. What's the most pragmatic step?

options.A: Temporarily disable the high-false-positive performance category while keeping the accurate ones.
options.B: Disable the entire review bot until every category is perfected.
options.C: Keep every category enabled but append "please be especially careful about performance claims" to the prompt.
options.D: Raise the model temperature so performance findings vary run to run.

why: Disabling the specific noisy category preserves trust and the value of accurate categories while you refine criteria — a sound short-term move.

explanations.A: Disabling the specific noisy category preserves trust and the value of accurate categories while you refine criteria — a sound short-term move.
explanations.B: Turning everything off discards the accurate, high-value categories unnecessarily.
explanations.C: Vague softening rarely cuts a category's false-positive rate meaningfully.
explanations.D: Adding randomness doesn't reduce the category's noise and harms reproducibility.

prompt: Developers disagree about what counts as a "high" severity finding, so the bot's labels feel arbitrary. What prompt change most improves consistency of severity labeling?

options.A: Define explicit severity criteria with concrete code examples for each level.
options.B: Tell the model to "use your best judgment" when assigning severity.
options.C: Ask the model to assign a numeric score from 1 to 10 without ever defining what the scale means.
options.D: Have the model copy whatever severity the previous reviewer assigned.

why: Concrete, example-anchored severity definitions give the model unambiguous boundaries, producing consistent labels rather than arbitrary ones.

explanations.A: Concrete, example-anchored severity definitions give the model unambiguous boundaries, producing consistent labels rather than arbitrary ones.
explanations.B: Best-judgment phrasing is exactly the ambiguity causing inconsistency.
explanations.C: An undefined numeric scale is just as arbitrary as undefined words.
explanations.D: Copying prior labels propagates inconsistency rather than defining clear criteria.

prompt: A post-incident review finds the bot's high false-positive rate caused developers to start ignoring all its comments, including a real SQL injection it had flagged. What's the key lesson for prompt design?

options.A: High-false-positive categories undermine trust, so accurate findings get ignored alongside the noise.
options.B: False positives are harmless as long as the true positives are also present.
options.C: The fix is to post even more findings so the real ones are statistically included.
options.D: Severity labels matter far more than precision, so just refine the labels and keep posting all findings.

why: When noise erodes trust, developers tune out everything — even correct, severe findings — so suppressing low-value categories protects the high-value ones.

explanations.A: When noise erodes trust, developers tune out everything — even correct, severe findings — so suppressing low-value categories protects the high-value ones.
explanations.B: False positives are not harmless; they cause real findings to be ignored.
explanations.C: More volume worsens trust erosion rather than helping.
explanations.D: Labeling doesn't fix the trust problem caused by noisy categories; precision does.

prompt: Your prompt currently reads: "Carefully review the diff and point out any problems you notice." Findings are wildly inconsistent across PRs. What's the single most impactful improvement?

options.A: Replace the vague instruction with explicit criteria listing what to report and what to ignore.
options.B: Add the word "thoroughly" so the model reviews more aggressively.
options.C: Tell the model to strictly limit itself to no more than exactly five findings on each pull request.
options.D: Ask the model to rephrase its findings more politely for developers.

why: Explicit criteria replace subjective "problems you notice" with defined report/skip categories, the most impactful lever for consistency.

explanations.A: Explicit criteria replace subjective "problems you notice" with defined report/skip categories, the most impactful lever for consistency.
explanations.B: Intensifiers don't define scope; they can increase noise.
explanations.C: An arbitrary cap can drop real issues and doesn't address vague criteria.
explanations.D: Tone changes nothing about which issues are surfaced or skipped.

prompt: You want the review bot to skip stylistic patterns that are intentional in your codebase (such as snapshot tests and a custom result type). How should you express this in the prompt for best precision?

options.A: Name the accepted patterns explicitly and instruct the model to treat them as out of scope.
options.B: Add "please avoid nitpicking style" and hope the model correctly infers all of your team's conventions.
options.C: Reduce max tokens so the model has no room to mention style issues.
options.D: Ask the model to rank style findings last so they're easy to ignore.

why: Explicitly enumerating accepted patterns as out-of-scope is far more precise than a general plea to avoid style nits.

explanations.A: Explicitly enumerating accepted patterns as out-of-scope is far more precise than a general plea to avoid style nits.
explanations.B: Vague "avoid nitpicking" leaves your specific conventions undefined, so violations persist.
explanations.C: Truncating output can drop important findings and doesn't target style specifically.
explanations.D: Ranking still posts the noise; naming patterns as out-of-scope removes it.

prompt: A teammate proposes that instead of an independent reviewer, you just enable extended thinking on the generating model so it scrutinizes its own output harder. Why is an independent review instance still preferable for catching defects?

options.A: An independent instance lacks the generation reasoning, so it questions the author's decisions.
options.B: Extended thinking disables all tool use, so the generating model cannot inspect the code under review at all.
options.C: An independent instance is always cheaper than enabling extended thinking.
options.D: Extended thinking only works on the first message of a session.

why: Independent review instances catch more than self-review or extended thinking because they don't carry the generation reasoning that makes a model defend its own choices.

explanations.A: Independent review instances catch more than self-review or extended thinking because they don't carry the generation reasoning that makes a model defend its own choices.
explanations.B: Extended thinking doesn't disable tools; the real issue is retained authorship bias.
explanations.C: A separate instance costs additional tokens; the advantage is bias removal, not cost.
explanations.D: This is false and not the reason; the point is the self-review limitation.

prompt: Your review for a large PR runs one local pass per file but misses a bug where one file's changed return type breaks a caller in another file. What additional pass closes this gap?

options.A: Add a cross-file integration pass that examines how the changed files interact together.
options.B: Re-run the per-file passes a second time to double-check each file.
options.C: Increase the max tokens on each individual per-file pass so that much more detail can fit in the output.
options.D: Lower the temperature on the per-file passes for steadier output.

why: Per-file passes catch local issues; a dedicated cross-file integration pass catches interactions like a changed return type breaking a caller elsewhere.

explanations.A: Per-file passes catch local issues; a dedicated cross-file integration pass catches interactions like a changed return type breaking a caller elsewhere.
explanations.B: Repeating local passes still never examines cross-file interaction.
explanations.C: More tokens per file doesn't surface relationships between files.
explanations.D: Temperature doesn't address the missing cross-file analysis.

prompt: Why does splitting a many-file review into per-file passes plus one integration pass tend to outperform a single pass over all files at once?

options.A: A single combined pass dilutes attention across files; focused passes keep depth consistent.
options.B: Per-file passes are cheaper, which is the only reason to prefer them.
options.C: A single combined pass simply cannot read more than one file at a time, no matter the context size.
options.D: Per-file passes guarantee zero false positives by construction.

why: Processing many files together dilutes attention, causing uneven depth and missed bugs; focused passes maintain consistent scrutiny, with integration handled separately.

explanations.A: Processing many files together dilutes attention, causing uneven depth and missed bugs; focused passes maintain consistent scrutiny, with integration handled separately.
explanations.B: The primary driver is attention/quality, not cost — and multiple passes can cost more.
explanations.C: A single pass can read many files; the problem is diluted attention, not an inability to read.
explanations.D: They improve focus but don't guarantee zero false positives.

prompt: You want to route only uncertain findings to a slower, more expensive verification step, while auto-posting confident ones. What design supports this calibrated routing?

options.A: Have the review report a confidence per finding and route low-confidence ones to verification.
options.B: Route every single finding through the slower verification step regardless of its reported confidence level.
options.C: Skip verification entirely and trust the first pass for all findings.
options.D: Route findings by their alphabetical category name to the verifier.

why: Verification passes with self-reported confidence enable calibrated routing: confident findings post directly while uncertain ones get a second-pass check.

explanations.A: Verification passes with self-reported confidence enable calibrated routing: confident findings post directly while uncertain ones get a second-pass check.
explanations.B: Verifying everything wastes the cost savings that calibrated routing provides.
explanations.C: No verification means uncertain findings go unfiltered, raising false positives.
explanations.D: Alphabetical routing is arbitrary and unrelated to a finding's uncertainty.

prompt: An engineer claims that running three identical full-PR review passes and majority-voting is the best way to raise reliability on a 20-file PR. What's the strongest critique?

options.A: Each full-PR pass still suffers attention dilution, so voting averages flawed passes.
options.B: Voting is completely invalid because the model is deterministic and so all three passes are identical.
options.C: Three passes always exceed rate limits, making the approach impossible.
options.D: Majority voting only works with an even number of passes.

why: Voting across full-PR passes doesn't fix the underlying dilution; per-file plus integration passes address the root cause instead.

explanations.A: Voting across full-PR passes doesn't fix the underlying dilution; per-file plus integration passes address the root cause instead.
explanations.B: The passes can vary, but the real problem is dilution per pass, not determinism.
explanations.C: Rate limits aren't the conceptual flaw; diluted per-pass attention is.
explanations.D: Voting works with odd counts; the substantive issue is dilution, not the count's parity.

prompt: Your hook-based design has Claude generate a database query and then asks a second Claude instance to check it against existing queries for duplication. Which statement about why this works is correct?

options.A: The second instance reviews without the first's reasoning, so it spots reuse the author missed.
options.B: The second instance fully inherits the first instance's plan, which ensures that it agrees with the original choice.
options.C: Both instances share one context window, so no extra tokens are spent reviewing.
options.D: The first instance is more reliable at reviewing its own query than a second instance.

why: A separate instance has fresh context and no attachment to the generated query, so it more readily identifies an existing function that should have been reused.

explanations.A: A separate instance has fresh context and no attachment to the generated query, so it more readily identifies an existing function that should have been reused.
explanations.B: Inheriting the plan would reproduce the bias; independence is what makes it effective.
explanations.C: They are separate instances with separate context and token usage.
explanations.D: Self-review is weaker due to retained reasoning; the independent instance is preferred.

prompt: For a small two-file PR, your team debates whether the per-file-plus-integration multi-pass design is worth it. What's the most reasonable guidance?

options.A: Multi-pass mainly pays off as file count grows; a small PR can use a single focused pass.
options.B: Always use the complete multi-pass design on every PR regardless of its size, purely for the sake of consistency.
options.C: Never use multi-pass; a single pass is always sufficient at any scale.
options.D: Use multi-pass only when the PR contains zero tests.

why: Attention dilution scales with file count, so the multi-pass overhead is justified for large PRs but unnecessary for a tiny two-file change.

explanations.A: Attention dilution scales with file count, so the multi-pass overhead is justified for large PRs but unnecessary for a tiny two-file change.
explanations.B: For tiny PRs the multi-pass overhead adds cost with little benefit.
explanations.C: Large PRs clearly benefit from multi-pass; "never" is wrong.
explanations.D: The relevant factor is file count and interaction complexity, not the presence of tests.

prompt: A reviewer wants the verification pass to recompute confidence rather than trust the first pass's self-reported value blindly. What's the calibration benefit of a separate verification pass?

options.A: A fresh pass re-assesses the finding independently, improving the confidence used to route it.
options.B: It guarantees the confidence value will always increase after verification.
options.C: It replaces the need to define any severity criteria at all.
options.D: It lets you safely skip all of the per-file passes entirely, since the verification pass already covers everything.

why: An independent verification pass gives a second, unbiased confidence assessment, making routing decisions better calibrated than trusting a single self-report.

explanations.A: An independent verification pass gives a second, unbiased confidence assessment, making routing decisions better calibrated than trusting a single self-report.
explanations.B: Verification can lower confidence; it doesn't always raise it.
explanations.C: Verification complements, not replaces, explicit severity criteria.
explanations.D: Verification refines findings; it doesn't substitute for the focused per-file passes.

prompt: You move the nightly tech-debt scan of 4,000 files to the Message Batches API. After submitting, you need to map each returned result back to the file it analyzed. What's the correct mechanism?

options.A: Set a `custom_id` per request and use it to correlate each result back to its file.
options.B: Rely on the results always being returned in exactly the same order in which you originally submitted them.
options.C: Include the filename in the prompt and grep for it in the response text.
options.D: Submit one batch per file so there is only ever one result to match.

why: The Batches API uses custom_id to correlate each request with its response, which is how you map a result back to its source file.

explanations.A: The Batches API uses custom_id to correlate each request with its response, which is how you map a result back to its source file.
explanations.B: Batch results are not guaranteed to be ordered; custom_id is the correlation key.
explanations.C: Parsing the prompt out of responses is fragile; custom_id is the designed mechanism.
explanations.D: One batch per file defeats the purpose of batching; custom_id correlates many requests in one batch.

prompt: A blocking pre-merge gate currently times out at 90 seconds. Someone suggests using the Batches API for it to cut cost. Why is this inappropriate?

options.A: Batches have no latency SLA and may take up to 24 hours, so they can't satisfy a blocking gate.
options.B: Batches reject prompts longer than 90 seconds of compute time.
options.C: Batches cost more than real-time calls, so they never help with cost.
options.D: Batches simply cannot return JSON output at all, so the blocking gate would have no way to parse the result.

why: The Batches API offers no latency guarantee and can take up to 24 hours, making it unsuitable for a synchronous, blocking pre-merge check.

explanations.A: The Batches API offers no latency guarantee and can take up to 24 hours, making it unsuitable for a synchronous, blocking pre-merge check.
explanations.B: There's no such per-prompt compute limit; the issue is the asynchronous, no-SLA processing model.
explanations.C: Batches actually cost less (about 50% savings); the disqualifier is latency, not cost.
explanations.D: Batches can return structured content; the real reason is the lack of a latency SLA.

prompt: Your reviewer design relies on multi-turn tool calling (read a file, then decide to read another) within a single request. A teammate wants to run it through the Batches API for the cost savings. What's the blocking limitation?

options.A: A batch request does not support multi-turn tool calling, so the interactive loop won't run.
options.B: Batches support tool calling but cap it at one tool per minute.
options.C: Batches require every tool to be defined as an MCP server, which this particular reviewer design completely lacks.
options.D: Batches allow tool calling only when `custom_id` is omitted.

why: The Batches API does not support multi-turn tool calling within a request, so an interactive read-then-decide review loop cannot run as a batch job.

explanations.A: The Batches API does not support multi-turn tool calling within a request, so an interactive read-then-decide review loop cannot run as a batch job.
explanations.B: There's no per-minute tool cap; multi-turn tool calling simply isn't supported in a batch request.
explanations.C: The limitation is the lack of multi-turn tool calling, not an MCP requirement.
explanations.D: custom_id is unrelated to tool calling; multi-turn tool use is the actual constraint.

prompt: Which workload is the best fit for the Message Batches API given its 50% cost savings and up-to-24-hour window?

options.A: An overnight batch summarizing review trends across yesterday's merged PRs for a morning report.
options.B: A blocking pre-commit hook that must return its findings before the developer is allowed to push the change.
options.C: An interactive review that reads files and follows up based on what it finds.
options.D: A live PR comment that must appear within seconds of opening the PR.

why: Overnight, latency-tolerant, independent-request jobs are the ideal batch use case — cost savings with no need for fast turnaround.

explanations.A: Overnight, latency-tolerant, independent-request jobs are the ideal batch use case — cost savings with no need for fast turnaround.
explanations.B: Pre-commit is synchronous and blocking, which batches' no-SLA model cannot serve.
explanations.C: Interactive multi-turn tool use isn't supported in a batch request.
explanations.D: Second-level latency requirements rule out the asynchronous batch model.

prompt: Finance asks for the most accurate one-line reason the nightly review job is half the cost of running the same prompts in real time. What should you say?

options.A: It uses the Message Batches API, which trades latency for roughly 50% lower cost.
options.B: It uses a smaller model, which is what halves the cost.
options.C: It caches every response, so repeated runs are free after the first.
options.D: It skips all of the tool calls, which is where the majority of the per-request cost normally goes.

why: The Batches API provides about 50% cost savings in exchange for relaxed latency — the accurate explanation for the cost difference.

explanations.A: The Batches API provides about 50% cost savings in exchange for relaxed latency — the accurate explanation for the cost difference.
explanations.B: The savings come from batch pricing, not necessarily a model change.
explanations.C: Caching is a different mechanism; the ~50% figure here is batch pricing.
explanations.D: The savings are from batch pricing, not from omitting tool calls.

prompt: Your nightly batch of 10,000 file-review requests returns, but a few hundred results seem mismatched to files in your tracking spreadsheet. The spreadsheet assumed submission order. What's the root cause and fix?

options.A: Order isn't guaranteed; correlate each result by its `custom_id` instead of position.
options.B: The batch silently dropped some requests; resubmit the entire batch again to fix the result ordering.
options.C: The model reordered findings; lower the temperature to keep order stable.
options.D: The spreadsheet is fine; the API must return results in submission order.

why: Because batch results aren't guaranteed to be in submission order, correlation must use custom_id rather than assuming positional alignment.

explanations.A: Because batch results aren't guaranteed to be in submission order, correlation must use custom_id rather than assuming positional alignment.
explanations.B: The mismatch is from relying on order, not dropped requests; custom_id resolves it without resubmitting.
explanations.C: Temperature doesn't control result ordering; custom_id correlation does.
explanations.D: The API does not promise submission order, so the spreadsheet's assumption is the bug.

prompt: Your team wants both nightly cost savings and the ability for the reviewer to iteratively read related files. How should you reconcile these with the Batches API constraints?

options.A: Pre-gather each file's context up front so each batch request is a single self-contained turn.
options.B: Keep the interactive multi-turn loop and submit it as a batch anyway.
options.C: Run the interactive loop in real time but bill it at the batch rate.
options.D: Split each file into two separate batches so that the second batch can react to the output of the first.

why: Since batches don't support multi-turn tool calling, you make each request self-contained by assembling the needed context beforehand, preserving batch eligibility and savings.

explanations.A: Since batches don't support multi-turn tool calling, you make each request self-contained by assembling the needed context beforehand, preserving batch eligibility and savings.
explanations.B: Multi-turn tool calling isn't supported in a batch request, so this won't run.
explanations.C: Real-time calls don't get batch pricing; you can't mix the two that way.
explanations.D: Batches process independently with no cross-request reactivity; pre-gathering context is the correct approach.

prompt: A developer expects batch results within a guaranteed five minutes so they can chain a follow-up job. Why is hard-coding a five-minute wait risky?

options.A: Batches provide no latency SLA and may take up to 24 hours, so a fixed wait can fail.
options.B: Batches always complete in exactly five minutes, so hard-coding the five-minute wait is perfectly fine here.
options.C: Batches return instantly, so any wait at all is wasted time.
options.D: Batches only complete on the hour, so five minutes never aligns.

why: Without a latency guarantee (up to 24 hours), assuming a fixed five-minute completion will intermittently break the chained job.

explanations.A: Without a latency guarantee (up to 24 hours), assuming a fixed five-minute completion will intermittently break the chained job.
explanations.B: There is no fixed completion time; the window can extend to 24 hours.
explanations.C: Batches are asynchronous, not instant; the risk is the opposite of instant.
explanations.D: There's no hourly schedule; the issue is the absence of any latency SLA.

prompt: Your review prompt describes what a good finding looks like in prose, but the bot keeps formatting findings inconsistently and mislabeling borderline cases. Which technique most reliably teaches it the desired shape?

options.A: Add a few example input diffs paired with ideal findings, wrapped in XML tags.
options.B: Rewrite the prose description of a good finding to be considerably longer and far more detailed than before.
options.C: Raise the temperature so the model explores formatting variations.
options.D: Ask the model to invent its own examples before reviewing.

why: Multi-shot examples of input/ideal-output pairs (clearly delimited with XML tags) show rather than tell, producing more consistent formatting and labeling than prose alone.

explanations.A: Multi-shot examples of input/ideal-output pairs (clearly delimited with XML tags) show rather than tell, producing more consistent formatting and labeling than prose alone.
explanations.B: More prose still tells rather than shows; concrete examples are more effective for subtle requirements.
explanations.C: Higher temperature increases variability, worsening consistency.
explanations.D: Self-generated examples don't anchor to your desired output; curated examples do.

prompt: The bot handles common bugs well but mislabels tricky edge cases — e.g., a defensive null check that looks redundant but is intentional. You have a few real cases like this. How should you use them?

options.A: Add multi-shot examples covering these edge cases with their correct ideal outputs.
options.B: Add a single generic example and assume it generalizes to all edge cases.
options.C: Remove all examples so the model reasons from first principles.
options.D: Describe each of the tricky edge cases only in words, without providing any concrete worked examples at all.

why: Multi-shot prompting is especially useful for handling tricky edge cases; showing examples of those exact corner cases with ideal labels teaches the desired handling.

explanations.A: Multi-shot prompting is especially useful for handling tricky edge cases; showing examples of those exact corner cases with ideal labels teaches the desired handling.
explanations.B: One generic example may not cover diverse corner cases; multi-shot across them is better.
explanations.C: Removing examples loses the strongest tool for steering edge-case behavior.
explanations.D: Words alone are exactly what's failing; demonstrating the cases is more reliable.

prompt: After running an evaluation suite on your review prompt, you want to pick which sample findings to embed as few-shot examples. Which selection strategy is recommended?

options.A: Use the highest-scoring outputs from the evaluation as the example input/output pairs.
options.B: Use the lowest-scoring outputs so the model learns what to avoid.
options.C: Pick examples at random to avoid biasing the model.
options.D: Use the longest outputs from the evaluation run, since longer outputs always contain the most detail.

why: Selecting your top-scoring evaluation outputs as examples shows Claude what ideal output looks like for your specific task.

explanations.A: Selecting your top-scoring evaluation outputs as examples shows Claude what ideal output looks like for your specific task.
explanations.B: Examples demonstrate the target; showing poor outputs as examples risks reinforcing them.
explanations.C: Random examples may include mediocre outputs; high-scoring ones are the recommended choice.
explanations.D: Length isn't the criterion; quality (score) is what makes a good exemplar.

prompt: You add example findings to the review prompt but the model still confuses where your examples end and the diff under review begins. What's the most effective structural fix?

options.A: Wrap each example and the target diff in distinct XML tags to delimit them clearly.
options.B: Put the examples and the diff in one block separated by blank lines.
options.C: Reduce to a single example so there's nothing to confuse.
options.D: Move all of the examples into `CLAUDE.md` and remove them from the review prompt entirely to avoid the confusion.

why: XML tags create clear delimiters so the model distinguishes example input/output from the actual content to review, reducing parsing confusion.

explanations.A: XML tags create clear delimiters so the model distinguishes example input/output from the actual content to review, reducing parsing confusion.
explanations.B: Blank lines are weak delimiters; XML tags make the structure unambiguous.
explanations.C: Dropping examples weakens guidance; clear delimiting solves the confusion without losing them.
explanations.D: Relocating doesn't address delimiting; the diff and examples still need clear boundaries via tags.

prompt: Your team adds review examples but writes them as bare pairs with no commentary. A reviewer suggests explaining each example. What's the benefit of annotating why an example output is ideal?

options.A: Explaining why an output is ideal helps the model grasp subtle requirements.
options.B: Annotations like this are only ever useful for one-shot prompts and provide no benefit in multi-shot prompts.
options.C: Annotations replace the need for XML tags around examples.
options.D: Annotations let you skip the example outputs entirely.

why: Annotating examples with why they're ideal conveys subtle intent the raw pair alone may not, improving how the model generalizes.

explanations.A: Annotating examples with why they're ideal conveys subtle intent the raw pair alone may not, improving how the model generalizes.
explanations.B: Explaining ideal outputs helps regardless of the number of examples.
explanations.C: Annotations and delimiting are complementary; tags are still recommended for structure.
explanations.D: The input/output pair is the core of the example; annotation supplements, not replaces it.

prompt: For a sentiment-style classification step that tags review comments as actionable or informational, the model handles obvious cases but misreads sarcastic or hedged wording. Which approach best addresses this?

options.A: Provide examples including sarcastic and hedged phrasings mapped to the correct label.
options.B: Instruct the model to "watch out for sarcasm" without any examples.
options.C: Lower the max tokens setting so that the model is forced to commit to a single label much faster.
options.D: Switch the labels to numeric codes to remove ambiguity.

why: Examples that demonstrate tricky phrasings (sarcasm, hedging) with their ideal labels teach the model to handle exactly those corner cases.

explanations.A: Examples that demonstrate tricky phrasings (sarcasm, hedging) with their ideal labels teach the model to handle exactly those corner cases.
explanations.B: A bare warning rarely fixes nuanced misreads; demonstrating the cases does.
explanations.C: Token limits don't improve interpretation of sarcasm or hedging.
explanations.D: Relabeling doesn't help the model interpret tricky language; examples do.

prompt: Your extraction system occasionally produces JSON with syntax errors that break the downstream parser. You currently ask the model to "respond only with valid JSON." What's the most reliable way to guarantee schema-compliant structured output?

options.A: Define an extraction tool with a JSON schema as its input parameters and extract structured data from the `tool_use` response.
options.B: Add more emphatic instructions like "YOU MUST respond with valid JSON only, with no exceptions and absolutely no surrounding prose."
options.C: Post-process the model's text output with a regex that repairs malformed JSON.
options.D: Lower the temperature to 0 so the JSON is always formatted identically.

why: Tool use with JSON schemas is the most reliable approach for guaranteed schema-compliant output, eliminating JSON syntax errors entirely.

explanations.A: Tool use with JSON schemas is the most reliable approach for guaranteed schema-compliant output, eliminating JSON syntax errors entirely.
explanations.B: Prose instructions remain probabilistic and don't eliminate syntax errors.
explanations.C: Regex repair is brittle and can silently corrupt data; it treats the symptom, not the cause.
explanations.D: Temperature 0 reduces variability but does not guarantee valid JSON structure.

prompt: Your extraction returns a document's `stated_total` but downstream reconciliation shows the line items frequently don't sum to it. Strict JSON schema via tool use is already in place. What design best surfaces these discrepancies?

options.A: Extract a `calculated_total` alongside `stated_total` and add a `conflict_detected` boolean so discrepancies are flagged for review.
options.B: Trust the `stated_total` as authoritative and discard the individual line items whenever the two happen to disagree with each other.
options.C: Retry the extraction until the totals happen to match.
options.D: Remove the total fields from the schema to avoid the conflict.

why: Strict schemas eliminate syntax errors but not semantic errors. Extracting both totals and flagging conflicts surfaces inconsistent source data for self-correction or review.

explanations.A: Strict schemas eliminate syntax errors but not semantic errors. Extracting both totals and flagging conflicts surfaces inconsistent source data for self-correction or review.
explanations.B: Silently discarding data hides genuine discrepancies that downstream systems need to know about.
explanations.C: Retries don't help when the source document itself is internally inconsistent.
explanations.D: Dropping required data to avoid surfacing a conflict defeats the purpose of extraction.

prompt: On retry, your extraction system re-sends the same prompt when a required field comes back empty. For documents that genuinely lack the field, retries waste tokens and still fail. How should you decide when retrying is worthwhile?

options.A: Retry with appended validation errors for format failures, but not when the field is simply absent.
options.B: Always retry up to five times regardless of the failure cause.
options.C: Never retry; accept the first extraction result.
options.D: Fabricate a plausible-looking value whenever the field is missing from the document, purely so that the strict schema validates successfully.

why: Retry-with-error-feedback helps format and structural errors, but retries are ineffective when the required information isn't present in the source — that distinction should drive the decision.

explanations.A: Retry-with-error-feedback helps format and structural errors, but retries are ineffective when the required information isn't present in the source — that distinction should drive the decision.
explanations.B: Blind retries waste tokens on documents that genuinely lack the field.
explanations.C: This gives up on recoverable format/structural errors that retries would fix.
explanations.D: Fabricating values to satisfy a required field is a serious accuracy violation.

prompt: You want the model to always return data through your extraction tool rather than sometimes replying in prose. With a single `extract_invoice` tool defined, which `tool_choice` setting forces that tool to be called on every request?

options.A: Set `tool_choice` to `{"type":"tool","name":"extract_invoice"}` so the model is required to call that specific tool.
options.B: Set `tool_choice` to `{"type":"auto"}` and trust the model to pick the tool itself.
options.C: Set `tool_choice` to `{"type":"none"}` to keep the tool available to the model without applying any selection pressure at all.
options.D: Omit `tool_choice` and lower temperature so the tool is selected reliably.

why: Forcing a named tool guarantees the model invokes `extract_invoice` and returns schema-shaped input rather than free text.

explanations.A: Forcing a named tool guarantees the model invokes `extract_invoice` and returns schema-shaped input rather than free text.
explanations.B: `auto` lets the model decide and it may answer in prose instead of calling the tool.
explanations.C: `none` prevents tool use altogether, the opposite of what is required here.
explanations.D: Omitting it defaults to auto behavior; temperature does not force a tool call.

prompt: Your pipeline defines three extraction tools and you want Claude to call exactly one of them (its choice) but never reply in plain text. Which `tool_choice` value matches this requirement?

options.A: Use `{"type":"any"}`, which requires the model to use one of the available tools but lets it choose which.
options.B: Use `{"type":"auto"}`, which forces a tool call when several tools are present.
options.C: Use a forced named tool so the model is locked to exactly one specific schema on every single request it receives.
options.D: Use `{"type":"none"}`, which permits any of the tools to be used freely.

why: `any` forces some tool call while leaving the selection among the defined tools to the model.

explanations.A: `any` forces some tool call while leaving the selection among the defined tools to the model.
explanations.B: `auto` allows the model to skip tools and answer in prose, so it does not guarantee a call.
explanations.C: A named force removes the model's freedom to pick among the three tools.
explanations.D: `none` disables tool use entirely rather than requiring one.

prompt: Many source documents omit a customer's middle name. With a strict schema, the model keeps inventing initials to fill a required `middle_name` string. What schema change best stops the fabrication?

options.A: Make `middle_name` optional or nullable so the model can omit it when the source provides no value.
options.B: Keep `middle_name` as a required field and instead instruct the model in prose to simply leave it blank whenever it is missing.
options.C: Add a regex pattern to `middle_name` that only accepts single capital letters.
options.D: Default `middle_name` to the string "N/A" whenever the document is unclear.

why: Optional/nullable fields let the model represent genuinely absent data instead of fabricating values to satisfy a required field.

explanations.A: Optional/nullable fields let the model represent genuinely absent data instead of fabricating values to satisfy a required field.
explanations.B: A required field still pressures the model to fill it; prose guidance is weaker than the schema constraint.
explanations.C: A pattern constrains format but still demands a value, encouraging invented initials.
explanations.D: A literal placeholder injects fake data downstream rather than signaling true absence.

prompt: Your `document_type` enum has fixed values, but new contract subtypes keep appearing and the model is forced to mis-bin them. You still want clean categories for the common cases. What enum design handles this best?

options.A: Keep the known enum values but add an `other` member plus a free-text `other_detail` field for the model to describe novel types.
options.B: Remove the enum and let `document_type` be any free-text string.
options.C: Expand the enum every single time a brand-new contract subtype appears and then redeploy the schema across the whole pipeline.
options.D: Map every unrecognized subtype to the closest existing enum value.

why: An `other`+detail pattern keeps clean categories for known types while capturing extensible cases without mis-binning.

explanations.A: An `other`+detail pattern keeps clean categories for known types while capturing extensible cases without mis-binning.
explanations.B: Dropping the enum loses the consistency that downstream systems rely on for the common cases.
explanations.C: Constant redeploys are operationally brittle and still fail until each new type is added.
explanations.D: Forcing novel types into existing bins is exactly the mis-binning you are trying to avoid.

prompt: Some scanned documents are too blurry to read a value confidently, yet the model still emits a guess for the `payment_status` enum. You want it to mark uncertainty instead. What is the cleanest schema-level approach?

options.A: Add an `unclear` member to the `payment_status` enum so the model can flag ambiguous cases explicitly.
options.B: Instruct the model in the prompt to skip fields it cannot read clearly.
options.C: Lower the temperature so the model's enum guesses become more consistent.
options.D: Return the most statistically common `payment_status` value seen across the corpus whenever the scanned document is too blurry to read.

why: An explicit `unclear` enum value gives the model a sanctioned way to signal ambiguity rather than guessing among the real categories.

explanations.A: An explicit `unclear` enum value gives the model a sanctioned way to signal ambiguity rather than guessing among the real categories.
explanations.B: Skipping a required enum field tends to produce schema violations or silent guesses, not a clean signal.
explanations.C: Consistency in a guess does not make the guess correct or flag the underlying ambiguity.
explanations.D: Defaulting to a common value masks ambiguity and injects likely-wrong data.

prompt: A teammate argues that switching from prose-JSON to tool-use schemas will also guarantee the extracted amounts are correct. Why is that claim wrong?

options.A: Schemas enforce structure and types, but cannot verify that a captured value actually matches what the document says — that is a semantic error.
options.B: Tool schemas only ever validate the very first field in the object, so every later amount in the record is left completely unchecked.
options.C: Schemas validate amounts only when temperature is set to zero.
options.D: Tool use disables validation, so amounts are never checked at all.

why: Strict schemas eliminate syntax and type errors, yet semantic mistakes like a misread amount still pass validation.

explanations.A: Strict schemas eliminate syntax and type errors, yet semantic mistakes like a misread amount still pass validation.
explanations.B: Schemas validate all declared fields, not just the first; the real gap is semantic, not positional.
explanations.C: Schema validation is independent of temperature; this is a fabricated dependency.
explanations.D: Standard tool use applies schema validation; it does not disable checking.

prompt: Downstream consumers need numeric amounts, but the model sometimes returns `"1,200.00"` as a string. The field is declared as a `number` in the schema with tool use enabled. What does this tell you?

options.A: Declaring the field as `number` makes the tool layer reject string values, pushing the model toward a typed numeric output.
options.B: The schema cannot influence output types at all, so the formatted-string amount should simply be parsed manually by your own code afterward.
options.C: String amounts are unavoidable, so the field should be declared as a `string`.
options.D: The model needs a higher max_tokens limit to format numbers correctly.

why: Typed schema fields constrain output: a `number` type prevents formatted-string amounts and yields parseable numerics.

explanations.A: Typed schema fields constrain output: a `number` type prevents formatted-string amounts and yields parseable numerics.
explanations.B: Schemas do control types; relying on manual parsing ignores the reliability the schema provides.
explanations.C: Declaring it a string concedes the problem and forces brittle downstream parsing.
explanations.D: Token limits have nothing to do with numeric typing of a field.

prompt: Your extraction tool's schema describes a deeply nested `line_items` array of objects. The model occasionally returns the right values but in a flat structure. What is the most direct fix?

options.A: Tighten the tool's `input_schema` to require the nested array of typed objects, with clear field descriptions for each level.
options.B: Add a prose instruction telling the model to nest the line items properly.
options.C: Flatten your downstream consumer to accept whatever shape arrives.
options.D: Run the extraction twice and then write code to merge the two flat outputs together into a single nested structure afterward.

why: Making the nesting and types explicit in the schema constrains the model to the intended structure rather than a flat shape.

explanations.A: Making the nesting and types explicit in the schema constrains the model to the intended structure rather than a flat shape.
explanations.B: Prose is less reliable than the schema itself for enforcing structure.
explanations.C: Reshaping the consumer to match inconsistent output abandons the schema's guarantees.
explanations.D: Merging duplicate runs is wasteful and does not address the schema definition.

prompt: A reviewer asks why you chose tool use over assistant-message prefilling with a code-block stop sequence for guaranteeing JSON. What is the strongest justification?

options.A: Tool use validates output against a declared schema, so structural and type guarantees are enforced rather than merely encouraged.
options.B: Prefilling cannot produce JSON at all, so tool use is the only option.
options.C: Tool use is always strictly cheaper in token cost than assistant-message prefilling, for any document of any size.
options.D: Stop sequences are deprecated and no longer supported by the API.

why: Prefill+stop coaxes raw JSON but offers no schema validation; tool use enforces the declared structure and types.

explanations.A: Prefill+stop coaxes raw JSON but offers no schema validation; tool use enforces the declared structure and types.
explanations.B: Prefilling can produce JSON; the difference is enforcement, not capability.
explanations.C: Cost is not the deciding factor and is not guaranteed lower; the schema guarantee is the point.
explanations.D: Stop sequences remain supported; this is factually incorrect.

prompt: Each extracted record must include a confidence flag the model sets itself, but you do not want the model to omit it. You are using tool use. How do you ensure the field is always present?

options.A: List the confidence field in the schema's `required` array so the tool layer enforces its presence.
options.B: Mention the field in the tool description and hope the model includes it.
options.C: Make the confidence field optional in the schema and then backfill a sensible default value in your own code whenever it is absent.
options.D: Add the field twice in the schema so the model is more likely to emit it.

why: Adding a field to `required` makes the tool validation reject output that omits it, guaranteeing presence.

explanations.A: Adding a field to `required` makes the tool validation reject output that omits it, guaranteeing presence.
explanations.B: Descriptions guide but do not enforce; the field can still be omitted.
explanations.C: Backfilling discards the model's own signal, which defeats the purpose of a model-set flag.
explanations.D: Duplicating a property is invalid schema design and does not enforce presence.

prompt: For a contract-extraction tool, you want the model to use the tool when a contract is present but answer normally for non-contract uploads. Which `tool_choice` fits this mixed behavior?

options.A: Use `{"type":"auto"}` so the model calls the tool only when the input warrants it and otherwise replies directly.
options.B: Use `{"type":"any"}` so the model always calls a tool regardless of input.
options.C: Force the named contract tool so that every single upload, contract or not, is unconditionally parsed as though it were a contract.
options.D: Use `{"type":"none"}` so the tool is reserved for explicit user requests only.

why: `auto` lets the model decide per input whether the tool is appropriate, which matches mixed-content handling.

explanations.A: `auto` lets the model decide per input whether the tool is appropriate, which matches mixed-content handling.
explanations.B: `any` forces a tool call even for non-contract uploads, which is wrong here.
explanations.C: Forcing the tool misapplies contract extraction to non-contract inputs.
explanations.D: `none` blocks the tool entirely, so contracts would never be extracted.

prompt: An auditor reports that some `effective_date` values are valid dates but clearly wrong for the document. The schema already enforces ISO-8601 date format. What is the realistic conclusion?

options.A: Format validation passed, so the failure is semantic; you need cross-checks or human review, not a stricter date pattern.
options.B: The schema's date pattern is clearly broken and must be replaced with a much stricter regular expression to fix the wrong values.
options.C: The model needs the date field marked optional to stop the errors.
options.D: Switching `tool_choice` to forced will correct the date values.

why: A well-formatted but incorrect date is a semantic error that schema format rules cannot catch; verification beyond the schema is needed.

explanations.A: A well-formatted but incorrect date is a semantic error that schema format rules cannot catch; verification beyond the schema is needed.
explanations.B: The dates are valid in format; a stricter pattern would not catch a wrong-but-valid date.
explanations.C: Making the field optional would drop dates entirely, not improve correctness.
explanations.D: `tool_choice` governs whether a tool is called, not the correctness of extracted values.

prompt: When an extraction fails validation, your retry currently resends the original prompt unchanged. Accuracy barely improves on structural failures. What single change makes retries most effective for those failures?

options.A: Append the specific validation errors to the retry prompt so the model knows exactly what to correct.
options.B: Increase the number of retries from three up to ten while leaving the original prompt completely unchanged on each attempt.
options.C: Raise the temperature on each retry to encourage a different answer.
options.D: Switch to a smaller, faster model so retries complete more quickly.

why: Retry-with-error-feedback gives the model the precise structural problem to fix, which is far more effective than an unchanged resend.

explanations.A: Retry-with-error-feedback gives the model the precise structural problem to fix, which is far more effective than an unchanged resend.
explanations.B: More identical retries without feedback rarely converges on a fix.
explanations.C: Higher temperature adds randomness, not targeted correction of the structural error.
explanations.D: A faster model does not address why the validation failed.

prompt: A subset of invoices is missing a tax field that the model can never recover, while another subset just has malformed numbers. Your retry budget is fixed. How should you route retries?

options.A: Spend retries on format and structural errors with error feedback, and stop retrying when the field is genuinely absent from the source.
options.B: Split the retry budget evenly across both subsets to be fair.
options.C: Prioritize spending retries on the missing-field invoices first, on the grounds that those particular errors are the most severe.
options.D: Disable retries entirely and queue every failure for human review.

why: Retries are effective for recoverable format/structural issues but ineffective when the information is simply not present, so the budget should follow that distinction.

explanations.A: Retries are effective for recoverable format/structural issues but ineffective when the information is simply not present, so the budget should follow that distinction.
explanations.B: Even splitting wastes retries on documents that can never produce the missing field.
explanations.C: Severity does not make an absent value recoverable; retries there are wasted.
explanations.D: This abandons easily recoverable format errors that feedback retries would fix.

prompt: Reviewers notice the model often dismisses fields as "not found" even when the value is present but oddly placed. You want to study the failure mode before changing prompts. What field best enables that analysis?

options.A: Add a `detected_pattern` field where the model records the layout or cue it used, so dismissal patterns can be analyzed.
options.B: Add a free-text `notes` field where the model can write down anything it wants about how the extraction went for that document.
options.C: Increase max_tokens so the model can explain itself at length.
options.D: Lower temperature so the dismissals become deterministic.

why: Capturing a `detected_pattern` lets you analyze why the model dismissed values, revealing systematic layout-related failures.

explanations.A: Capturing a `detected_pattern` lets you analyze why the model dismissed values, revealing systematic layout-related failures.
explanations.B: Unstructured notes are hard to aggregate and won't reliably reveal dismissal patterns.
explanations.C: More room for prose does not produce a structured signal you can analyze.
explanations.D: Determinism does not explain the cause of the dismissals.

prompt: Reconciliation needs to know whether a mismatch between the printed total and the line-item sum was the document's fault or the model's. Which extraction design surfaces that distinction?

options.A: Extract `calculated_total` and `stated_total` plus a `conflict_detected` flag, so a mismatch points to inconsistent source data.
options.B: Extract only the `stated_total` and trust it as authoritative.
options.C: Recompute the total in a downstream step and silently overwrite whatever value the model originally returned for that field.
options.D: Round both numbers to whole units so small mismatches disappear.

why: Capturing both totals and a conflict flag lets reconciliation see that the source itself disagrees, separating document errors from extraction errors.

explanations.A: Capturing both totals and a conflict flag lets reconciliation see that the source itself disagrees, separating document errors from extraction errors.
explanations.B: With only one total there is nothing to compare, so the distinction is lost.
explanations.C: Overwriting hides the original conflict that reconciliation needs to see.
explanations.D: Rounding masks genuine discrepancies instead of revealing their source.

prompt: After three feedback-driven retries, a particular document still fails because the required clause does not exist in the text. What is the correct terminal behavior?

options.A: Stop retrying and mark the field as genuinely absent, since feedback cannot recover information that is not in the source.
options.B: Continue retrying with stronger wording until the clause appears.
options.C: Insert the most common clause text that has been seen across other similar documents in the corpus to fill the gap.
options.D: Lower the validation strictness so the empty value passes silently.

why: Retries are ineffective for absent information; the right outcome is to record the field as missing rather than burn more attempts.

explanations.A: Retries are ineffective for absent information; the right outcome is to record the field as missing rather than burn more attempts.
explanations.B: No wording can extract a clause that is not present in the document.
explanations.C: Inserting borrowed text fabricates content not in this source.
explanations.D: Relaxing validation hides the absence instead of recording it honestly.

prompt: Your retry handler appends a generic message: "Your previous output was invalid, try again." Accuracy on structural errors is still poor. What refinement helps most?

options.A: Include the exact validator messages, such as which field failed and why, instead of a generic invalidity notice.
options.B: Repeat the same generic message twice for emphasis.
options.C: Remove the message entirely and just resend the document.
options.D: Add the full JSON schema into the retry prompt again, but still include no details about which specific field actually failed.

why: Specific, field-level error feedback gives the model an actionable target; generic notices do not.

explanations.A: Specific, field-level error feedback gives the model an actionable target; generic notices do not.
explanations.B: Repetition of a vague message adds no actionable information.
explanations.C: Removing feedback returns to a blind resend, which underperforms.
explanations.D: Re-stating the schema without telling the model what failed is far less effective than specific errors.

prompt: Vendors format dates and addresses inconsistently, and your extractions vary in formatting run to run. Schema enforcement is already in place for types. What technique most improves formatting consistency?

options.A: Add a few-shot block of input/output pairs showing the exact normalized formatting you expect.
options.B: Add yet another strongly worded, emphatic sentence to the prompt asking the model for perfectly consistent formatting every time.
options.C: Raise max_tokens so the model has room to reformat carefully.
options.D: Run the extraction at temperature 1 to explore more formats.

why: Few-shot examples are the most effective way to lock in consistent formatting that the schema's type rules alone cannot specify.

explanations.A: Few-shot examples are the most effective way to lock in consistent formatting that the schema's type rules alone cannot specify.
explanations.B: Prose emphasis is weaker than concrete demonstrations for formatting consistency.
explanations.C: Token headroom does not teach the model the target format.
explanations.D: Higher temperature increases variability, the opposite of consistency.

prompt: Some incoming documents are clean tables while others are messy free-form letters, and the model handles the messy ones poorly. How should your few-shot examples be chosen?

options.A: Include examples that demonstrate handling both clean tables and messy free-form structures so the model generalizes across them.
options.B: Use only the very cleanest table examples in the prompt so that the model learns the single ideal format and nothing else.
options.C: Use a single example and rely on the schema to cover the rest.
options.D: Provide many examples of the same messy letter repeatedly.

why: Demonstrating varied document structures teaches the model to handle the full range, including the messy cases it currently fails.

explanations.A: Demonstrating varied document structures teaches the model to handle the full range, including the messy cases it currently fails.
explanations.B: Clean-only examples leave the model unprepared for the messy documents that fail.
explanations.C: One example cannot demonstrate the varied structures the model struggles with.
explanations.D: Repeating one structure does not teach generalization across formats.

prompt: Field notes record quantities informally, like "a couple dozen" or "half a pallet," and the model often returns null for these. You want consistent numeric estimates. What is the best prompting approach?

options.A: Provide few-shot examples mapping informal measurements to the normalized numeric values you expect.
options.B: Tell the model in prose that it must always estimate some number for the quantity field and may never under any circumstances return null.
options.C: Mark the quantity field nullable and accept the nulls.
options.D: Set temperature to 1 so the model is more willing to estimate.

why: Examples that show how informal measurements convert to numbers reduce empty/null extraction and teach the desired normalization.

explanations.A: Examples that show how informal measurements convert to numbers reduce empty/null extraction and teach the desired normalization.
explanations.B: A blunt instruction can push the model to guess wildly without showing the intended mapping.
explanations.C: Accepting nulls abandons the goal of consistent numeric estimates.
explanations.D: Temperature does not teach the conversion and harms consistency.

prompt: Audits show the model frequently returns empty strings for fields whose values are present but phrased unusually. Which prompt change most directly reduces this empty/null extraction?

options.A: Add few-shot examples where unusually phrased values are correctly extracted, showing the model the value really is present.
options.B: Instruct the model that it must never return an empty string for any field under any circumstance whatsoever, no matter what.
options.C: Reduce max_tokens to force the model to be more decisive.
options.D: Add the field name again in the schema description for emphasis.

why: Examples demonstrating extraction from unusual phrasing reduce hallucinated emptiness by showing the model the value exists.
