explanations.A: Examples demonstrating extraction from unusual phrasing reduce hallucinated emptiness by showing the model the value exists.
explanations.B: A blanket ban can push fabrication when a value truly is absent; examples are more precise.
explanations.C: Constraining length does not help the model recognize unusual phrasing.
explanations.D: Repeating the name does not demonstrate how to read unusual phrasing.

prompt: A colleague worries that adding examples will make the model copy the example values into real extractions. How do you both keep few-shot benefits and avoid that leakage?

options.A: Use clearly fictitious example values and wrap examples in distinct tags so the model treats them as patterns, not data to copy.
options.B: Drop examples entirely to remove any risk of copying.
options.C: Use real prior customer documents directly as the examples so that all of the demonstrated values look fully authentic to the model.
options.D: Place the examples after the document so they are read last.

why: Clearly delimited, obviously fake examples teach the pattern while signaling they are not the document to extract from.

explanations.A: Clearly delimited, obviously fake examples teach the pattern while signaling they are not the document to extract from.
explanations.B: Removing examples loses the consistency benefit they provide.
explanations.C: Realistic values increase the chance the model copies them and risk leaking customer data.
explanations.D: Ordering alone does not prevent copying and can reduce the examples' guiding effect.

prompt: You added examples but the model still hallucinates plausible-sounding values for fields that are absent. Which adjustment to your few-shot set best curbs this?

options.A: Include an example where a field is genuinely absent and the ideal output leaves it null, demonstrating restraint.
options.B: Add many more examples to the prompt in which every single field is always fully populated with a value.
options.C: Increase the number of examples regardless of their content.
options.D: Tell the model the examples are only suggestions it can ignore.

why: Showing a correct null output for an absent field teaches the model that omission is the right behavior, reducing hallucination.

explanations.A: Showing a correct null output for an absent field teaches the model that omission is the right behavior, reducing hallucination.
explanations.B: All-populated examples reinforce the habit of always filling fields, worsening hallucination.
explanations.C: Quantity without an absence example does not teach restraint.
explanations.D: Undercutting the examples weakens their guidance entirely.

prompt: You must extract fields from 80,000 archived PDFs overnight, and latency per document does not matter. Cost does. Which approach fits best?

options.A: Submit the documents through the Message Batches API, which offers about 50% cost savings within a 24-hour window.
options.B: Send each of the PDFs as an individual real-time synchronous request, firing them off as fast as your account rate limit will allow.
options.C: Stream every document and stop early to save output tokens.
options.D: Concatenate all 80,000 PDFs into one request to amortize overhead.

why: Batch processing is ideal for large, latency-tolerant volumes and provides roughly 50% savings over standard calls within the 24-hour window.

explanations.A: Batch processing is ideal for large, latency-tolerant volumes and provides roughly 50% savings over standard calls within the 24-hour window.
explanations.B: Real-time calls cost more and provide no benefit when latency does not matter.
explanations.C: Streaming addresses UX latency, not bulk cost for an overnight job.
explanations.D: A single giant request will exceed context limits and is not how batching works.

prompt: A batch of 10,000 documents finishes and 240 entries returned errors. You want to reprocess only those without resubmitting the whole batch. What is the right mechanism?

options.A: Match results to inputs by their `custom_id` and resubmit only the failed `custom_id` entries in a new batch.
options.B: Resubmit the entire batch since results arrive in submission order.
options.C: Switch all of the failed documents over to real-time synchronous calls without any reliable way of tracking which ones actually failed.
options.D: Wait and re-poll the same batch until the errors resolve themselves.

why: Each batch request carries a `custom_id`, letting you isolate and resubmit only the failed documents instead of the whole batch.

explanations.A: Each batch request carries a `custom_id`, letting you isolate and resubmit only the failed documents instead of the whole batch.
explanations.B: Batch results are not guaranteed in order, and resubmitting all 10,000 wastes cost.
explanations.C: Without `custom_id` matching you cannot reliably identify which entries failed.
explanations.D: Completed errors do not self-heal by re-polling the finished batch.

prompt: In a large batch, a handful of documents failed because they exceeded the context window. What is the appropriate corrective step for just those documents?

options.A: Chunk each oversized document into context-sized pieces and re-extract, then merge the per-chunk results.
options.B: Resubmit the oversized documents completely unchanged in a fresh batch and simply hope that they happen to fit within the window next time.
options.C: Truncate each oversized document to fit and accept the lost content.
options.D: Move the oversized documents to a higher max_tokens setting.

why: Documents that exceed the context window must be chunked to fit; merging chunk results recovers the full extraction.

explanations.A: Documents that exceed the context window must be chunked to fit; merging chunk results recovers the full extraction.
explanations.B: The documents will exceed the window every time unless they are chunked.
explanations.C: Truncation silently drops content that may contain required fields.
explanations.D: max_tokens limits output length, not the input context window.

prompt: Before launching a 200,000-document batch, your lead asks how to avoid discovering a bad prompt only after spending the full budget. What is the most cost-effective safeguard?

options.A: Prompt-refine on a small representative sample first, then run the full batch once accuracy is acceptable.
options.B: Run the full 200,000-document batch first and then inspect a random sample of the results once everything has completed.
options.C: Split the batch into ten and run all ten simultaneously.
options.D: Trust the schema to catch any prompt issues during the run.

why: Refining the prompt on a sample before committing the large batch catches prompt problems cheaply, avoiding a costly bad run.

explanations.A: Refining the prompt on a sample before committing the large batch catches prompt problems cheaply, avoiding a costly bad run.
explanations.B: Inspecting afterward means the full cost is already spent on a possibly bad prompt.
explanations.C: Parallel batches do not validate the prompt and still spend the full budget.
explanations.D: The schema catches structure, not whether the prompt extracts the right things.

prompt: A stakeholder needs results from a batch within minutes for an interactive dashboard. Why is the Message Batches API a poor fit here?

options.A: Batch processing is asynchronous with up to a 24-hour completion window, so it cannot meet a minutes-level interactive need.
options.B: Batch processing simply cannot use tool-use schemas at all, so the structured data feeding the dashboard would always come back malformed.
options.C: Batch processing costs more than real-time calls, which a dashboard cannot afford.
options.D: Batch processing limits each job to a single document, too few for a dashboard.

why: The batch API trades latency for cost; its 24-hour window makes it unsuitable for interactive, minutes-level responses.

explanations.A: The batch API trades latency for cost; its 24-hour window makes it unsuitable for interactive, minutes-level responses.
explanations.B: Batches can use tool-use schemas; the real issue is latency, not schema support.
explanations.C: Batches are cheaper, not more expensive; cost is not the problem here.
explanations.D: Batches handle many documents; the single-document claim is false.

prompt: Your team wants to track which extracted record came from which source file across an asynchronous batch whose results may arrive out of order. What is the cleanest design?

options.A: Assign each request a meaningful `custom_id` keyed to the source file, then join results back by that id.
options.B: Rely on the order of the results array to map back to input files.
options.C: Embed the source filename directly inside each document's text and then parse it back out of the model's response afterward.
options.D: Submit one document per batch so correlation is trivial.

why: A meaningful `custom_id` is the intended way to correlate out-of-order batch results back to their source files.

explanations.A: A meaningful `custom_id` is the intended way to correlate out-of-order batch results back to their source files.
explanations.B: Batch results are not guaranteed in submission order, so positional mapping is unsafe.
explanations.C: This is fragile and unnecessary when `custom_id` exists for exactly this purpose.
explanations.D: One-per-batch defeats the cost and efficiency benefits of batching.

prompt: Different annotators disagree on what counts as the "primary vendor" on multi-vendor invoices, so extractions are inconsistent. What is the most effective first step?

options.A: Write explicit extraction criteria defining exactly how to identify the primary vendor, then encode them in the prompt.
options.B: Let the model decide the primary vendor however it sees fit each time.
options.C: Pick whichever vendor simply happens to appear first in the document by its physical position on the page.
options.D: Add more retries so disagreements average out across attempts.

why: Ambiguous, undefined criteria cause inconsistency; stating explicit, unambiguous rules is the foundational fix before tuning anything else.

explanations.A: Ambiguous, undefined criteria cause inconsistency; stating explicit, unambiguous rules is the foundational fix before tuning anything else.
explanations.B: Leaving the definition implicit perpetuates the inconsistency.
explanations.C: A positional shortcut may not reflect the true primary vendor and bakes in a wrong rule.
explanations.D: Retries cannot resolve an ambiguity that the criteria never defined.

prompt: You are drafting the extraction prompt for a new document type. Which instruction set most reduces downstream ambiguity about what to capture?

options.A: Spell out each target field, its expected format, and how to treat missing or conflicting values explicitly.
options.B: Tell the model to extract all the important information from the document.
options.C: Provide only the bare JSON schema and simply assume that the field names alone are completely self-explanatory to the model.
options.D: Ask the model to summarize the document and infer fields from the summary.

why: Explicit per-field criteria covering format and edge handling give the model an unambiguous target, the core of D4.1.

explanations.A: Explicit per-field criteria covering format and edge handling give the model an unambiguous target, the core of D4.1.
explanations.B: "Important" is subjective and leaves the model guessing what matters.
explanations.C: Field names alone rarely convey format and edge-case rules, leaving ambiguity.
explanations.D: Summarize-then-infer adds a lossy step and does not pin down explicit criteria.

prompt: Your validation layer rejects an extraction because a date is in the future, which your business rules forbid. The format is valid. What kind of check caught this and what should the retry include?

options.A: A semantic business-rule check caught it; the retry should include that specific rule violation as feedback.
options.B: A syntax check caught it; the retry should resend the document unchanged.
options.C: A schema type check is what caught it, and so the retry should respond by loosening the declared type on the date field.
options.D: Nothing meaningful caught it; the rule should be removed.

why: Format was fine, so a semantic/business-rule validator flagged it; feeding that specific violation back makes the retry actionable.

explanations.A: Format was fine, so a semantic/business-rule validator flagged it; feeding that specific violation back makes the retry actionable.
explanations.B: The format was valid, so this is not a syntax error, and a blind resend is unhelpful.
explanations.C: The type was valid; loosening it would let bad dates through.
explanations.D: The business rule is doing its job; removing it ignores a real requirement.

prompt: You want categories like `region` to stay clean for the five known regions but still capture rare territories without breaking validation. Which schema pattern is most maintainable?

options.A: Define the five regions as enum values plus an `other` option, paired with an optional `region_detail` string.
options.B: Make `region` a free-text field and normalize it downstream every time.
options.C: Hard-code every conceivable world territory and region into the enum up front before the system ever goes live.
options.D: Reject any document whose region is not one of the five known values.

why: Enum-plus-`other`-with-detail keeps the common cases clean while extensibly capturing rare territories without constant schema edits.

explanations.A: Enum-plus-`other`-with-detail keeps the common cases clean while extensibly capturing rare territories without constant schema edits.
explanations.B: Free text loses the clean categorization for the common five regions.
explanations.C: An exhaustive enum is unmaintainable and still breaks on anything unforeseen.
explanations.D: Rejecting valid documents with rare regions is overly brittle.

prompt: Your conflict-detection logic sets `conflict_detected` to true whenever `calculated_total` and `stated_total` differ by any amount, flooding reviewers with rounding-cent mismatches. What is the best refinement?

options.A: Flag a conflict only when the difference exceeds a defined tolerance, so trivial rounding does not trigger review.
options.B: Stop extracting `calculated_total` so there is nothing to compare.
options.C: Route every flagged document to human review regardless of size.
options.D: Round both the calculated and stated totals to the nearest hundred currency units before comparing them to each other.

why: A tolerance threshold keeps the conflict flag meaningful, surfacing real discrepancies while ignoring negligible rounding noise.

explanations.A: A tolerance threshold keeps the conflict flag meaningful, surfacing real discrepancies while ignoring negligible rounding noise.
explanations.B: Removing the comparison loses the ability to detect genuine discrepancies.
explanations.C: That keeps flooding reviewers with trivial cent-level mismatches.
explanations.D: Coarse rounding could hide real mid-size discrepancies, not just cents.

prompt: Your few-shot examples improved formatting, but the model now rigidly copies the example's field order even when a document presents fields differently. What is the balanced fix?

options.A: Vary the field order across several examples so the model learns the content mapping rather than a fixed sequence.
options.B: Remove all examples to eliminate the rigidity.
options.C: Add a prose note that field order does not matter.
options.D: Reorder the fields in every incoming document to exactly match the example's order before running the extraction.

why: Diverse example orderings teach the model to map by meaning, not position, while preserving the formatting gains.

explanations.A: Diverse example orderings teach the model to map by meaning, not position, while preserving the formatting gains.
explanations.B: Removing examples sacrifices the formatting consistency they provided.
explanations.C: A note is weaker than demonstrating varied orders across examples.
explanations.D: Pre-reordering documents is costly and brittle compared to varying examples.

prompt: When you feed a very long document, the model reliably extracts fields described near the top and bottom of the prompt but misses the field defined in the middle. What is the most likely cause?

options.A: The model is exhibiting the "lost in the middle" effect, where information in the center of a long context gets less attention.
options.B: The model has run out of training data for that field type.
options.C: The schema validates the top and bottom fields of the object first and then simply times out before it reaches the middle ones.
options.D: The temperature setting only affects the middle of the response.

why: Models tend to recall content at the start and end of long contexts better than the middle, which explains the missed central field.

explanations.A: Models tend to recall content at the start and end of long contexts better than the middle, which explains the missed central field.
explanations.B: Position-dependent misses point to context handling, not training coverage.
explanations.C: Schema validation is not positional and does not time out by location.
explanations.D: Temperature applies uniformly and does not target a region of the prompt.

prompt: Your prompt buries the critical extraction instructions in the middle of a long block of boilerplate context, and accuracy suffers. What placement strategy helps most?

options.A: Move the key instructions and the most important content to the start and end of the prompt.
options.B: Repeat the boilerplate so the instructions blend in naturally.
options.C: Center the instructions exactly in the middle for balance.
options.D: Lower the temperature toward zero so that the instructions buried in the middle of the prompt are followed more precisely.

why: Placing key information at the beginning and end counteracts the lost-in-the-middle effect and improves recall.

explanations.A: Placing key information at the beginning and end counteracts the lost-in-the-middle effect and improves recall.
explanations.B: More boilerplate dilutes attention rather than focusing it on instructions.
explanations.C: The middle is precisely where attention is weakest in long contexts.
explanations.D: Temperature does not change positional attention within the prompt.

prompt: A tool your pipeline calls returns a huge raw API payload, most of which is irrelevant to extraction, and it pushes the prompt near the context limit. What is the best handling?

options.A: Trim the tool output down to the relevant fields before placing it back into the context.
options.B: Pass the entire raw payload so the model has all possible information.
options.C: Increase max_tokens so the large payload fits.
options.D: Compress the entire raw payload with gzip first and then add the compressed bytes directly into the prompt context.

why: Trimming verbose tool outputs to only relevant fields conserves context and keeps the model focused on what matters.

explanations.A: Trimming verbose tool outputs to only relevant fields conserves context and keeps the model focused on what matters.
explanations.B: Dumping the full payload wastes context and buries the relevant data.
explanations.C: max_tokens governs output length, not input context capacity.
explanations.D: The model cannot read gzip bytes; it needs readable, relevant text.

prompt: A developer assumes that because the previous extraction call established the schema, the next standalone call will remember it. Extractions on the new call ignore the schema. Why?

options.A: The model is stateless, so each request must include all it needs.
options.B: The schema expires after a fixed number of seconds on the server.
options.C: The second call used a different temperature, dropping the schema.
options.D: Schemas only persist when streaming is enabled across calls.

why: Claude retains no memory across separate API calls; the schema and instructions must be supplied every time.

explanations.A: Claude retains no memory across separate API calls; the schema and instructions must be supplied every time.
explanations.B: There is no server-side schema timer; the cause is statelessness, not expiry.
explanations.C: Temperature does not carry or drop a schema between calls.
explanations.D: Streaming does not create cross-call memory; calls remain independent.

prompt: Downstream auditors must be able to verify each extracted claim against the document. What output design best supports that requirement?

options.A: Produce claim-source mappings that link each extracted value to the location or quote it came from.
options.B: Return only the final values and trust the model's accuracy.
options.C: Append the entire source document after the extracted values so that reviewers have it available for reference if needed.
options.D: Add a single overall confidence score for the whole record.

why: Claim-source mappings give auditors traceable provenance from each value back to its supporting text.

explanations.A: Claim-source mappings give auditors traceable provenance from each value back to its supporting text.
explanations.B: Values without provenance cannot be independently verified by auditors.
explanations.C: Dumping the whole document does not link specific claims to specific sources.
explanations.D: One global score does not let an auditor trace an individual claim to its source.

prompt: A market report cites two different figures for the same metric from two analysts. Your extraction must not silently pick one. What is the correct approach?

options.A: Annotate both conflicting statistics with their respective sources rather than choosing a single value.
options.B: Average the two figures and report the single mean.
options.C: Report only the larger of the two figures on the grounds that the larger number is the more conservative choice to make.
options.D: Drop the metric entirely because the sources disagree.

why: Attributing each conflicting figure to its source preserves provenance and avoids fabricating a false consensus.

explanations.A: Attributing each conflicting figure to its source preserves provenance and avoids fabricating a false consensus.
explanations.B: Averaging invents a number neither source reported and erases the conflict.
explanations.C: Arbitrarily picking one silently discards the other source's claim.
explanations.D: Dropping the data loses information the consumer may need with attribution.

prompt: Two source documents report different revenue numbers, and downstream consumers flag them as contradictions even though the documents cover different fiscal years. What extraction requirement prevents this misreading?

options.A: Require a publication or collection date for each figure so temporal differences are not misread as contradictions.
options.B: Keep only the most recent figure and discard the older one.
options.C: Mark both of the revenue figures as inherently unreliable simply because the two numbers happen to disagree with each other.
options.D: Force the two numbers to reconcile by averaging them.

why: Capturing dates lets consumers see the figures describe different periods rather than conflicting facts.

explanations.A: Capturing dates lets consumers see the figures describe different periods rather than conflicting facts.
explanations.B: Discarding the older figure loses valid historical data the consumer may need.
explanations.C: The figures are not unreliable; they simply describe different periods.
explanations.D: Averaging across different fiscal years produces a meaningless figure.

prompt: Your system extracts both financial tables and news commentary, but rendering everything as identical flat key-value pairs makes the output hard to consume. What output strategy is most appropriate?

options.A: Render financial data as structured tables and narrative news as prose, matching format to content type.
options.B: Force all content into a single uniform key-value format for consistency.
options.C: Render absolutely everything as flowing prose paragraphs, including all of the structured financial tables and their figures.
options.D: Drop the news commentary and keep only the financial tables.

why: Choosing a representation that fits each content type (tables for figures, prose for narrative) improves usability over one uniform format.

explanations.A: Choosing a representation that fits each content type (tables for figures, prose for narrative) improves usability over one uniform format.
explanations.B: One uniform format misrepresents narrative content and hurts usability.
explanations.C: Prose obscures tabular financial relationships that tables convey clearly.
explanations.D: Discarding the commentary loses information the consumer wants.

prompt: Your dashboard proudly reports "97% overall extraction accuracy," yet a key client's contract type keeps producing errors. What is the most likely problem with that metric?

options.A: An aggregate accuracy figure can mask poor performance on specific document types or fields that are rarer in the mix.
options.B: 97% is simply too low and should be raised to 100% before launch.
options.C: The metric itself is fundamentally wrong because accuracy simply cannot be measured for a structured extraction task at all.
options.D: Overall accuracy is fine; the client's documents must be defective.

why: A high overall number can hide systematically poor results on a minority document type, which is exactly what the client is hitting.

explanations.A: A high overall number can hide systematically poor results on a minority document type, which is exactly what the client is hitting.
explanations.B: The issue is hidden variance by type, not the headline number being too low.
explanations.C: Extraction accuracy is measurable; the problem is aggregation masking subgroups.
explanations.D: Dismissing the subgroup ignores a real, measurable performance gap.

prompt: You need a trustworthy estimate of error rates across many document types without hand-labeling everything. What sampling approach gives the most representative measurement?

options.A: Use stratified random sampling so each document type's error rate is measurable.
options.B: Hand-pick the documents that look hardest to extract.
options.C: Sample only the most common document type to save effort.
options.D: Review the first 100 documents from the queue in order.

why: Stratified random sampling ensures each document type is measured, surfacing per-type error rates that a simple aggregate would hide.

explanations.A: Stratified random sampling ensures each document type is measured, surfacing per-type error rates that a simple aggregate would hide.
explanations.B: Cherry-picking hard cases biases the estimate and is not representative.
explanations.C: Ignoring rarer types leaves their error rates unmeasured.
explanations.D: Sequential sampling can reflect arrival order, not the true type distribution.

prompt: Your model emits a confidence number per field, but those numbers do not track actual correctness. How do you make field-level confidence scores trustworthy?

options.A: Calibrate field-level confidence scores against a labeled validation set.
options.B: Multiply every raw confidence by a fixed constant to scale it.
options.C: Trust the raw scores as-is since the model produced them.
options.D: Replace per-field scores with one document-level guess.

why: Calibrating confidence on a labeled validation set aligns the scores with observed correctness, making them actionable.

explanations.A: Calibrating confidence on a labeled validation set aligns the scores with observed correctness, making them actionable.
explanations.B: An arbitrary scaling factor does not align scores with actual accuracy.
explanations.C: Uncalibrated model confidence is known to be unreliable on its own.
explanations.D: Collapsing to one score loses the field granularity you need.

prompt: Most extractions are high-confidence, but a steady stream of low-confidence or ambiguous ones risks shipping wrong data to finance systems. What is the most reliable handling?

options.A: Route low-confidence extractions to human review; auto-process the rest.
options.B: Auto-process everything and correct mistakes after they reach finance.
options.C: Send every extraction to human review to be safe.
options.D: Drop all low-confidence extractions without any review.

why: A confidence threshold that diverts uncertain cases to human review protects accuracy without slowing the confident majority.

explanations.A: A confidence threshold that diverts uncertain cases to human review protects accuracy without slowing the confident majority.
explanations.B: Letting wrong data flow downstream and fixing it later is costly and risky.
explanations.C: Reviewing the confident majority wastes human effort unnecessarily.
explanations.D: Silently dropping uncertain cases loses data that review could recover.

prompt: Your prompt prepends thousands of tokens of unrelated company policy before every document. Extraction quality drops as documents grow. What is the most effective context fix?

options.A: Strip context irrelevant to extraction so the prompt stays focused.
options.B: Add even more policy text so the model is fully informed.
options.C: Place the policy text in the exact middle of the prompt.
options.D: Raise max_tokens so the larger prompt is accepted.

why: Pruning irrelevant context preserves the model's attention for the document and counters degradation as inputs grow.

explanations.A: Pruning irrelevant context preserves the model's attention for the document and counters degradation as inputs grow.
explanations.B: More irrelevant text worsens the dilution and context pressure.
explanations.C: Relocating irrelevant text does not solve the underlying bloat.
explanations.D: max_tokens controls output length, not input attention or prompt size handling.

prompt: A consumer disputes an extracted figure, claiming the model invented it. You have no record of where each value came from. What design would have prevented the dispute?

options.A: Store claim-source mappings tying each value to its supporting passage.
options.B: Logging only the model's overall response time per document.
options.C: Keeping a count of how many fields were extracted per document.
options.D: Saving the temperature used for each extraction call.

why: Provenance via claim-source mappings lets you immediately show where a value came from, settling disputes.

explanations.A: Provenance via claim-source mappings lets you immediately show where a value came from, settling disputes.
explanations.B: Timing logs say nothing about where a value originated.
explanations.C: A field count does not trace any individual value to its source.
explanations.D: Temperature metadata cannot establish the source of a value.

prompt: Leadership wants a single accuracy number to advertise, but your QA lead objects. What is the strongest argument for reporting stratified results instead?

options.A: A single number can hide that some types or fields perform far worse.
options.B: A single number is harder to compute than per-type numbers.
options.C: Stratified results always look better, so they market the system more favorably.
options.D: A single number cannot be calculated without a labeled set.

why: Stratified reporting exposes per-type weaknesses that an aggregate conceals, giving an honest risk picture.

explanations.A: Stratified reporting exposes per-type weaknesses that an aggregate conceals, giving an honest risk picture.
explanations.B: Computation difficulty is not the issue; masking subgroup performance is.
explanations.C: The point is honesty about weak spots, not making numbers look better.
explanations.D: Both aggregate and stratified numbers need labels; that is not the distinguishing concern.

prompt: You assemble the prompt as: long boilerplate, then the document, then the extraction instructions at the very end. Central document fields are sometimes missed. Which adjustment best addresses recall of key content?

options.A: Surface the key fields near both the start and end of the document.
options.B: Move all instructions to the exact middle of the document.
options.C: Delete the extraction instructions to shorten the prompt.
options.D: Duplicate the boilerplate before and after the document.

why: Bracketing the document with key cues at the start and end leverages the strongest attention positions and improves recall.

explanations.A: Bracketing the document with key cues at the start and end leverages the strongest attention positions and improves recall.
explanations.B: The middle is the weakest position for recall in long contexts.
explanations.C: Removing instructions leaves the model without guidance entirely.
explanations.D: Adding boilerplate at the edges crowds out the key cues that belong there.

prompt: An extracted dataset blends hard numbers from filings with speculative statements from press releases, and consumers treat them with equal weight. What provenance practice fixes this?

options.A: Attribute each value to its source so consumers can weight them.
options.B: Strip all source information so the data looks uniform and clean.
options.C: Keep only the press-release statements since they are more recent.
options.D: Tag every value with the same generic "document" label.

why: Source attribution lets consumers judge reliability per origin instead of treating all values as equivalent.

explanations.A: Source attribution lets consumers judge reliability per origin instead of treating all values as equivalent.
explanations.B: Removing provenance is the cause of the equal-weighting problem, not the fix.
explanations.C: Discarding the filings loses the most reliable figures.
explanations.D: A single generic label does not distinguish reliable from speculative sources.

prompt: You set a confidence threshold for routing to human review, but reviewers say many routed items were actually obvious and correct. What does this indicate about your scores?

options.A: The scores are poorly calibrated; re-fit them on labeled data.
options.B: The human reviewers are misjudging the difficulty of the items.
options.C: The threshold should simply be raised so fewer items route.
options.D: Confidence scoring should be abandoned for this pipeline.

why: If low scores land on obviously correct items, the scores do not reflect real accuracy and need calibration.

explanations.A: If low scores land on obviously correct items, the scores do not reflect real accuracy and need calibration.
explanations.B: Blaming reviewers ignores the clear signal that the scores are miscalibrated.
explanations.C: Raising the threshold on miscalibrated scores can also let real errors through.
explanations.D: Calibration fixes the scores; abandoning them removes a useful triage signal.

prompt: Across a multi-turn extraction workflow you assume earlier tool outputs are still "in the model's memory" on later calls, but they are not present in the messages you resend. Why do later extractions lose that data?

options.A: The model is stateless: only content in the request is available.
options.B: Because tool outputs are automatically purged after one minute server-side.
options.C: Because later calls used a different model alias.
options.D: Because tool outputs can only be read by the tool that produced them.

why: Statelessness means each call sees only what you send; prior tool outputs must be carried forward explicitly to remain available.

explanations.A: Statelessness means each call sees only what you send; prior tool outputs must be carried forward explicitly to remain available.
explanations.B: There is no automatic server-side purge timer; the cause is statelessness.
explanations.C: A model alias does not erase context you fail to include.
explanations.D: Tool outputs placed in the messages are readable by the model; the issue is they were omitted.

prompt: Consumers keep flagging two extracted survey percentages as a contradiction. Investigation shows the surveys were run a year apart. Which extraction field would have prevented the false flag?

options.A: A collection date on each percentage, so the year gap explains it.
options.B: A combined average of the two percentages with no dates.
options.C: A flag marking one survey as the official one.
options.D: A note saying the two numbers are unreliable.

why: Recording when each datum was collected lets consumers attribute the difference to time rather than contradiction.

explanations.A: Recording when each datum was collected lets consumers attribute the difference to time rather than contradiction.
explanations.B: Averaging hides the temporal difference and invents a non-reported figure.
explanations.C: Arbitrarily privileging one survey discards valid data without explaining the gap.
explanations.D: The numbers are reliable for their respective dates; the missing piece is the date itself.

prompt: Your team wants confidence scores that genuinely help triage which extractions to trust. Which combination of practices delivers that most reliably?

options.A: Calibrate field scores on labeled data; route low ones to review.
options.B: Report a single uncalibrated document score and trust it directly.
options.C: Use the model's raw token probabilities as the only signal.
options.D: Skip scoring and review a fixed random 10% of all output.

why: Calibrated field-level confidence plus threshold-based human routing turns scores into a dependable triage mechanism.

explanations.A: Calibrated field-level confidence plus threshold-based human routing turns scores into a dependable triage mechanism.
explanations.B: An uncalibrated, coarse score is not a reliable basis for triage.
explanations.C: Raw probabilities are uncalibrated and not directly meaningful as field confidence.
explanations.D: Fixed random review ignores confidence and can miss the riskiest cases.

prompt: Each extraction call includes a verbose system preamble, a long retrieved context, and the document, frequently nearing the context window. Quality is inconsistent. What is the highest-leverage first move?

options.A: Trim context to what extraction needs; keep key instructions at the edges.
options.B: Add a longer preamble explaining the task in more detail.
options.C: Keep all context but lower the temperature to 0.
options.D: Move the document to the middle to balance the prompt.

why: Reducing irrelevant context and positioning key instructions at the start and end directly addresses both crowding and the lost-in-the-middle effect.

explanations.A: Reducing irrelevant context and positioning key instructions at the start and end directly addresses both crowding and the lost-in-the-middle effect.
explanations.B: Lengthening the preamble worsens the context pressure rather than easing it.
explanations.C: Temperature does not relieve context crowding or positional attention loss.
explanations.D: Centering the document places key content in the weakest attention zone.

prompt: A regulated workflow requires that every extracted financial figure be auditable and that conflicting source claims never be silently resolved. Which output design satisfies both constraints?

options.A: Attach claim-source mappings and keep conflicting claims separately attributed.
options.B: Resolve conflicts internally and emit one clean value per figure.
options.C: Emit figures without sources but log the prompt for auditing.
options.D: Keep only the highest-confidence figure and drop the rest.

why: Provenance mappings make figures auditable while keeping conflicting claims attributed rather than silently merged.

explanations.A: Provenance mappings make figures auditable while keeping conflicting claims attributed rather than silently merged.
explanations.B: Silent resolution violates the requirement to not hide conflicting claims.
explanations.C: A prompt log does not tie each figure to its supporting source.
explanations.D: Dropping conflicting figures silently resolves the conflict, which is forbidden.

situation: Data shows that in 12% of cases the agent skips `get_customer` and calls `lookup_order` using only the customer’s name, which leads to incorrect refunds.
prompt: Which change is most effective?

options.A: Add a programmatic precondition that blocks `lookup_order` and `process_refund` until an ID is obtained from `get_customer`
options.B: Improve the system prompt
options.C: Add few-shot examples
options.D: Implement a routing classifier

why: When critical business logic requires a specific tool sequence, software provides **deterministic guarantees** that prompt-based approaches (B, C) cannot. D addresses availability, not tool ordering.

situation: The agent often calls `get_customer` instead of `lookup_order` for order-related questions. Tool descriptions are minimal and similar.
prompt: What is the first step?

options.A: Few-shot examples
options.B: Expand each tool’s description with input formats, examples, and boundaries
options.C: Add a routing layer
options.D: Merge the tools

why: Tool descriptions are the model’s primary selection mechanism. This is the lowest-effort, highest-impact fix. A adds tokens without addressing the root cause. C is overengineering. D requires more effort than justified.

situation: The agent resolves only 55% of issues with a target of 80%. It escalates simple cases and tries to handle complex policy exceptions autonomously.
prompt: How do you improve calibration?

options.A: Add explicit escalation criteria with few-shot examples
options.B: Self-rated confidence (1–10) with automatic escalation
options.C: A separate classifier trained on historical data
options.D: Sentiment analysis

why: It directly addresses the root cause—unclear decision boundaries. B is unreliable (the model can be confidently wrong). C is overengineering. D solves a different problem (mood != complexity).

situation: You need a custom `/review` command for standard code review that is available to the whole team when they clone the repository.
prompt: Where should you create the command file?

options.A: `.claude/commands/` in the project repository
options.B: `~/.claude/commands/`
options.C: Root `CLAUDE.md`
options.D: `.claude/config.json`

why: Project commands stored in `.claude/commands/` are version-controlled and automatically available to everyone. B is for personal commands. C is for instructions, not command definitions. D does not exist.

situation: You need to restructure a monolith into microservices (dozens of files, service-boundary decisions).
prompt: What approach should you use?

options.A: Planning mode: explore the codebase, understand dependencies, design an approach
options.B: Direct execution incrementally
options.C: Direct execution with detailed up-front instructions
options.D: Direct execution and switch to planning when it gets hard

why: Planning mode is designed for large changes, multiple possible approaches, and architectural decisions. B risks expensive rework. C assumes you already know the structure. D is reactive.

situation: A codebase has different conventions across areas (React, API, database). Tests are co-located with code. You want conventions to be applied automatically.
prompt: What approach should you use?

options.A: `.claude/rules/` files with YAML frontmatter and glob patterns
options.B: Put everything in the root CLAUDE.md
options.C: Skills in `.claude/skills/`
options.D: CLAUDE.md in every directory

why: `.claude/rules/` with glob patterns (e.g., `**/*.test.tsx`) enables automatic convention application based on file paths—ideal for tests spread across the codebase. B relies on model inference. C is manual/on-demand. D does not work well when relevant files are in many directories.

situation: The system researches “AI impact on creative industries,” but reports cover only visual art. The coordinator decomposed the topic into: “AI in digital art,” “AI in graphic design,” “AI in photography.”
prompt: What’s the cause?

options.A: The synthesis agent does not detect gaps
options.B: The coordinator decomposed the task too narrowly
options.C: The web search agent does not search thoroughly enough
options.D: The document analysis agent filters out non-visual sources

why: The logs show the coordinator decomposed “creative industries” only into visual subtopics, completely missing music, literature, and film. Subagents executed correctly—the issue is what they were assigned.

situation: The synthesis agent often needs to verify specific claims while merging results. Currently, when verification is needed, the synthesis agent hands control back to the coordinator, which calls the web-search agent and then re-runs synthesis with the new results. This adds 2–3 extra round trips per task and increases latency by 40%. Your assessment shows that 85% of these checks are simple fact checks (dates, names, statistics), while 15% require deeper investigation.
prompt: How do you reduce overhead while maintaining reliability?

options.A: Give the synthesis agent a limited `verify_fact` tool for simple checks, and continue routing complex verification through the coordinator
options.B: Accumulate all verification needs into a batch and return them to the coordinator at the end
options.C: Give the synthesis agent full access to all web-search tools
options.D: Proactively cache additional context around each source

why: This applies the principle of least privilege: the synthesis agent gets exactly what it needs for the 85% common case (simple fact checks) while preserving the coordinator-mediated path for complex investigations. B introduces blocking dependencies (later synthesis steps may depend on earlier verified facts). C breaks separation of responsibilities. D relies on speculative caching that cannot reliably predict needs.

situation: A pipeline runs `claude "Analyze this pull request for security issues"`, but hangs waiting for interactive input.
prompt: What is the correct approach?

options.A: Use the `-p` flag: `claude -p "Analyze this pull request for security issues"`
options.B: Set `CLAUDE_HEADLESS=true`
options.C: Redirect stdin from `/dev/null`
options.D: Use `--batch`

why: `-p` (or `--print`) is the documented way to run Claude Code in non-interactive mode. It processes the prompt, prints to stdout, and exits. The other options are either non-existent features or Unix workarounds.

situation: The team wants to reduce API cost for automated analysis. Claude currently serves two workflows in real time: (1) a blocking pre-merge check that must complete before developers can merge a PR, and (2) a tech-debt report generated overnight for morning review. A manager proposes moving both to the Message Batches API to save 50%.
prompt: How should you evaluate this proposal?

options.A: Use batch processing only for tech-debt reports; keep real-time calls for pre-merge checks
options.B: Move both workflows to batch processing and poll for completion
options.C: Keep real-time calls for both to avoid ordering issues in batch results
options.D: Move both to batch processing with a fallback to real time if a batch takes too long

why: The Message Batches API saves 50%, but processing time can be up to 24 hours with no guaranteed latency SLA. That makes it unsuitable for blocking pre-merge checks where developers are waiting, but ideal for overnight batch workloads like tech-debt reports.

situation: A pull request changes 14 files in an inventory tracking module. A single-pass review of all files produces inconsistent results: detailed comments for some files but superficial ones for others, missed obvious bugs, and contradictory feedback (a pattern is flagged as problematic in one file but approved in identical code in another file).
prompt: How should you restructure the review?

options.A: Split into focused passes: analyze each file individually for local issues, then run a separate integration pass for cross-file data flows
options.B: Require developers to split large PRs into submissions of 3–4 files
options.C: Switch to a higher-tier model with a larger context window to review all 14 files in one pass
options.D: Run three independent full-PR review passes and report only issues found in at least two runs

why: Focused passes directly address the root cause—attention dilution when processing many files at once. Per-file analysis ensures consistent depth, and a separate integration pass catches cross-file issues. B shifts burden to developers without improving the system. C is a misconception: larger context does not fix attention quality. D suppresses real bugs by requiring consensus across inconsistent detections.

situation: A document analysis agent discovers that two credible sources contain directly contradictory statistics for a key metric: a government report states 40% growth, while an industry analysis states 12%. Both sources look credible, and the discrepancy could materially affect the research conclusions. How should the document analysis agent handle this situation most effectively?
prompt: Which approach is most effective?

options.A: Apply credibility heuristics to pick the most likely correct number, finish analysis with that value, and add a footnote mentioning the discrepancy.
options.B: Include both numbers in the analysis output without marking them as conflicting, letting the synthesis agent decide which to use based on broader context.
options.C: Stop analysis and immediately escalate to the coordinator, asking it to decide which source is more authoritative before continuing.
options.D: Complete analysis with both numbers, explicitly annotate the conflict with source attribution, and let the coordinator decide how to reconcile the data before passing to synthesis.

why: This approach preserves separation of responsibilities: the analysis agent completes its core work without blocking, preserves both conflicting values with clear attribution, and correctly passes reconciliation to the coordinator, which has broader context.

situation: The web-search and document-analysis agents have completed their tasks and returned results to the coordinator. What is the next step for creating an integrated research report?
prompt: Which next step is most appropriate?

options.A: Each agent sends its results directly to the report-writing agent, bypassing the coordinator.
options.B: The document analysis agent requests web-search results and merges them internally.
options.C: The coordinator passes both sets of results to the synthesis agent for a unified integration.
options.D: The coordinator concatenates the raw outputs from both agents and returns them as the final result.

why: In a coordinator–subagent architecture, the coordinator forwards both result sets to the synthesis agent for centralized integration, preserving control and ensuring high-quality merging.

situation: A document analysis subagent frequently fails when processing PDF files: some have corrupted sections that trigger parsing exceptions, others are password-protected, and sometimes the parsing library hangs on large files. Currently, any exception immediately terminates the subagent and returns an error to the coordinator, which must decide whether to retry, skip, or fail the whole task. This causes excessive coordinator involvement in routine error handling. What architectural improvement is most effective?
prompt: Which improvement is most effective?

options.A: Create a dedicated error-handling agent that monitors all failures via a shared queue and decides recovery actions, sending restart commands directly to subagents.
options.B: Configure the subagent to always return partial results with a success status, embedding error details in metadata; the coordinator treats all responses as successful.
options.C: Make the coordinator validate all documents before sending them to the subagent, rejecting documents that might cause failures.
options.D: Implement local recovery in the subagent for transient failures and escalate to the coordinator only errors it cannot resolve, including attempted steps and partial results.

why: Handle errors at the lowest level capable of resolving them. Local recovery reduces coordinator workload while still escalating truly unrecoverable issues with full context and partial progress.

situation: After running the system on “AI impact on creative industries,” you observe that every subagent completes successfully: the web-search agent finds relevant articles, the document analysis agent summarizes them correctly, and the synthesis agent produces coherent text. However, final reports cover only visual art and completely miss music, literature, and film. In the coordinator logs, you see it decomposed the topic into three subtasks: “AI in digital art,” “AI in graphic design,” and “AI in photography.” What is the most likely root cause?
prompt: What is the most likely root cause?

options.A: The synthesis agent lacks instructions to detect coverage gaps.
options.B: The document analysis agent filters out non-visual sources due to overly strict relevance criteria.
options.C: The coordinator’s task decomposition is too narrow, assigning subagents work that does not cover all relevant areas.
options.D: The web-search agent’s queries are insufficient and should be broadened to cover more sectors.

why: The coordinator decomposed a broad topic only into visual-art subtasks, missing music, literature, and film entirely. Since subagents executed their assignments correctly, the narrow decomposition is the obvious root cause.

situation: The web-search subagent returns results for only 3 of 5 requested source categories (competitor sites and industry reports succeed, but news archives and social feeds time out). The document analysis subagent successfully processes all provided documents. The synthesis subagent must produce a summary from mixed-quality upstream inputs. Which error-propagation strategy is most effective?
prompt: Which error-propagation strategy is most effective?

options.A: Continue synthesis using only successful sources and produce an output without mentioning which data was unavailable.
options.B: The synthesis subagent returns an error to the coordinator, triggering a full retry or task failure due to incomplete data.
options.C: The synthesis subagent asks the coordinator to retry timed-out sources with a longer timeout before starting synthesis.
options.D: Structure the synthesis output with coverage annotations that indicate which conclusions are well-supported and where gaps exist due to unavailable sources.

why: Coverage annotations implement graceful degradation with transparency, preserving value from completed work while propagating uncertainty to enable informed decisions about confidence.

situation: The document analysis subagent encounters a corrupted PDF file that it cannot parse. When designing the system’s error handling, what is the most effective way to handle this failure?
prompt: Which approach is most effective?

options.A: Return an error with context to the coordinator agent, allowing it to decide how to proceed.
options.B: Silently skip the corrupted document and continue processing the remaining files to avoid interrupting the workflow.
options.C: Automatically retry parsing the document three times with exponential backoff before reporting a failure.
options.D: Throw an exception that terminates the entire research workflow.

why: Returning an error with context to the coordinator is the most effective approach because it lets the coordinator make an informed decision—skip the file, try an alternative parsing method, or notify the user—while maintaining visibility into the failure.

situation: Production logs show a persistent pattern: requests like “analyze the uploaded quarterly report” are routed to the web-search agent 45% of the time instead of the document analysis agent. Reviewing tool definitions, you find that the web-search agent has a tool `analyze_content` described as “analyzes content and extracts key information,” while the document analysis agent has a tool `analyze_document` described as “analyzes documents and extracts key information.” How should you fix the misrouting problem?
prompt: How should you fix the misrouting problem?

options.A: Add a pre-routing classifier that detects whether the user refers to uploaded files or web content before the coordinator decides on delegation.
options.B: Rename the web-search tool to `extract_web_results` and update its description to “processes and returns information retrieved from web search and URLs.”
options.C: Add few-shot examples to the coordinator prompt showing correct routing: “User uploads a quarterly report → document analysis agent” and “User asks about a web page → web-search agent.”
options.D: Expand the document analysis tool description with usage examples like “Use for uploaded PDFs, Word docs, and spreadsheets,” leaving the web-search tool unchanged.

why: Renaming the web-search tool to `extract_web_results` and updating its description to explicitly reference web search and URLs directly removes the root cause by eliminating semantic overlap between the two tool names and descriptions. This makes each tool’s purpose unambiguous, enabling the coordinator to reliably distinguish document analysis from web search.

situation: A colleague proposes that the document analysis agent should send its results directly to the synthesis agent, bypassing the coordinator. What is the main advantage of keeping the coordinator as the central hub for all communication between subagents?
prompt: What is the main advantage of keeping the coordinator as the central hub?

options.A: The coordinator can observe all interactions, handle errors uniformly, and decide what information each subagent should receive.
options.B: The coordinator batches multiple requests to subagents, reducing total API calls and overall latency.
options.C: Routing through the coordinator enables automatic retry logic that direct inter-agent calls cannot support.
options.D: Subagents use isolated memory, and direct communication would require complex serialization that only the coordinator can perform.

why: The coordinator pattern provides centralized visibility into all interactions, uniform error handling across the system, and fine-grained control over what information each subagent receives—these are the primary advantages of a star-shaped communication topology.

situation: In your system design, you gave the document analysis agent access to a general-purpose tool `fetch_url` so it could download documents by URL. Production logs show this agent now frequently downloads search engine results pages to perform ad hoc web search—behavior that should be routed through the web-search agent—causing inconsistent results. Which fix is most effective?
prompt: Which fix is most effective?

options.A: Replace `fetch_url` with a `load_document` tool that validates that URLs point to document formats.
options.B: Remove `fetch_url` from the document analysis agent and route all URL fetching through the coordinator to the web-search agent.
options.C: Implement filtering that blocks `fetch_url` calls to known search engine domains while allowing other URLs.
options.D: Add instructions to the document analysis agent prompt that `fetch_url` should only be used to download document URLs, not to search.

why: Replacing a general-purpose tool with a document-specific tool that validates URLs against document formats fixes the root cause by constraining capability at the interface level. This follows the principle of least privilege, making undesired search behavior impossible rather than merely discouraged.

situation: While researching a broad topic, you observe that the web-search agent and the document analysis agent investigate the same subtopics, leading to substantial duplication in their outputs. Token usage nearly doubles without a proportional increase in research breadth or depth. What is the most effective way to address this?
prompt: What is the most effective way to address this?

options.A: Allow both agents to finish in parallel, then have the coordinator deduplicate overlapping results before passing them to the synthesis agent.
options.B: The coordinator explicitly partitions the research space before delegating, assigning each agent distinct subtopics or source types.
options.C: Implement a shared-state mechanism where agents log their current focus area so other agents can dynamically avoid duplication during execution.
options.D: Switch to sequential execution where document analysis runs only after web search completes, using web-search results as context to avoid duplication.

why: Having the coordinator explicitly partition the research space before delegating is most effective because it addresses the root cause—unclear task boundaries—before any work begins. It preserves parallelism while preventing duplicated effort and wasted tokens.

situation: During research, the web-search subagent queries three source categories with different outcomes: academic databases return 15 relevant papers, industry reports return “0 results,” and patent databases return “Connection timeout.” When designing error propagation to the coordinator, which approach enables the best recovery decisions?
prompt: Which approach enables the best recovery decisions?

options.A: Aggregate the results into a single success-percentage metric (e.g., “67% source coverage”) with detailed logs available on demand.
options.B: Report both “timeout” and “0 results” as failures requiring coordinator intervention.
options.C: Retry transient failures internally and report only persistent errors.
options.D: Distinguish access failures (timeout) that require a retry decision from valid empty results (“0 results”) that represent successful queries.

why: A timeout (access failure) and “0 results” (valid empty result) are semantically different outcomes requiring different responses. Distinguishing them allows the coordinator to retry the patent database while accepting the industry reports “0 results” as a valid, informative finding.

situation: Production monitoring shows inconsistent synthesis quality. When aggregated results are ~75K tokens, the synthesis agent reliably cites information from the first 15K tokens (web-search headlines/snippets) and the last 10K tokens (document analysis conclusions), but often misses critical findings in the middle 50K tokens—even when they directly answer the research question. How should you restructure the aggregated input?
prompt: How should you restructure the aggregated input?

options.A: Summarize all subagent outputs to under 20K tokens before aggregation to keep content within the model’s reliable processing range.
options.B: Stream subagent results to the synthesis agent incrementally, processing web-search results first to completion, then adding document analysis results.
options.C: Place a key-findings summary at the start of the aggregated input and organize detailed results with explicit section headings for easier navigation.
options.D: Implement rotation that alternates which subagent’s results appear first across research tasks to ensure both sources get equal top positioning over time.

why: Putting a key-findings summary at the start leverages primacy effects so critical information sits in the most reliably processed position. Adding explicit section headings throughout helps the model navigate and attend to mid-input content, directly mitigating the “lost in the middle” phenomenon.

situation: In testing, the combined output of the web-search agent (85K tokens including page content) and the document analysis agent (70K tokens including chains of thought) totals 155K tokens, but the synthesis agent performs best with inputs under 50K tokens. Which solution is most effective?
prompt: Which solution is most effective?

options.A: Modify upstream agents to return structured data (key facts, quotes, relevance scores) instead of verbose content and reasoning.
options.B: Add an intermediate summarization agent that condenses findings before passing them to synthesis.
options.C: Have the synthesis agent process findings in sequential batches, maintaining state between calls.
options.D: Store findings in a vector database and give the synthesis agent search tools to query during its work.

why: Modifying upstream agents to return structured data fixes the root cause by reducing token volume at the source while preserving essential information. It avoids passing bulky page content and reasoning traces that inflate tokens without improving the synthesis step.

situation: In testing, you observe that the synthesis agent often needs to verify specific claims while merging results. Currently, when verification is needed, the synthesis agent returns control to the coordinator, which calls the web-search agent and then re-invokes synthesis with the results. This adds 2–3 extra loops per task and increases latency by 40%. Your assessment shows 85% of these verifications are simple fact checks (dates, names, stats) and 15% require deeper research. Which approach most effectively reduces overhead while preserving system reliability?
prompt: Which approach is most effective?

options.A: Give the synthesis agent access to all web-search tools so it can handle any verification need directly without coordinator loops.
options.B: Have the synthesis agent accumulate all verification needs and return them as a batch to the coordinator at the end, which then sends them all to the web-search agent at once.
options.C: Have the web-search agent proactively cache extra context around each source during initial research in anticipation of synthesis needing verification.
options.D: Give the synthesis agent a limited-scope `verify_fact` tool for simple checks, while routing complex verifications through the coordinator to the web-search agent.

why: A limited-scope fact-verification tool lets the synthesis agent handle 85% of simple checks directly, eliminating most loops, while preserving the coordinator delegation path for the 15% of complex verifications. This applies least privilege while significantly reducing latency.

situation: Your CI pipeline runs the Claude Code CLI (in `--print` mode) using CLAUDE.md to provide project context for code review, and developers generally find the reviews substantive. However, they report that integrating findings into the workflow is difficult—Claude outputs narrative paragraphs that must be manually copied into PR comments. The team wants to automatically post each finding as a separate inline PR comment at the relevant place in code, which requires structured data with file path, line number, severity level, and suggested fix. Which approach is most effective?
prompt: Which approach is most effective?

options.A: Add an “Output Format for Review” section to CLAUDE.md with examples of structured findings so Claude learns the expected format from project context.
options.B: Use the CLI flags `--output-format json` and `--json-schema` to enforce structured findings, then parse the output to post inline comments via the GitHub API.
options.C: Include explicit formatting instructions in the review prompt requiring each finding to follow a parseable template like `[FILE:path] [LINE:n] [SEVERITY:level] ...`.
options.D: Keep narrative review format but add a summarization step that uses Claude to generate a structured JSON summary of findings.

why: Using `--output-format json` with `--json-schema` enforces structured output at the CLI level, guaranteeing well-formed JSON with the required fields (file path, line number, severity, suggested fix) that can be reliably parsed and posted as inline PR comments via the GitHub API. It leverages built-in CLI capabilities designed specifically for structured output.

situation: Your team uses Claude Code for generating code suggestions, but you notice a pattern: non-obvious issues—performance optimizations that break edge cases, cleanups that unexpectedly change behavior—are only caught when another team member reviews the PR. Claude’s reasoning during generation shows it considered these cases but concluded its approach was correct. Which approach directly addresses the root cause of this self-check limitation?
prompt: Which approach directly addresses the root cause?

options.A: Run a second independent instance of Claude Code to review the changes without access to the generator’s reasoning.
options.B: Enable extended thinking mode for the generation stage to allow more thorough deliberation before producing suggestions.
options.C: Add explicit self-review instructions to the generation prompt asking Claude to critique its own suggestions before finalizing output.
options.D: Include full test files and documentation in prompt context so Claude better understands expected behavior during generation.

why: A second independent Claude Code instance without access to the generator’s reasoning directly addresses the root cause by avoiding confirmation bias. This “fresh eyes” perspective mirrors human peer review, where another reviewer catches issues the author rationalized.

situation: Your code review component is iterative: Claude analyzes the changed file, then may request related files (imports, base classes, tests) via tool calls to understand context before providing final feedback. Your application defines a tool that lets Claude request file contents; Claude calls the tool, gets results, and continues analysis. You’re evaluating batch processing to reduce API cost. What is the primary technical limitation when considering batch processing for this workflow?
prompt: What is the primary technical limitation?

options.A: Batch processing does not include correlation IDs to map outputs back to input requests.
options.B: The asynchronous model cannot execute tools mid-request and return results for Claude to continue analysis.
options.C: The Batch API does not support tool definitions in request parameters.
options.D: The batch processing latency of up to 24 hours is too slow for pull request feedback, although the workflow would otherwise function.

why: A “fire-and-forget” asynchronous Batch API model has no mechanism to intercept a tool call during a request, execute the tool, and return results for Claude to continue analysis. This is fundamentally incompatible with iterative tool-calling workflows that require multiple tool request/response rounds within a single logical interaction.

situation: Your CI/CD system runs three Claude-based analyses: (1) fast style checks on every PR that block merging until completion, (2) comprehensive weekly security audits of the entire codebase, and (3) nightly test-case generation for recently changed modules. The Message Batches API offers 50% savings but processing can take up to 24 hours. You want to optimize API cost while maintaining an acceptable developer experience. Which combination correctly matches each task to an API approach?
prompt: Which combination is correct?

options.A: Use the Message Batches API for all three tasks to maximize 50% savings, configuring the pipeline to poll for batch completion.
options.B: Use synchronous calls for PR style checks; use the Message Batches API for weekly security audits and nightly test generation.
options.C: Use synchronous calls for all three tasks for consistent response times, relying on prompt caching to reduce costs across workloads.
options.D: Use synchronous calls for PR style checks and nightly test generation; use the Message Batches API only for weekly security audits.

why: PR style checks block developers and require immediate responses via synchronous calls, while weekly security audits and nightly test generation are scheduled tasks with flexible deadlines that can tolerate up to a 24-hour batch window—capturing 50% savings for both.

situation: Your automated reviews find real issues, but developers report the feedback is not actionable. Findings include phrases like “complex ticket routing logic” or “potential null pointer” without specifying what exactly to change. When you add detailed instructions like “always include concrete fix suggestions,” the model still produces inconsistent output—sometimes detailed, sometimes vague. Which prompting technique most reliably produces consistently actionable feedback?
prompt: Which prompting technique is most reliable?

options.A: Further refine instructions with more explicit requirements for each part of the feedback format (location, issue, severity, proposed fix).
options.B: Expand the context window to include more surrounding codebase so the model has enough information to propose concrete fixes.
options.C: Implement a two-pass approach where one prompt identifies issues and a second generates fixes, allowing specialization.
options.D: Add 3–4 few-shot examples showing the exact required format: identified issue, location in code, concrete fix suggestion.

why: Few-shot examples are the most effective technique for achieving consistent output format when instructions alone produce variable results. Providing 3–4 examples that show the exact desired structure (issue, location, concrete fix) gives the model a concrete pattern to follow, which is more reliable than abstract instructions.

situation: Your CI pipeline includes two Claude-based code review modes: a pre-merge-commit hook that blocks PR merge until completion, and a “deep analysis” that runs overnight, polls for batch completion, and posts detailed suggestions to the PR. You want to reduce API cost using the Message Batches API, which offers 50% savings but requires polling and can take up to 24 hours. Which mode should use batch processing?
prompt: Which mode should use batch processing?

options.A: Only the pre-merge-commit hook.
options.B: Only the deep analysis.
options.C: Both modes.
options.D: Neither mode.

why: Deep analysis is an ideal candidate for batch processing because it already runs overnight, tolerates delay, and uses a polling model before publishing results—matching the asynchronous, polling-based architecture of the Message Batches API while capturing 50% savings.

situation: Your automated review analyzes comments and docstrings. The current prompt instructs Claude to “check that comments are accurate and up to date.” Findings often flag acceptable patterns (TODO markers, simple descriptions) while missing comments describing behavior the code no longer implements. What change addresses the root cause of this inconsistent analysis?
prompt: What change addresses the root cause?

options.A: Include `git blame` data so Claude can identify comments that predate recent code changes.
options.B: Add few-shot examples of misleading comments to help the model recognize similar patterns in the codebase.
options.C: Filter TODO, FIXME, and descriptive comment patterns before analysis to reduce noise.
options.D: Specify explicit criteria: flag comments only when the behavior they claim contradicts the code’s actual behavior.

why: Explicit criteria—flagging comments only when claimed behavior contradicts actual code behavior—directly addresses the root cause by replacing a vague instruction with a precise definition of what constitutes a problem. This reduces false positives on acceptable patterns and misses of truly misleading comments.

situation: Your automated code review system shows inconsistent severity ratings—similar issues like null pointer risks are rated “critical” in some PRs but only “medium” in others. Developer surveys show growing distrust—many start dismissing findings without reading because “half are wrong.” High-false-positive categories erode trust in accurate categories. Which approach best restores developer trust while improving the system?
prompt: Which approach best restores developer trust?

options.A: Temporarily disable high-false-positive categories (style, naming, documentation) and keep only high-precision categories while improving prompts.
options.B: Keep all categories enabled but display confidence scores with each finding so developers can decide what to investigate.
options.C: Keep all categories enabled and add few-shot examples to improve accuracy for each category over the next few weeks.
options.D: Apply a uniform strictness reduction across all categories to bring the overall false-positive rate down.

why: Temporarily disabling high-false-positive categories immediately stops trust erosion by removing noisy findings that cause developers to dismiss everything, while preserving value from high-precision categories like security and correctness. It also creates space to improve prompts for problematic categories before re-enabling them.

situation: Your automated review generates test-case suggestions for each PR. Reviewing a PR that adds course completion tracking, Claude suggests 10 test cases, but developer feedback shows that 6 duplicate scenarios already covered by the existing test suite. What change most effectively reduces duplicate suggestions?
prompt: What change is most effective?

options.A: Include the existing test file in context so Claude can determine what scenarios are already covered.
options.B: Reduce the requested number of suggestions from 10 to 5, assuming Claude prioritizes the most valuable cases first.
options.C: Add instructions directing Claude to focus exclusively on edge cases and error conditions rather than success paths.
options.D: Implement post-processing that filters suggestions whose descriptions match existing test names via keyword overlap.

why: Including the existing test file fixes the root cause of duplication: Claude can only avoid suggesting already-covered scenarios if it knows what tests already exist. This gives Claude the information needed to propose genuinely new, valuable tests.

situation: After an initial automated review identifies 12 findings, a developer pushes new commits to address issues. Re-running review produces 8 findings, but developers report that 5 duplicate previous comments on code that was already fixed in the new commits. What is the most effective way to eliminate this redundant feedback while maintaining thoroughness?
prompt: What is the most effective way to eliminate redundant feedback?

options.A: Run review only when the PR is created and in the final pre-merge state, skipping intermediate commits.
options.B: Add a post-processing filter that removes findings that match previous ones by file paths and issue descriptions before posting comments.
options.C: Restrict review scope to files changed in the most recent push, excluding files from earlier commits.
options.D: Include previous review findings in context and instruct Claude to report only new or still-unresolved issues.

why: Including prior review findings in context lets Claude distinguish new problems from those already addressed in recent commits. This preserves review thoroughness while using Claude’s reasoning to avoid redundant feedback on fixed code.

situation: A pull request changes 14 files in an inventory tracking module. A single-pass review that analyzes all files together produces inconsistent results: detailed feedback on some files but shallow comments on others, missed obvious bugs, and contradictory feedback (a pattern is flagged in one file but identical code is approved in another file in the same PR). How should you restructure the review?
prompt: How should you restructure the review?
